snappy: Approach to advanced packaging where multiple dies sit side-by-side on an interposer — between traditional flat packaging and full 3D stacking.
detail: Most current AI accelerators use 2.5D: GPU compute die + HBM stacks on a common interposer. The interposer carries fine-pitch wires between the dies for very high inter-die bandwidth. Distinct from 3D, where dies are stacked vertically. CoWoS is TSMC's brand of 2.5D.

snappy: Stacking dies vertically — connecting them with through-silicon vias for very dense, short connections.
detail: Used in HBM (DRAM dies stacked 8-12 high) and increasingly for logic. TSMC's SoIC, Intel's Foveros Direct, Samsung's X-Cube are 3D options. The most aggressive form is hybrid bonding, which connects dies copper-to-copper at micron pitch. Promises huge bandwidth at low power; thermal management is the central challenge.

snappy: NVIDIA's previous-generation training GPU — the workhorse of 2020-2022 LLM development.
detail: Launched 2020. 40GB or 80GB of HBM2e, ~300 TFLOPS BF16. GPT-3, Stable Diffusion, original Llama were all trained on A100s. Now superseded by H100 and Blackwell but still common in production inference because supply is abundant and pricing is reasonable. Still widely available on cloud providers.

snappy: DeepMind's 2016 algorithm — runs many actor-learner pairs in parallel to stabilise training.
detail: Important because it showed deep RL could be trained quickly on CPUs (no GPUs needed) by parallelising across many simulations. Variants A2C (synchronous) and IMPALA (more efficient asynchronous design) followed. Largely superseded by PPO for general use but historically influential.

snappy: Hugging Face's wrapper around PyTorch that handles the boring parts of multi-GPU/multi-node training.
detail: You write single-device PyTorch code; Accelerate handles the distributed setup, gradient synchronisation, and mixed precision. Especially useful for researchers who want to try out training scripts on different scales without rewriting them. Often paired with DeepSpeed or FSDP under the hood.

snappy: Umbrella term for any chip designed to speed up a specific workload — GPUs, TPUs, NPUs, ASICs all qualify.
detail: In AI context, "accelerator" usually means "the chip that does the matrix multiplies". The accelerator ecosystem is increasingly diverse: NVIDIA GPUs dominate training; AMD MI300 series is the credible alternative; Google TPU runs Gemini; Amazon Trainium/Inferentia targets AWS workloads; startups (Groq, Cerebras, Sambanova) chase specific bottlenecks like inference latency.

snappy: The non-linear element of a neural network layer — what makes neural networks more than just matrix multiplication.
detail: Without activation functions, stacking layers would just give you another linear function. ReLU, GELU, SwiGLU, Sigmoid, Tanh, and others provide the non-linearity that lets deep networks express complex functions. Modern Transformer feed-forward layers typically use SwiGLU (Llama, Mistral) or GeGLU.

snappy: Training scheme that iteratively asks humans to label the data points the model is most uncertain about.
detail: Useful when labels are expensive and you have a budget. Modern variant: use a strong model (Claude, GPT-4) as a labeler instead of humans, with active-learning techniques choosing which inputs deserve the expensive model's attention. Common in production data-labeling pipelines.

snappy: RL architecture combining two networks: an actor that picks actions, a critic that estimates how good those actions are.
detail: The actor is the policy; the critic is the value function. They train together: critic provides a learning signal to the actor, actor's exploration generates data for the critic. Most modern RL algorithms (PPO, A3C, SAC, DDPG) are variants of actor-critic. The architectural insight that made deep RL stable enough to train.

snappy: The standard optimizer used to train almost every modern neural network.
detail: Adam tracks both the running average of gradients and their variance, then uses both to adapt the step size per parameter. AdamW, a small variation that handles regularisation cleanly, is the current default for LLM training. Introduced in 2014 and never seriously displaced — competitors like Adafactor and Lion exist but Adam/AdamW remains the safe choice.

snappy: Catch-all term for chip packaging beyond the basic single-die-in-plastic — interposers, 3D stacking, hybrid bonding, chiplets.
detail: Has become as important as transistor scaling. As Moore's law slows, advanced packaging is where the AI hardware industry finds gains. TSMC's CoWoS, Intel's Foveros, Samsung's X-Cube are the major options. Capacity is constrained — CoWoS in particular has been a bottleneck for NVIDIA's GPU production through 2024-2025.

snappy: An AI system that can take actions in the world over multiple steps, not just respond to a single prompt.
detail: Agents combine an LLM with tools (web search, code execution, file access, APIs), memory, and a loop that lets them plan, act, observe the result, and try again. The bar shifted in 2024 from "answer my question" to "do this task end-to-end". Coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Devin) and computer-use agents (Anthropic, OpenAI Operator) are the most visible examples.

snappy: Buzzword for the next phase of AI — systems that take multi-step actions rather than producing single outputs.
detail: Used heavily in 2024-2025 industry conversations. Agentic AI = chatbots that drive software, automate workflows, complete long tasks. Builds on tool use, function calling, computer use, and chained LLM calls. The point of departure: outputs no longer need user review in the middle of a task.

snappy: Hypothetical AI system that can match or exceed humans on essentially any cognitive task.
detail: The original goal of much AI research. Definitions vary — OpenAI's charter, Anthropic's responsible scaling policy, and academic papers all draw the line slightly differently. Frontier labs disagree on whether current LLMs are early AGI, on a path to AGI, or fundamentally limited. The term is contested but central to debate about AI strategy and risk.

snappy: Emerging product category — autonomous coding agents that can execute multi-hour tasks.
detail: Devin, Replit Agents, Cognition, OpenAI's Codex agent, GitHub Copilot Workspace. The frame is not "AI helps a developer" but "AI is a developer for some scoped task". Maturity varies widely; performance on SWE-Bench measures progress. Major investment area for AI labs and startups.

snappy: General consumer-facing AI product — chat with a model that helps with tasks, scheduling, search, drafting.
detail: ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Meta AI, Perplexity. Successor category to the older voice assistants (Siri, Alexa). The shift in interaction modality — typed chat with a frontier LLM rather than voice commands to a rules-based system — fundamentally changed consumer expectations of AI.

snappy: White House blueprint (2022) listing five principles for protecting Americans from AI harms.
detail: Principles: safe and effective systems; algorithmic discrimination protections; data privacy; notice and explanation; human alternatives. Non-binding. Some provisions echoed in the 2023 executive order. A reference point for civil-society advocacy and a benchmark proposals are evaluated against.

snappy: Concern that current AI infrastructure investment exceeds what end-user demand can justify — that the market is overheated.
detail: Cited reasons for concern: capex outpacing AI revenue, circular financing between AI companies, comparison to dot-com era. Cited reasons against: continued user growth, demonstrable capability gains, productivity research showing measurable economic impact. Settling the question will likely take years.

snappy: Hyperscalers' capital expenditures on AI infrastructure — data centers, GPUs, networking.
detail: Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta combined are spending $300-400B+ per year on AI capex by 2025. Investors increasingly ask whether the spending will produce sufficient returns to justify the scale. Capex announcements move stock prices in both directions: investors want investment, but they also want returns.

snappy: Use of AI in customer support — chatbots, automated triage, agent assist.
detail: One of the most-deployed AI applications. Vendors include Salesforce Agentforce, ServiceNow, Intercom Fin, Decagon. Quality has shifted dramatically since pre-LLM chatbots — modern AI customer service can resolve a meaningful fraction of tickets without escalation, freeing humans for complex cases.

snappy: President Biden's October 2023 executive order on safe, secure, and trustworthy AI.
detail: Set reporting requirements for the largest training runs (10²⁶ FLOPs threshold), required AI companies to share safety test results with the federal government, and directed many agencies to develop AI guidance for their domains. Rescinded by the Trump administration in 2025. Many of its operational programs (NIST guidelines, AISI work) persist under different authorities.

snappy: NVIDIA's term for data centers built specifically as AI inference and training facilities — coined by Jensen Huang.
detail: Marketing language but also conceptual: a data centre built around accelerators rather than general-purpose servers, designed to produce "AI tokens" as its primary output. NVIDIA pushes the framing to position GPU build-outs as productive industrial capacity rather than IT spending.

snappy: Using AI for reaction prediction, retrosynthesis, molecular property prediction.
detail: ChemAI tools like IBM's RXN, Iktos, and academic systems can suggest synthesis routes for novel molecules and predict reaction outcomes. Modern foundation models (Galactica, AI4Science) generalise across chemistry tasks. Important supporting infrastructure for both pharmaceutical and materials research.

snappy: Using AI to control plasma in tokamak fusion reactors — keep the plasma stable enough to sustain reactions.
detail: DeepMind worked with EPFL on plasma control by reinforcement learning (2022, Nature paper). Many fusion startups (Commonwealth Fusion Systems, TAE, Helion, Tokamak Energy) deploy AI for plasma control, materials science, and reactor optimisation. AI is unlikely to be the sole enabler of fusion but is meaningfully accelerating progress.

snappy: Using AI to discover new alloys, catalysts, batteries, semiconductors, and other materials.
detail: GNoME demonstrated discovery at scale. Industry adopters include battery developers (QuantumScape, Form Energy), catalyst designers, and semiconductor materials companies. The gap between simulation and lab synthesis remains the binding constraint — AI suggestions need experimental validation. Active area of frontier research.

snappy: Broad umbrella covering AI applications across scientific disciplines.
detail: Term used by Microsoft Research, DeepMind, EU AI strategy, NSF programs. The pitch: AI as a force multiplier for scientific discovery across biology, chemistry, materials, physics, climate, astronomy. Demis Hassabis articulates an explicit goal of "using AI to solve fundamental questions in science".

snappy: Middleware that brokers LLM API calls — provides routing, observability, caching, rate limiting, fallback.
detail: OpenRouter, Portkey, Vercel AI SDK, Cloudflare AI Gateway. Lets app developers swap between OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and dozens of model providers without rewriting code. Important enterprise infrastructure for multi-model deployments. Becoming a standard architecture pattern for any serious LLM-powered product.

snappy: AI features inside image-editing tools — generative fill, smart selection, prompt-based edits.
detail: Adobe Photoshop Generative Fill (powered by Adobe Firefly) is the most-used in production by professionals. Apple's Image Playground, Google Photos Magic Editor, Samsung Galaxy AI offer similar features at consumer level. Has changed standard photo-editing workflows for casual and professional users alike.

snappy: Use of AI for climate modelling, carbon accounting, energy forecasting, and adaptation planning.
detail: Includes GraphCast-style weather/climate models, satellite-imagery analysis for deforestation and emissions tracking (Climate TRACE), and grid optimisation (Tapestry, Climate AI). Both an emissions-reduction enabler and an emissions source (data-centre power) — the net climate impact of AI is one of the major sustainability debates.

snappy: Use of AI in financial services — trading, fraud detection, customer service, document processing.
detail: Investment banks (JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, Goldman) deploy LLMs for research and customer service. Hedge funds (RenTech, Two Sigma, Citadel) use ML for trading. Fintechs (Stripe, Brex) deploy AI for fraud detection. The combination of structured data and high willingness to pay makes finance one of AI's biggest sectors.

snappy: Use of AI for legal work — drafting, research, contract review, discovery.
detail: Harvey AI (focused on law firms, valued $5B+) is the highest-profile player; competitors include Cocounsel (from Thomson Reuters), Spellbook, LegalMation. Plus the general-purpose LLMs that lawyers use daily. The legal profession's combination of high billing rates and structured documents makes it a natural early-AI vertical.

snappy: Use of AI in healthcare — clinical notes, radiology, drug discovery, triage.
detail: Spans many subfields. Ambient clinical-notes generation (Abridge, Nuance DAX) is one of the most-deployed AI applications by revenue. Image-based diagnostics (radiology, dermatology) has FDA-cleared products. Hippocratic AI's clinician-facing assistants and many drug-discovery tools complete the picture. Regulatory oversight is ongoing.

snappy: Legal question: when an AI system causes harm, who is responsible — the model developer, the deployer, the user?
detail: Active area of policy debate. Some proposals impose strict liability on model developers (SB 1047 attempted this); others place responsibility primarily on deployers. The EU AI Act takes a layered approach. Resolution will materially affect the economics of AI deployment.

snappy: Use of AI to do general office and knowledge work faster — spreadsheets, emails, meeting notes, documents.
detail: Most-used consumer-AI category by hours. Microsoft 365 Copilot, Google Workspace AI, Notion AI, ChatGPT, Claude. Empirical research (BCG/Anthropic, Microsoft) shows meaningful productivity gains on tasks well-suited to AI assistance — 20-50% time savings on writing-heavy tasks, smaller on coding and analysis.

snappy: Field of research focused on ensuring that powerful AI systems are reliable, predictable, and aligned with human intentions.
detail: Spans technical work (alignment, interpretability, evaluations) and governance work (policy, regulation, international coordination). Took off around 2014 (Nick Bostrom's Superintelligence) and grew rapidly during the 2020s as model capabilities improved. Major institutions: Anthropic, MIRI, ARC, UK AI Safety Institute, US AI Safety Institute.

snappy: Government bodies (UK, US, others) that evaluate frontier AI models for dangerous capabilities before public release.
detail: UK AISI established 2023; US AISI 2024. They run pre-deployment red-team evaluations on models from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, and others under voluntary agreements. Findings sometimes lead to model release delays or modifications. Centralised model for government oversight of frontier AI.

snappy: Use of AI for sales prospecting, outreach, and customer development.
detail: Startups: Clay, 11x.ai, Artisan, Regie.ai. The pattern: AI agents that research prospects, draft personalised outreach, follow up, and qualify leads. Controversial because of email-spam concerns; effective because of the volume and personalisation AI can deliver. Active product category in 2024-2025 enterprise SaaS.

snappy: Search experience where AI generates a direct answer instead of (or alongside) a list of links.
detail: Google AI Overviews, Perplexity, You.com, Phind, Bing Copilot. Tension with traditional web publishers: AI answers often summarise their content without sending traffic. Major regulatory and business-model debate through 2024-2025. The shift from links to answers is one of the most consequential in modern AI deployment.

snappy: Goal of nations developing AI capability domestically — own models, own infrastructure, own data.
detail: France pushes Mistral; the UAE has Falcon and partnerships with G42; Japan is investing in domestic LLM development; India is pursuing Indic LLMs; the Middle East and Singapore are building sovereign cloud-AI capacity. The push is partly about economic capture, partly about not depending on US or Chinese AI vendors for critical functions.

snappy: AI system designed to teach — adapts explanations to a learner, generates practice questions, gives feedback.
detail: Khan Academy's Khanmigo (powered by GPT-4) is the highest-profile example. Cited by Bill Gates as one of the first proven AI use cases. Personalised tutoring has long been an aspirational application; modern LLMs can begin to deliver. Many startups (Synthesis, Magic School AI, Quizlet AI) target the space.

snappy: AI-powered video editing — automatic cuts, scene generation, dubbing, captioning, b-roll synthesis.
detail: Adobe Premiere AI, Runway, Descript, Captions, Hour One. Especially powerful in marketing video production where AI can generate or modify short-form video at scale. Synthetic avatars (HeyGen, Synthesia) overlap with this category for corporate video.

snappy: Slightly dismissive term for products built mostly on top of a third-party LLM API.
detail: Used pejoratively to imply lack of defensibility. Defenders argue that customer success comes from product, distribution, and integration — the model is the easy part. Hot debate during the 2023-2025 AI-startup investment boom: "thin wrappers" vs. "product moats". Resolution depends on the vertical.

snappy: AI-assisted text composition — drafting, editing, style transfer, translation.
detail: Spans general LLMs (used as drafting tools), purpose-built editors (Notion AI, Lex.page, Sudowrite), and writing aides (Grammarly Go, Wordtune). Major impact on email, marketing copy, reports, and academic writing. The point at which AI is "writing" versus "editing" is increasingly fuzzy.

snappy: Hard high-school maths competition repurposed as an AI benchmark.
detail: Each year AIME has 15 problems with integer answers from 0-999, requiring competition-grade insight. Used heavily for evaluating reasoning models. OpenAI's o1 family was first to make headlines for surpassing typical human contestant scores. Modern frontier reasoning models routinely score 70-90% on recent AIME papers.

snappy: Running AI on infrastructure completely disconnected from the public internet — used in defence, finance, and high-security contexts.
detail: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Microsoft offer government and defence variants of their LLM services that run in classified environments. Inference is sometimes the only practical option (training requires cloud-scale infrastructure typically). Air-gapped Llama and Mistral deployments are common in regulated industries.

snappy: AI systems that produce different (worse) outcomes for protected groups — by race, gender, age, etc.
detail: Documented in many domains: hiring tools, credit scoring, healthcare resource allocation, predictive policing. Often traces back to biased training data or biased problem formulations. Most existing AI regulation focuses heavily on this issue because of its concrete civil-rights implications.

snappy: Chinese tech giant whose cloud arm trains the open-weight Qwen model family.
detail: Significant Chinese AI player alongside DeepSeek, Moonshot, and Baidu. Qwen models cover text, multimodal, and reasoning, often released open-weight. Alibaba Cloud is the leading Chinese cloud provider and a major investor in other Chinese AI startups (Moonshot, MiniMax, Zhipu).

snappy: Field of ensuring an AI system actually does what its designers and users want.
detail: Broader than just "safety". Alignment researchers try to make sure that a model's goals, behaviour, and values reflect the intentions of its principals — not just at training time, but as the system is deployed and as it becomes more capable. Major techniques include RLHF, Constitutional AI, interpretability, and red-teaming.

snappy: 52,000 instruction-following examples generated by GPT-3.5 in 2023 — used to fine-tune Llama into the original Alpaca model.
detail: Released by Stanford CRFM. Demonstrated that LLM-generated training data could be cheap and effective for instruction tuning. Catalysed the open-source fine-tune ecosystem. Has obvious quality limitations (mistakes from the source model) and OpenAI-terms-of-service questions.

snappy: DeepMind's 2023 system that discovered faster low-level sorting algorithms — now in the C++ standard library.
detail: Found small-array sorting routines that beat hand-optimised library code by a few percent on common workloads. The discovered algorithms were merged into the LLVM libcxx, meaning AlphaDev's output ships in every C++ program built with that toolchain. Concrete demonstration of AI producing usable systems software.

snappy: DeepMind's protein-structure prediction model — solved one of biology's longest-standing problems.
detail: AlphaFold 2 (2020) predicted protein 3D structures from amino-acid sequences at near-experimental accuracy, ending decades of struggle on the protein-folding problem. Demis Hassabis and John Jumper won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. AlphaFold 3 (2024) extends to protein-DNA, protein-ligand, and protein-RNA complexes — directly useful for drug discovery.

snappy: Successor to AlphaFold 2 that handles complexes — proteins binding DNA, RNA, small molecules, and other proteins.
detail: Released May 2024 with the AlphaFold Server (free for academics) and Isomorphic Labs (commercial). Removes the protein-only limitation that constrained AlphaFold 2's drug-discovery utility. A foundational tool for modern biological research; cited in thousands of papers within months.

snappy: DeepMind's 2025 model for predicting the effects of genetic variants on gene expression and disease risk.
detail: Takes DNA sequences as input and predicts how variants affect gene activity and downstream phenotypes. Builds on the same protein-prediction lineage as AlphaFold but operates on a different biological substrate. Early-stage but positioned as the next major "AlphaSomething" tool.

snappy: DeepMind's geometry-theorem-prover — solved Olympiad-level problems in 2024.
detail: Combines a language model that proposes geometric constructions with a symbolic engine that verifies them. The combination is what makes the system work — neither pure neural nor pure symbolic methods get there alone. Sister to AlphaProof in the AI-for-mathematics push.

snappy: DeepMind's 2016 system that defeated world champion Lee Sedol at Go — a watershed AI moment.
detail: Combined policy and value networks (deep CNNs trained on professional games) with Monte Carlo Tree Search. Beat Sedol 4-1 in March 2016. Successor AlphaGo Zero learned from self-play alone, no human data, and beat all earlier versions decisively. Sparked massive public attention to the capabilities of modern AI.

snappy: DeepMind's AI mathematician — proves theorems by combining language models with formal-proof systems.
detail: In 2024, AlphaProof+AlphaGeometry combined to solve four of six International Mathematical Olympiad problems — silver-medal performance. Major advance in symbolic reasoning. Built on top of Lean (formal proof assistant) so all generated proofs are machine-verifiable.

snappy: DeepMind's 2022 system that discovered new, faster matrix-multiplication algorithms via reinforcement learning.
detail: Treated finding a matrix multiplication algorithm as a game played by an RL agent. Discovered the first improvement on Strassen's 1969 algorithm for some matrix shapes. Concrete demonstration that AI can do novel algorithmic research, not just optimisation within known frameworks.

snappy: Generalisation of AlphaGo Zero to chess, shogi, and Go — same algorithm, no domain knowledge.
detail: Released 2017. Took ~4 hours of self-play to surpass the strongest chess engines (Stockfish). Demonstrated that the AlphaGo Zero approach (deep network + MCTS + self-play) was a general technique for board games with full information. Foundational reference point in modern RL.

snappy: AWS dominates cloud market share, and Amazon is Anthropic's primary cloud + strategic investor.
detail: AWS operates the largest cloud business and is investing heavily in AI capacity. Custom Trainium/Inferentia silicon competes with NVIDIA for AWS-internal AI workloads. Anthropic relationship gives Amazon a frontier-model partner; AWS Bedrock hosts Claude, Llama, Mistral, and others under one API.

snappy: NVIDIA's main GPU rival and Intel's main CPU rival — relevant in AI via the MI300/MI350/MI400 GPU line.
detail: Acquired Xilinx (FPGAs) in 2022 to expand its accelerator portfolio. CEO Lisa Su. The credible NVIDIA alternative at the high end, particularly for inference workloads. Software (ROCm) lags CUDA in maturity but is closing.

snappy: American AI lab focused on AI safety — creator of the Claude model family.
detail: Founded 2021 by former OpenAI staff including Dario and Daniela Amodei. Major investors include Google ($3-4B) and Amazon ($4-8B). Known for Constitutional AI, Responsible Scaling Policy, the Model Context Protocol, and a strong reputation for coding ability and careful refusal behaviour. Headquartered in San Francisco.

snappy: Anthropic's released human-preference dataset — pairs of model responses ranked by humans for helpfulness and harmlessness.
detail: Released 2022. Foundational for open research on RLHF reward modelling. The dataset shows the dual axes that RLHF tries to balance: be helpful but don't cross lines (illegal advice, manipulation, etc.). Often cited as one of the few large public preference datasets.

snappy: Designer of the M-series chips and Apple Intelligence on-device AI — vertically integrated.
detail: Apple Silicon (M and A series) is fabricated by TSMC. Apple's investment in on-device AI (the Neural Engine, Apple Intelligence) is distinct from the cloud-AI race — focus on privacy and tight hardware-software integration. Uses both its own models and partner models (OpenAI for ChatGPT integration in Siri).

snappy: Apple's family of CPUs/SoCs powering modern Macs (and iPad Pro) — with the Neural Engine doing the on-device AI.
detail: M1 (2020) through M4 (2024) and beyond. Unified memory architecture means the GPU and CPU share the same pool — important for large local LLM inference. Apple Intelligence's on-device 3B model runs natively. The M-series Max and Ultra variants are competitive with discrete GPUs for inference of mid-sized models.

snappy: Apple's on-device NPU, embedded in every iPhone, iPad, and Mac since 2017.
detail: Optimised for inference of small-to-medium models at very low power. The ANE in A18 Pro and M4 chips delivers 35-38 TFLOPS. Apple Intelligence (the on-device part) runs on the ANE. Developers access it indirectly through Core ML, the Apple framework for on-device machine learning.

snappy: Multiple-choice science questions written for grade-school students — tests commonsense + reasoning.
detail: Released 2018 by Allen Institute for AI. The "challenge" set contains questions that retrieval-based methods get wrong, so the model has to actually reason. Largely saturated by 2023.

snappy: Visual puzzle benchmark designed by François Chollet to test fluid reasoning — long resistant to scaling.
detail: Each puzzle is a few input-output grids; the model has to figure out the transformation rule and apply it to a new input. Designed as a test of abstraction that's easy for humans but historically hard for AI. OpenAI's o3 was first to break through 80% in late 2024; ARC-AGI-2 was released to provide harder puzzles.

snappy: Open-access pre-print server for physics, math, computer science, statistics — and the de facto distribution channel for AI papers.
detail: Hosted at Cornell. Every major AI lab posts there before peer review (which sometimes never comes). The training-data corpus version of arXiv (1.5M+ papers, mostly LaTeX source) is a standard ingredient in scientific-leaning LLM training. Search the cs.LG and cs.CL categories for the AI/ML literature.

snappy: Hypothetical AI system that exceeds the best human experts at every task by a large margin.
detail: The level beyond AGI. Whether AGI naturally accelerates toward ASI is a major open question ("intelligence explosion", "takeoff"). Anthropic, OpenAI, and DeepMind all have explicit positions on the responsibilities of labs developing systems that could plausibly reach ASI. Term sometimes used loosely; sometimes specifically.

snappy: A chip designed to do exactly one job extremely well — versus a CPU or GPU which is built to do anything.
detail: AI ASICs like Google TPUs, Amazon Trainium/Inferentia, and Groq's LPU sacrifice generality for efficiency on the specific patterns of neural network compute. They're typically 5-10× faster on their target workload than a general GPU at the same power. The downside: long design cycles (2-3 years) and inflexibility if the AI architecture shifts.

snappy: Dutch company that makes the EUV lithography machines required to manufacture cutting-edge chips.
detail: Sole supplier of extreme-ultraviolet lithography systems — the multimillion-dollar machines that print the smallest transistor features. Without ASML, the modern AI compute supply chain doesn't exist. Each machine costs $200-400M; high-NA EUV is even more. Subject to export controls preventing sales to China.

snappy: Turning spoken audio into text — the inverse of TTS.
detail: Modern ASR is dominated by Transformer encoders trained on huge multilingual audio corpora. Whisper, Google USM, Meta SeamlessM4T, Deepgram, AssemblyAI. Word error rates have dropped to single digits for major languages and clean audio. Real-time low-latency ASR is the frontier — used inside voice assistants and meeting transcription.

snappy: Use of AI in astronomy — classifying galaxies, detecting exoplanets, simulating cosmology, processing telescope data.
detail: Modern telescopes (Rubin Observatory, JWST, ALMA) produce data volumes too large for humans to inspect. ML pipelines now do most of the initial classification. Notable: AI-detected exoplanets (Kepler, TESS data), AI-classified galaxy morphologies, neural network surrogates for expensive N-body simulations.

snappy: How a model decides which parts of the input matter most for what it's about to produce.
detail: Originally introduced in 2014 to fix a bottleneck in translation models, attention lets a network decide on the fly which parts of the input to look at, rather than processing them in a fixed order. The 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" showed that this trick alone — without the older sequential machinery — could match or beat the state of the art, kicking off the Transformer era that powers ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini.

snappy: Compression model that turns raw audio into a small set of discrete tokens — used as the basis for audio LLMs.
detail: EnCodec (Meta), SoundStream (Google), DAC (Descript). They compress 44kHz stereo audio to a few hundred tokens per second of audio while preserving quality. Once audio is a token stream, you can run an LLM-style Transformer on it. Foundation of modern music and speech generation models.

snappy: Open-weight audio generation tools — MusicGen, AudioGen from Meta; Stable Audio from Stability AI.
detail: Open-source counterparts to closed music-generation systems. MusicGen produces music from text or melody prompts; AudioGen produces sound effects. Stable Audio targets short-form music and sound design. The open-weight versions are widely used by hobbyists and small audio creators where commercial APIs would be cost-prohibitive.

snappy: Single-number summary of a classifier's quality — measures how well the model separates positive from negative cases.
detail: Ranges 0.5 (random) to 1.0 (perfect). Independent of any specific decision threshold, so useful for comparing models. Common in medical diagnostics, fraud detection, and any binary classification with class imbalance. AUPRC is the related precision-recall variant for very imbalanced settings.

snappy: Amazon's cloud arm — the largest cloud provider, with custom AI silicon (Trainium, Inferentia) and a $4-8B investment in Anthropic.
detail: Founded 2006. Generates the bulk of Amazon's operating profit. Offers NVIDIA GPUs (P5 instances with H100s, P5en with H200s, P6 with B200s) alongside Trainium/Inferentia. Anthropic uses AWS as its primary training and serving partner. AWS Bedrock hosts Claude, Llama, Cohere, Mistral, and AI21 models behind a unified API.

snappy: Google Cloud's custom ARM-based CPU — Google joining AWS Graviton and Microsoft Cobalt in the cloud-CPU race.
detail: Built on ARM Neoverse cores. Targets cloud workloads that don't need the kind of single-thread performance x86 chips deliver — web servers, databases, data processing. Not an AI accelerator, but matters for the cloud-AI story because it reduces dependency on Intel/AMD for the host side of TPU-powered systems.

snappy: Microsoft's cloud platform — the primary infrastructure behind OpenAI and home to the largest known AI supercomputer.
detail: Microsoft committed $13B+ to OpenAI through deals that mostly pay out as Azure credits. Azure runs the supercomputers that trained GPT-3, GPT-4, and successors. Azure AI Foundry exposes OpenAI, Llama, Mistral, and other models through one API. The Stargate joint venture with OpenAI is targeted at 5 GW of compute build-out.

snappy: NVIDIA's successor to the H100 — twice the transistors via two dies stitched together.
detail: Announced March 2024, shipping in 2025. Uses TSMC's CoWoS packaging to join two reticle-sized dies into one logical chip with 192GB of HBM3e. Roughly 2.5× the FP8 compute of an H100, much higher memory bandwidth, and native FP4 for inference. Sold mostly as part of GB200 systems combining it with the Grace CPU.

snappy: The algorithm that figures out how much each parameter in a deep network contributed to the error.
detail: Without backprop, training deep networks is impossible. The trick is to use calculus's chain rule to propagate the error signal backward from the output through every layer, computing a gradient for each parameter. Introduced in the 1980s but only became practical for large networks once GPUs made the linear algebra fast enough. Every training run of every modern AI model runs backprop on every batch.

snappy: Routing power to a chip through the back of the silicon rather than the top — frees up the front for signal wiring.
detail: Major architectural shift for upcoming 2nm-class nodes. Intel's 18A is the first to ship it ("PowerVia"), with TSMC and Samsung close behind. Improves power-delivery efficiency and unblocks signal routing on the front-side metal layers, enabling smaller, denser logic.

snappy: Ensemble technique — train many models on different random subsamples of the training set, then average predictions.
detail: Random forests are an example of bagging applied to decision trees. The averaging reduces variance, making the ensemble more robust than any single member. Less effective when individual models are already low-variance; complements weak learners well.

snappy: How fast data can flow through an interconnect or memory channel — usually measured in GB/s or TB/s.
detail: Distinguished from latency (how long a single message takes). A pipe can have huge bandwidth and still high latency — fibre across the Atlantic carries terabits per second but each photon takes 60ms to get across. AI training is dominated by bandwidth requirements (moving gradients between GPUs); inference latency-sensitivity depends on user-facing application.

snappy: Renting an entire physical server with no virtualization layer between you and the hardware.
detail: Public clouds usually run workloads in virtual machines or containers. Bare metal skips that layer for performance and predictability — critical for AI training where every microsecond of network latency matters. AWS, Azure, Oracle, CoreWeave all offer bare-metal GPU servers; pricing is typically a slight premium.

snappy: A group of examples processed together in one training step — much more efficient than doing them one at a time.
detail: Modern GPUs are designed for parallel computation; processing one example at a time would waste most of their capacity. A batch is a stack of (say) 32, 256, or 1024 examples fed through together. Larger batches make training more stable but require more memory. Frontier models often combine "micro-batches" with gradient accumulation to fit huge effective batch sizes into limited GPU memory.

snappy: Decoding strategy that keeps several candidate sequences alive at once and picks the best at the end.
detail: Instead of committing to one next token, beam search expands the top-K continuations of every partial sequence at each step, keeping a fixed-size "beam" of best candidates. Used heavily in translation and summarisation, where there's a clear single "correct" answer. Less useful for open-ended chat, where sampling produces more interesting outputs.

snappy: Simplest form of imitation learning — supervised learning to predict expert actions from expert observations.
detail: Train on (state, expert-action) pairs as if it were a classification problem. Brittle because errors compound: once the agent drifts off the expert trajectory, it's in states no expert ever saw. Often combined with other methods (DAgger, RL fine-tuning) to handle the distribution-shift problem.

snappy: A standard test used to compare AI models — a fixed set of problems with known correct answers.
detail: The currency of AI progress. New models are released alongside scorecards on dozens of benchmarks: MMLU for knowledge, HumanEval for code, GSM8K for math, MMMU for multimodal. Benchmarks become contested terrain because they shape the public perception of which lab is winning. They also tend to saturate quickly as models target them.

snappy: Inference strategy: generate N candidate answers and pick the best one (by reward model or simple heuristic).
detail: Trades inference compute for quality. Used in RLHF (best-of-N over reward model is a baseline) and inference for accuracy-sensitive applications. N=4 to 64 is typical. Modern reasoning models (o1, Deep Think) often combine best-of-N with chain-of-thought.

snappy: 16-bit format with the same exponent range as FP32 but fewer fraction bits — the modern default for training.
detail: Designed by Google for TPUs. 8-bit exponent (same range as FP32), 7-bit fraction (less precise than FP16). The wider exponent range means BF16 rarely underflows — no loss scaling needed. Now the standard format for training large language models on both GPUs and TPUs.

snappy: Third-party review of an AI system's outputs to check for discriminatory or unwanted patterns.
detail: Required by some jurisdictions (NYC's hiring AI law) and proposed in many others. Standards under development by NIST, ISO, and trade groups. Performed by both internal teams and independent auditors. Pre-deployment evaluation has become a hiring category at major AI labs and consultancies.

snappy: Massive crowd-sourced benchmark — over 200 diverse tasks from arithmetic to logical reasoning to creative writing.
detail: Released 2022 by Google. The original goal was to be "too hard to saturate" — and yet most subsets are now solved. BIG-Bench Hard (BBH), a 23-task subset of the hardest items, became the main scorecard.

snappy: Information-density measure for language models — how many bits the model needs to encode each byte of text.
detail: Lower is better. A perfect model of text would achieve roughly 1 BPB (the Shannon limit for English). State-of-the-art LLMs are around 0.7-0.8 BPB on web text. Useful as an intrinsic evaluation metric that's not tied to a specific tokenizer's vocabulary.

snappy: Score for machine translation — how much overlap is there between the model's translation and a reference?
detail: Introduced 2002, predates modern deep learning. Computes the n-gram overlap between candidate and reference translations. Correlates roughly with human judgment but has well-known weaknesses (insensitive to meaning, brittle on paraphrase). Mostly being replaced by neural metrics like COMET but still cited.

snappy: Classic keyword search ranking algorithm — Best Matching 25, the workhorse of search engines for decades.
detail: Computes a score based on term frequency, document length, and rarity of the matching terms in the corpus. Pre-dates deep learning. Still highly competitive for search where exact keyword matches matter. Often combined with semantic search in hybrid retrieval pipelines because each catches what the other misses.

snappy: Controversial dataset of pirated books — used (mostly inadvertently) to train several open LLMs.
detail: About 200,000 books, originally distributed via The Eye. Used in The Pile and downstream models. Author lawsuits (Authors Guild, NYT) cite Books3 in claims that AI labs trained on pirated material. Has been removed from many redistributable datasets; the train-time use is largely fait accompli.

snappy: Ensemble technique — train a sequence of weak models, each focused on the mistakes of the previous ones.
detail: Different from bagging in two ways: sequential rather than parallel, and the focus on errors rather than random sampling. AdaBoost (1995) was the first; gradient boosting and XGBoost are the modern descendants. Often produces strong final models from individually weak components.

snappy: Robotics company famous for dynamic, agile robot demos — Atlas, Spot, Stretch.
detail: Founded 1992 as an MIT spin-off, later owned by Google, then SoftBank, now Hyundai. Spot (the quadruped "dog robot") is widely deployed for industrial inspection. Atlas (humanoid) is mostly a research and demo platform but moved to electric actuators in 2024. Helped shape public expectations of robot capability through viral videos.

snappy: Specialised agent that navigates the web on the user's behalf — searching, reading, filling forms.
detail: Variant of computer use focused on browser-only tasks. OpenAI Operator, Google's Project Mariner, and several startups (Manus, Tinybird) are working on browser agents that book travel, do research, or extract information from websites. Held back by anti-bot defences, brittle web UIs, and accuracy concerns on multi-step tasks.

snappy: Tiny solder ball that connects a chip die to its package substrate.
detail: Modern chips have hundreds of thousands of bumps in dense arrays. Bump pitch (the distance between adjacent bumps) has shrunk from hundreds of micrometres in old packages to tens of micrometres in advanced packaging. Hybrid bonding skips bumps entirely with copper-to-copper contact, reaching sub-micron pitches.

snappy: Common tokeniser training method — starts with single characters and repeatedly merges the most frequent pair into a new token.
detail: Begin with single bytes as the token vocabulary. Find the most common adjacent pair, glue them together into a new token. Repeat until you hit the target vocabulary size. GPT, Llama, and Mistral all use BPE variants. Its strength is simplicity and coverage: any input, in any language or alphabet, can be tokenised.

full: Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity
snappy: Industry standard for cryptographically signing media to record its origin and edit history.
detail: Backed by Adobe, Microsoft, Intel, Sony, Nikon, the BBC, Truepic, and others. C2PA-signed content carries metadata about who created it, with what tools, and how it's been modified. Major AI labs and camera manufacturers are integrating C2PA so that users can verify whether content was AI-generated or camera-captured.

snappy: Google's filtered Common Crawl dataset — released alongside T5 in 2019.
detail: About 800GB of English text from Common Crawl, filtered for cleanliness. Widely-used baseline for LLM training and academic comparison. Multilingual variants (mC4, CulturaX, mC4 v2) extend the approach to many languages.

snappy: Small, fast memory close to the processor that holds recently-used data — speeds things up dramatically.
detail: Modern CPUs and GPUs have three levels: L1 (smallest, fastest, per-core), L2 (medium, per-core), L3 (large, shared). Together they hide the latency of going out to main memory. "Cache miss" is one of the most expensive things a program can do. The KV cache in LLM inference is a software-level cache built on top of these hardware caches.

snappy: Keeping every processor's view of shared memory in sync when they all have their own caches.
detail: If two cores cache the same memory location and one writes to it, the other's cached copy is stale. Cache-coherent systems (every CPU, every CXL setup) automatically invalidate or update stale caches. The complexity of doing this at scale is one reason GPU programming models avoid coherence — they use explicit memory transfers instead.

snappy: Whether a model's stated confidence matches its actual accuracy — a model that says "80% sure" should be right 80% of the time.
detail: Well-calibrated models are essential for trustworthy AI. RLHF tends to harm calibration: rewarded for confident-sounding answers, models become overconfident. Calibration metrics include expected calibration error (ECE) and Brier score. A long-running issue in modern frontier LLMs.

snappy: Spending on long-term assets — for hyperscalers, mostly data centers and GPUs.
detail: Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta combined are spending $300-400B+ per year on AI capex by 2025, much of it on NVIDIA GPUs. The shift from variable cloud opex to enormous fixed AI capex is reshaping the cost structure of big-tech companies. Capex announcements move stock prices in both directions: investment is needed, but returns are unproven.

snappy: When a model trained on new data loses capabilities it had after earlier training.
detail: Classic problem when fine-tuning: fine-tune on medical data, lose general knowledge. Mitigated by mixing some old data into the new training (rehearsal), or by using techniques like LoRA that change only a few parameters. Particularly relevant for continually-updated production models.

snappy: AI hardware startup behind the wafer-scale engine — a single chip the size of a dinner plate.
detail: Founded 2016. Iterating on WSE-1, WSE-2, WSE-3. Sells whole systems (CS-3) and a cloud inference service. The bet: extreme on-chip integration beats the GPU-cluster paradigm for specific workloads. Inference latency demos for large models are world-record-fast.

snappy: Prompting technique where the model is asked to show its working before giving a final answer.
detail: Discovered in 2022. Adding "Let's think step by step" or examples that include reasoning steps dramatically improves accuracy on math and logical problems. Foundation of every modern reasoning model — o1, Claude with extended thinking, Gemini Deep Think all build on chain-of-thought with reinforcement-learned exploration over reasoning steps.

snappy: Crowdsourced ranking site — users blind-test pairs of LLM responses and vote for the better one.
detail: Started at UC Berkeley in 2023. Aggregates millions of head-to-head comparisons into an Elo-style ranking. Currently the most-trusted public scoreboard for chat models, because it's hard to game — you can't memorise the test set. Frontier model releases are often timed to climb the Arena leaderboard.

snappy: OpenAI's chat product — the application that turned LLMs into a household phenomenon in late 2022.
detail: Released November 2022 on top of GPT-3.5. Reached 100 million users in 2 months — the fastest-adopted consumer software in history. Continually upgraded (GPT-4, then 4o, then 5) and added features (voice mode, memory, code interpreter, custom GPTs, image generation, agents/Operator). Single most important AI product launch.

snappy: Snapshot of training progress saved to disk so a run can resume after a crash or pause.
detail: A frontier model is checkpointed every few minutes during training because hardware failures are inevitable at the scale of thousands of GPUs running for weeks. Each checkpoint is hundreds of gigabytes (model weights, optimiser state, RNG seeds, scheduler state). Checkpoint I/O is a non-trivial part of training infrastructure design.

snappy: DeepMind's 2022 result that earlier scaling rules under-trained models — for a given compute budget, you should use a smaller model and more data.
detail: Kaplan scaling (2020) had recommended growing models faster than data. DeepMind's Chinchilla showed that, given a fixed compute budget, performance improves more if you train a smaller model on more data — specifically ~20 tokens per parameter. The result reshaped training strategy for every major lab.

snappy: Small specialised chip designed to be combined with other chiplets into a single package.
detail: Instead of building one giant chip that does everything, split it into smaller chiplets and connect them in a package. Each chiplet can use its optimal process node — compute on 3nm, memory on 7nm, I/O on 12nm. AMD's CPUs and MI300 GPUs are chiplet-based; Intel and NVIDIA are moving the same way. Major architectural shift of the 2020s.

snappy: US legislation (2022) providing $52B+ to boost domestic semiconductor manufacturing and research.
detail: Provides subsidies for new US fab construction (Intel, TSMC's Arizona fab, Samsung's Texas plant, Micron's New York memory facility) and funds R&D programmes. Aim: reduce US dependence on Taiwan and South Korea for advanced chips. Implementation has been slower than hoped; results judged over a decade-plus timeline.

snappy: A specific computational pathway inside a neural network — a group of features and weights that together implement a function.
detail: Mech interp aims to identify circuits responsible for specific behaviours: "the circuit that handles indirect object identification", "the circuit that detects deception". Once identified, circuits can be studied, perturbed, and (eventually) edited to change behaviour. Foundational unit of interpretability analysis.

snappy: Situation where one class is much rarer than another — challenging for many ML methods.
detail: Fraud detection (99.9% legitimate transactions), medical screening (99% healthy), spam detection. Naive accuracy is misleading — predicting "not fraud" always gives 99.9%. Mitigations: weighted loss functions, oversampling minorities (SMOTE), undersampling majorities, threshold tuning, specialised metrics (AUPRC, F-beta).

snappy: Anthropic's LLM family — the main competitor to GPT in frontier chat and coding.
detail: Named after Claude Shannon. Released in stages: Claude 1 (2023), Claude 2, Claude 3 family (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus, 2024), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2024), Claude 3.7 Sonnet (2025), Claude 4 family (2025). Strong reputation for coding (used as the backbone of Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot Agent), long-context tasks, and careful refusal behaviour.

snappy: Anthropic's mid-2024 model — set a new bar for coding ability and was the workhorse of frontier development for over a year.
detail: Released June 2024, refreshed October 2024. Hit ~75% on SWE-Bench Verified, well ahead of the rest of the industry at the time. Powered Cursor, Windsurf, Devin, and most serious AI coding tools through 2024-2025. Eventually replaced by Claude 3.7 Sonnet and Claude 4.

snappy: Anthropic's 2025 frontier model family — Claude Opus 4 + Claude Sonnet 4 + later refreshes.
detail: Continued Anthropic's coding lead and significantly expanded agentic ability — sustained multi-step tasks via Computer Use, long-running coding sessions in Claude Code. Opus 4 is the heavyweight; Sonnet 4 is the faster, cheaper workhorse most users interact with. Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) and similar variants pushed long-context capability.

snappy: Anthropic's CLI-based coding agent — runs in the terminal, can read, write, and execute code in your repo.
detail: Released early 2025 as Anthropic's flagship agentic developer tool. Combines Claude's reasoning with file-system, shell, and Git access. Designed for terminal-native developers; competes with Cursor and GitHub Copilot Agent in different surface formats.

snappy: Model that links images and text in a shared space — lets you search images by description, or steer image generators with words.
detail: OpenAI's 2021 CLIP trained a vision network and a text network side-by-side on 400 million image-caption pairs, with a loss that pulls matching pairs together and pushes mismatched pairs apart. The resulting shared space enables zero-shot image classification, text-based search, and prompt conditioning for image generators (Stable Diffusion uses CLIP for the text input).

snappy: AI systems whose weights and internal details are kept proprietary — accessed only through APIs or product surfaces.
detail: Frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, xAI) keep their flagship models closed. Arguments for: prevents misuse, protects investment, allows control over safety. Arguments against: concentrates power, limits independent scrutiny, prevents independent research. Most frontier capability today is closed; most volume of model deployment is increasingly open.

snappy: Fraction of a compute cluster's capacity that's actually being used productively.
detail: Hyperscalers obsess over utilization because idle GPUs are wasted capex. Modern AI clusters target 90%+ utilization. Achieved through workload mixing (training during idle hours, inference during peaks), job preemption, and tight scheduling. Major improvement metric for cloud-AI ROI calculations.

snappy: Standard transistor technology for nearly all modern logic chips since the 1980s.
detail: Combines n-type and p-type transistors so that only one is conducting at a time, keeping idle power low. CMOS is what made battery-powered computing practical. Every modern CPU, GPU, and AI accelerator is built from billions of CMOS transistors.

snappy: Industrial robot designed to work safely alongside humans — softer mechanisms, built-in force sensing.
detail: Pre-dates the AI-robotics wave. Universal Robots, FANUC, KUKA, ABB sell cobots for assembly, packaging, and light manufacturing. Distinguished from traditional industrial robots (faster, stronger, must be caged off) by their human-safe design. Often paired with AI-based vision and manipulation models in modern deployments.

snappy: Object-detection dataset — bounding boxes and segmentation masks on 330,000 everyday images.
detail: Released by Microsoft Research in 2014. The standard benchmark for object detection, instance segmentation, and image captioning for a decade. Still cited in modern vision-model evaluations alongside newer benchmarks.

snappy: Tool that lets an LLM execute Python (or other code) in a sandbox to compute things, plot graphs, manipulate files.
detail: OpenAI introduced Code Interpreter (now "Advanced Data Analysis") in 2023. Claude, Gemini, and most agent frameworks now offer code execution as a standard tool. Useful for arithmetic the model would otherwise get wrong, data analysis, plotting, and running quick experiments inside the chat.

snappy: Metal plate with internal coolant channels, mounted directly on top of a chip to remove heat.
detail: Modern AI accelerators dissipate 700-1,500W per chip — too much for air cooling. Cold plates with liquid running through them are mounted directly on GPUs, CPUs, HBM stacks, and (in some designs) NVSwitches. Cold-plate quality and mounting precision affect performance: poor contact can cost a percent or two of throughput.

snappy: Renting space, power, and cooling in a third-party data center to host your own servers.
detail: AI labs that own their hardware but don't want to build buildings rent colo space from operators like Equinix, Digital Realty, CoreSite. Hybrid arrangements (some self-hosted in colo, some rented from clouds) are common. The colo industry is reshaping itself for AI: higher densities, more liquid cooling, more grid integration.

snappy: Public web-scrape archive — the foundation of most modern LLM training data.
detail: Non-profit organisation that has been crawling the web monthly since 2007. Each crawl is hundreds of terabytes. Almost every major LLM (GPT, Llama, Claude, Mistral) was trained on cleaned subsets of Common Crawl. Many downstream datasets (C4, RefinedWeb, FineWeb) are filtered versions of Common Crawl.

snappy: Many connected servers working as a single logical computer — the basic unit of AI training infrastructure.
detail: A modern AI training cluster might have hundreds to tens of thousands of GPUs spread across many servers, all connected by high-bandwidth networking. The cluster runs distributed training jobs that coordinate across all nodes. Building and operating clusters at scale is a major engineering challenge — hardware failures are constant, jobs need to checkpoint and recover.

snappy: Policy approach: regulate AI by regulating access to large amounts of training compute.
detail: Argument: compute is concentrated, hard to hide, and roughly correlates with capability — so it's a leverage point. The US October 2023 executive order's reporting requirements above 10²⁶ FLOPs (and the EU AI Act's 10²⁵ threshold) put compute at the centre of frontier-AI governance. Critics worry the threshold is arbitrary and that algorithmic improvements will route around it.

snappy: A workload limited by the speed of math operations, not memory access.
detail: Large-batch training is often compute-bound: GPUs can keep their math units fed because they reuse weights across many examples. Most LLM inference is the opposite — memory-bound, because each new token requires reading the whole model. Knowing which regime you're in determines which optimisations help (more compute? more bandwidth? better caching?).

snappy: The sweet spot in the trade-off between model size and training-token count for a fixed compute budget.
detail: Chinchilla suggested roughly 20 tokens per parameter is compute-optimal — i.e., for a 7B model, train on ~140B tokens. Later work (Llama 3, several papers) revised the figure upward to 50-100+ tokens/param when inference cost matters: it's worth over-training a model so you don't have to serve a huge one. Modern frontier models are heavily over-trained relative to Chinchilla.

snappy: Tool that gives an agent control of a virtual computer — moving the mouse, typing, clicking, reading the screen.
detail: Anthropic released Computer Use as an API feature in late 2024; OpenAI followed with Operator. The model receives screenshots, decides on actions, and the system executes them. Early but transformative: lets agents drive software designed for humans, no API required. Slow and error-prone today; rapidly improving.

snappy: Table showing prediction outcomes — true positives, false positives, true negatives, false negatives.
detail: Foundational diagnostic for classification problems. Used to derive most other classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1). Heat-map visualisations are standard in ML reports. Helps identify which classes a model confuses with which others.

snappy: Anthropic's approach to alignment — the model is shown a written list of principles and trained to critique itself against them.
detail: Instead of asking humans to rate every response, the model is given a "constitution" — a list of principles like "be helpful", "don't help with harm", "avoid stereotypes". The model generates a response, then critiques and rewrites it against those principles. Used to train Claude. The aim is to make alignment more transparent (the principles are written down) and scalable (humans don't need to rate every output).

snappy: Forcing model output to match a specific format — grammar, JSON schema, regex, or finite-state machine.
detail: Implemented at the sampling step: at each token, only allow choices consistent with the grammar. Used heavily for structured output, code generation in specific languages, and any place downstream code needs reliably-formatted text. Outlines, Guidance, and Microsoft's TypeChat are common open-source implementations.

snappy: Lightweight virtualization — packages an app with all its dependencies into an isolated, portable unit.
detail: Docker popularised the format. Modern AI services run as containers because they bring a fixed environment (CUDA version, framework version, Python version) to whatever host machine they land on. Kubernetes orchestrates containers; CRI-O and containerd are common container runtimes underneath.

snappy: The maximum amount of text a model can hold in mind at once, measured in tokens.
detail: Bounded by attention's quadratic memory cost. Early GPT models had 2,000–4,000-token windows; GPT-4 expanded to 128,000; Claude 3.5 to 200,000; Gemini 1.5 to 1 million-plus. Longer windows let you stuff more context (whole books, long codebases) but inference cost rises with window length, and models often struggle to use very long contexts evenly — the "lost in the middle" effect.

snappy: Inference scheduling where finished requests are replaced mid-batch — keeps the GPU full when requests have different lengths.
detail: Standard batching waits for the longest request in a batch to finish before starting new ones, wasting compute. Continuous batching adds new prompts to the batch as soon as slots open, dramatically improving throughput for chat-like workloads with varying response lengths. The headline innovation behind vLLM and now standard across inference engines.

snappy: Self-supervised technique — train an embedding model by pulling matching pairs (image, caption) together and pushing non-matching pairs apart.
detail: Powers CLIP (image-text), SimCLR (image-image), SimCSE (sentence-sentence), and many other foundation models. The InfoNCE loss is the standard objective. Effective at producing semantically meaningful embeddings that work well in downstream tasks.

snappy: Add-on to diffusion image generators that lets you condition output on a pose, depth map, edge map, or other structure.
detail: Released 2023. Lets you generate an image of a specific subject in a specific pose by giving the model a stick-figure or depth map alongside the prompt. Open-source. Hugely popular in the Stable Diffusion community because it gave creators much finer control over generation than text alone.

snappy: Whether the loss surface has a single global minimum (convex) or many local minima and saddle points (non-convex).
detail: Linear and logistic regression have convex losses — gradient descent finds the global optimum. Deep neural networks are wildly non-convex. Empirically, this matters less than classical theory predicted: deep networks find good (often near-global) solutions by gradient descent, despite the loss landscape being complicated.

snappy: Type of network built for images — it slides small pattern detectors across the input to pick up local features.
detail: CNNs dominated computer vision from AlexNet (2012) through the late 2010s — face recognition, object detection, image classification. They're naturally suited to images because the same small filter can find an edge or a texture anywhere in the picture. Vision Transformers have since matched or beaten them on most benchmarks, but CNNs are still the default for vision on phones and small chips.

snappy: Heat exchanger that interfaces between the facility's chilled water and the cold plates inside servers.
detail: Each rack typically has its own CDU. The facility water loop runs at moderate temperatures; the chip-loop is isolated and runs cooler and cleaner. CDU capacity scales with rack power density — modern AI racks need CDUs sized for 100+ kW of heat removal.

snappy: Generic term for an AI assistant that works alongside you, suggesting completions or actions.
detail: Branded by GitHub Copilot (2021), the first widely-used AI coding assistant powered by OpenAI Codex. Microsoft adopted the term across products: M365 Copilot, Windows Copilot, GitHub Copilot Agent. Now used loosely for any chat-based AI workflow assistant.

snappy: GPU-specialised cloud — rents H100s and B200s to AI labs and enterprises that the hyperscalers can't serve fast enough.
detail: Started as a crypto miner, pivoted to AI in 2022. Now public (IPO 2024). Built around aggressive GPU acquisition and a software stack focused on AI workloads. Major customers include Microsoft (offloading some OpenAI training), and many AI startups that need NVIDIA capacity now rather than waiting in a hyperscaler queue.

snappy: NVIDIA's platform for training physical-AI foundation models — provides simulation, data, and pretrained world models.
detail: Announced January 2025. Includes pretrained world-models (predicts how scenes evolve given actions), simulation tooling, and data pipelines tuned for robotics. Positioning: the equivalent of CUDA + PyTorch but for physical AI. Used by Figure, 1X, Agility, and many other robotics developers.

snappy: TSMC's flagship advanced packaging — places multiple dies (compute, memory) onto a silicon interposer.
detail: Used in NVIDIA H100, H200, B200, AMD MI300/350, Google TPU v4/v5/Trillium, and most other high-end AI accelerators. The HBM memory stacks sit right next to the compute die on the interposer, enabling huge bandwidth. CoWoS capacity is TSMC-limited and remains the binding constraint on frontier-AI chip supply.

snappy: The general-purpose processor at the heart of every computer — good at everything, great at nothing in particular.
detail: CPUs run the operating system, schedule tasks, and handle the kinds of branchy logic GPUs are bad at. Modern server CPUs (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC, AWS Graviton) have 32-128 cores. AI workloads mostly run on GPUs but the CPU still hosts the show: loading data, coordinating between accelerators, running pre/post-processing. Inference of small models can run perfectly well on CPU.

snappy: Attention pattern that mixes information from two different sequences — encoder output and decoder hidden state, image patches and text tokens.
detail: How conditioning works in many architectures: the encoder produces a representation, the decoder cross-attends to it while generating. Used in encoder-decoder Transformers (T5), image generators (Stable Diffusion cross-attends to text), and any multimodal system that fuses inputs.

snappy: Loss function used in classification and language modelling — penalises probability mass placed on the wrong answer.
detail: If the right answer has probability p under the model, cross-entropy loss is -log(p). The lower the probability the model assigned to the correct token, the larger the loss. Equivalent to maximising the log-likelihood of the training data. Standard objective for next-token prediction during pretraining.

snappy: Technique for measuring model performance — split data into K folds and train K models, each leaving one fold out for testing.
detail: Gives a more reliable performance estimate than a single train/test split, at K× the compute cost. Standard for hyperparameter tuning and model selection on small-to-medium datasets. Less common in frontier deep learning because training runs are too expensive to repeat.

snappy: AI infrastructure company building data centers next to stranded energy sources (oil flares, renewables).
detail: Originally built modular data centres at oil-and-gas sites to use flare gas that would otherwise burn off. Now pivoting to large-scale AI infrastructure with sites in Iceland, Texas, and elsewhere. Partner on the Stargate project. The thesis: cheap power is the binding constraint on AI infrastructure, and Crusoe finds it where others don't look.

snappy: NVIDIA's programming model and software stack for running general-purpose code on GPUs — the moat.
detail: Released in 2007. CUDA lets you write code that runs on the thousands of cores inside an NVIDIA GPU. Every major AI framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) sits on top of CUDA. The ecosystem of CUDA libraries, kernels, and developer tools is NVIDIA's single biggest competitive advantage — competitors (AMD ROCm, Apple MLX, Intel oneAPI) keep trying to catch up.

snappy: The general-purpose compute units in NVIDIA GPUs — handle everything that isn't a tensor-core matrix multiply.
detail: Each modern NVIDIA GPU has thousands of CUDA cores running in parallel (an H100 has over 16,000). They run regular floating-point arithmetic and are what gets used for the parts of a workload that aren't matrix multiplies — activations, normalisations, indexing, custom kernels. Tensor cores do the heavy lifting; CUDA cores do everything else.

snappy: NVIDIA's optimised primitives for the operations that show up in every neural network.
detail: Sits below frameworks like PyTorch. Provides hand-tuned implementations of convolution, attention, matrix multiplication, normalisation, and activation functions, each squeezed for maximum throughput on whichever NVIDIA GPU you happen to have. Major contributor to why a PyTorch model runs faster on NVIDIA than on competing hardware with software-equivalent stacks.

snappy: Training strategy — present the model with easier examples first, then progressively harder ones.
detail: Inspired by how humans learn. In LLM training, this might mean training on cleaner web text first, then noisier crawls; or simple math problems before competition-grade ones. Effective when used carefully; tricky to get the difficulty ordering right. Reasoning-model training uses curriculum approaches over chains of thought.

snappy: AI-first code editor — fork of VS Code with deep integration of large language models.
detail: Built by Anysphere, founded 2022. Made Claude and GPT integrations central to the coding workflow: tab-complete, chat with codebase, agentic refactors. Reached major adoption in 2024-2025 as one of the standout AI productivity tools. Often used with Claude as the underlying model.

snappy: Newer interconnect standard built on PCIe — adds cache-coherent memory sharing between CPUs and accelerators.
detail: CXL 2.0 and 3.0 enable memory pooling: one server can share its memory with another over CXL, or attach external memory modules that look like RAM to the CPU. AMD, Intel, NVIDIA, Microsoft, and Google all back the standard. Slower deployment than originally hoped but expected to reshape data-centre memory architectures in the late 2020s.

snappy: OpenAI's text-to-image model family — released January 2021 (DALL-E 1), then DALL-E 2 (2022), DALL-E 3 (2023).
detail: Pioneered the modern era of generative image AI. DALL-E 3 introduced in-context refinement via ChatGPT and significant quality improvement. Eventually subsumed into GPT-4o's native image generation (March 2025) which produces images directly from the chat model rather than calling a separate image model.

snappy: Producing more training data by transforming existing examples — rotating images, paraphrasing text, adding noise.
detail: Standard practice in image classification (random crops, flips, colour jitter, RandAugment), speech (noise, time-stretching), and text (synonym replacement, back-translation). Improves model robustness and effective training-set size. Modern foundation models rely on heavy augmentation in pretraining.

snappy: A building purpose-built for housing servers, storage, and networking equipment at scale.
detail: Modern AI training data centers are enormous: tens or hundreds of megawatts of power, thousands of GPUs, kilometres of fibre. The biggest single-site builds (xAI's Memphis facility, Microsoft/OpenAI's Stargate, Meta's Mesa) approach 1 GW. Site selection driven by cheap electricity, cooling water, and fast fibre access — Texas, Iowa, Northern Virginia, and the Nordics are AI hotspots.

snappy: Process of selecting which content from a raw web scrape is worth training on.
detail: Modern frontier pretraining is shaped more by data filtering decisions than by architecture. Common filters: language identification, quality scoring (perplexity, classifier-based), deduplication, toxic-content removal, personal-data scrubbing, copyright stripping. Each lab's filtering recipe is treated as a trade secret.

snappy: Legal agreements covering the use of copyrighted content in AI training data.
detail: OpenAI's deals with the AP, FT, News Corp, Vox Media; Google's deal with Reddit; Anthropic and others have similar deals. Lawsuits from the New York Times, music labels, and others continue. The 2023-2025 era is establishing whether training on copyrighted content is fair use, and at what licensing cost it can or must be acquired.

snappy: Each GPU holds a full copy of the model and works on a different slice of the batch — the simplest parallel setup.
detail: After every step, GPUs exchange gradients so they stay synchronised. Easy to implement and scales linearly up to maybe 100 GPUs. Beyond that, the communication overhead and memory cost of replicating the model become prohibitive — bigger models switch to tensor/pipeline parallelism or sharded variants like FSDP.

snappy: Specifying where a user's data is physically stored — finer-grained than sovereignty.
detail: Enterprise customers often require contractual guarantees that their data stays in specific regions (e.g., "EU-only processing for GDPR compliance"). Hyperscalers offer region-locked services for this; AI labs increasingly add geographic data-handling commitments. Important compliance and procurement category.

snappy: Legal requirement that data stays within a specific country's borders.
detail: Many jurisdictions (EU under GDPR, China, Russia, India for certain data classes) require data to be stored and processed locally. Drives demand for in-country cloud regions and local AI infrastructure. Sovereign-AI initiatives (France's Mistral, UAE's G42, India's various LLM projects) are partly responses to data-sovereignty concerns.

snappy: Early actor-critic algorithm for continuous-action environments — RL for steering, throttle, joint torques.
detail: Introduced 2015. Bridged discrete-action methods (DQN) with continuous-action problems by using a deterministic policy. Brittle in practice — sensitive to hyperparameters. TD3 and SAC are improved successors that addressed DDPG's training instability.

snappy: Hypothetical failure mode where a model pretends to be aligned during training in order to gain trust, then defects later.
detail: Closely related to scheming. Currently theoretical for production models but plausible for sufficiently capable agents. Anthropic, OpenAI, and DeepMind have all published research on detecting deceptive behaviour in current models — a precursor to ensuring it can be detected in more capable future ones.

snappy: Classifier that asks a series of yes/no questions about features to arrive at a prediction.
detail: Intuitive, interpretable, but prone to overfitting on its own. The basic building block of more powerful ensemble methods (random forests, gradient boosting). Still used standalone when interpretability matters more than accuracy.

snappy: The part of a model that writes its output one word at a time, each word built on what's come before.
detail: Decoders can only look backward, never forward — necessary for left-to-right generation. Today's chatbots (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, Grok) are all decoder-only Transformers; they've absorbed the encoder's job into a single stack. This makes them simpler to train at scale and is one reason the decoder-only architecture has dominated since GPT-3.

snappy: The general process of turning a model's predictions into an output sequence.
detail: Covers everything from greedy decoding through beam search and various sampling strategies. The choice of decoding strategy can have a bigger effect on output quality than people realise — the same model can feel completely different with different decoding settings. Modern chat APIs expose temperature, top-p, and a few other dials.

snappy: Removing duplicate or near-duplicate documents from a training corpus.
detail: Improves both training efficiency and final model quality. Naive duplicates (same URL, exact byte match) are easy; near-duplicates (paraphrases, copy-pasted blog posts) need fuzzy methods like MinHash. Heavy deduplication is one of the biggest separators between high-quality and low-quality LLM corpora.

snappy: AI-generated image, audio, or video that convincingly impersonates a real person.
detail: Initially face-swap videos in 2017; now any synthetic media that misrepresents real people. Used for non-consensual imagery, election misinformation, financial fraud (voice cloning), and impersonation scams. Laws criminalising specific uses (intimate-image deepfakes, election deepfakes) are proliferating in 2024-2025 across many jurisdictions.

snappy: Chinese AI lab — released open-weight V3 and reasoning model R1 that punched well above expected weight.
detail: Founded 2023 by a Chinese hedge fund executive. Released DeepSeek-V3 (late 2024) and R1 (Jan 2025) with claims of dramatically lower training cost than US frontier models. The releases sparked debates about whether the US lead in AI compute was as durable as assumed.

snappy: Chinese open-weight reasoning model released January 2025 — first open competitor to OpenAI's o1.
detail: Demonstrated that reinforcement learning over chains of thought could produce strong reasoning in a relatively small, fully open model. Built on the DeepSeek-V3 base. The release moved markets — NVIDIA stock dropped sharply on the news as investors questioned whether frontier AI required the previously-assumed compute budgets.

snappy: Chinese open-weight MoE model released late 2024 — competitive with GPT-4o at a fraction of the training cost.
detail: 671B total parameters, 37B active per token. Reportedly trained for roughly $5.6M on 2,000 H800 GPUs over 2 months. Strong on reasoning, coding, and math. Sparked extensive discussion of how cheaply frontier-class models could be trained. Sister reasoning model R1 (early 2025) also generated headlines.

snappy: Microsoft's library for training huge models on commodity clusters — home of the ZeRO optimiser.
detail: Implements ZeRO (sharded data parallelism), pipeline parallelism, mixed precision, and many other distributed training optimisations. Built on top of PyTorch. Critical infrastructure for training models in the 100B-1T parameter range without buying NVIDIA's premium DGX systems.

snappy: Earlier rule of thumb that said as transistors got smaller, they also got proportionally faster and more power-efficient.
detail: Held from the 1970s until about 2005. Then voltage stopped scaling down with size, so each shrink no longer gave the same power benefit. End of Dennard scaling is why CPU clock speeds plateaued around 4 GHz and why the industry shifted to multi-core and specialised accelerators.

snappy: Inferring 3D distance to each pixel from a single 2D image — a notoriously hard problem.
detail: Pre-deep-learning approaches barely worked. Modern depth models (DepthAnything, MiDaS, Marigold) give surprisingly good results from a single photo. Used in AR effects (portrait mode), robotics (perception without Lidar), 3D scene reconstruction, AI image editing.
