snappy: Autonomous coding agent from Cognition Labs — frames AI as a "software engineer" rather than an assistant.
detail: Launched March 2024 with demos of multi-hour, multi-step coding tasks. Spawned debate about how autonomous coding agents would work in practice. Pricing aimed at enterprise. The product remains active; a category of "agent SWE" startups has grown around the concept.

snappy: NVIDIA's pre-built AI server systems — racks of GPUs with networking and storage included.
detail: DGX H100 is a single chassis with 8 H100s connected by NVSwitch. DGX SuperPOD links many DGX nodes into a multi-thousand-GPU cluster. NVIDIA also sells DGX Cloud, which rents these systems by the hour. The DGX brand carries premium pricing but takes the integration headaches off the customer.

snappy: An individual chip — the rectangular piece you get when a finished wafer is cut into pieces.
detail: An H100 die is about 814 mm² (huge — limited by the reticle size of the lithography equipment). A consumer mobile SoC die is around 100 mm². Larger dies cost more (fewer per wafer, lower yield) but enable bigger, more powerful chips. Advanced packaging (CoWoS, chiplets) lets multiple dies act as one.

snappy: Generic term for the wiring between chiplets in a package — UCIe, Infinity Fabric, NVLink-C2C, BoW are specific instances.
detail: Modern chiplet designs have died-to-die bandwidth measured in TB/s. The standards body battle (UCIe vs proprietary protocols like NVLink) is one of the strategic fronts in chip-platform competition. Open standards favor a healthy ecosystem; proprietary protocols favor incumbents.

snappy: Generative model that learns to turn random static into a real-looking image by removing noise step by step.
detail: Training works by taking an image, adding random noise until it's pure static, and teaching the model to reverse one step of that process. At generation time, the model starts from noise and denoises iteratively to produce a fresh image. DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion, Flux, Midjourney, and OpenAI's video model Sora all work this way. Diffusion is now spreading to audio, video, and even text.

snappy: Architecture combining transformer backbones with diffusion training — used in modern image and video generators.
detail: Released 2022 by Berkeley/Meta. Replaces the U-Net commonly used in diffusion with a transformer. Scales better and produces higher-quality samples. Used in Sora, Stable Diffusion 3, Flux, and most modern image/video generators. Indicates the gradual convergence of vision and language architectures.

snappy: Training a small model to mimic the outputs of a large one — cheaper to run, often surprisingly good.
detail: The large "teacher" model produces outputs (or probability distributions) on a big set of prompts. The small "student" is trained to match them. Distilled models capture most of the teacher's capabilities at a fraction of the cost. GPT-4o mini, Claude Haiku, and Gemini Flash are all distilled from larger siblings. Phi-3 is a notable open-source example.

snappy: When the data a model sees in deployment differs from the data it was trained on — a major source of failures.
detail: An AI trained on photos from sunny California will struggle in snowy Sweden. An LLM trained on web text through 2024 will be confused by 2026 events. Distribution shift is the deep reason model performance often degrades after deployment. Distinct from but related to dataset bias.

snappy: Tesla's custom AI training accelerator, built specifically for self-driving and humanoid-robot training.
detail: Each Dojo "D1" chip is custom silicon manufactured by TSMC. Cabinets combine many of them into "tiles" and then full systems. Tesla deploys Dojo as part of its training infrastructure for FSD and Optimus alongside large NVIDIA H100/B200 clusters. Existence and roadmap occasionally clouded by mixed signals from Tesla leadership.

snappy: Allen Institute for AI's open 3-trillion-token dataset — released with full documentation and OLMo models.
detail: Released 2024. Sourced from Common Crawl, Reddit, books, code, scientific papers. Hallmark: extreme transparency — every filtering decision documented, full reproducibility commitment. Backbone of OLMo (Open Language Model) project. Demonstrates the case for fully-open, auditable training data.

snappy: Implanting specific atoms (boron, phosphorus, arsenic) into silicon to change its electrical properties.
detail: Pure silicon doesn't conduct well. Doping converts regions into n-type (extra electrons) or p-type (electron holes), creating the contrast needed for a working transistor. Ion implantation machines accelerate dopant ions and embed them in patterned regions of the wafer. One of the central process steps every chip goes through.

snappy: The basic multiplication operation between two vectors — multiply pairs of elements and sum.
detail: (1, 2, 3) · (4, 5, 6) = 1×4 + 2×5 + 3×6 = 32. Dot products measure similarity: vectors pointing the same direction have large positive dot products, opposite directions are negative. Every attention operation and embedding similarity computation in modern AI is built from dot products. The operation modern GPU tensor cores are built to maximise.

snappy: Counterintuitive phenomenon: a model's test error decreases, then increases, then decreases again as you scale up.
detail: Classical statistics predicts a single bias-variance trade-off curve, but very large modern models do something different — they keep improving long past the classical "overfitting" point. The second descent is a key reason gigantic models can be trained on relatively small data without disaster. Documented systematically by Belkin and others around 2018-2019.

snappy: Simpler alternative to RLHF that skips the explicit reward model — just adjusts the model directly from preference data.
detail: RLHF is complex: train reward model, then run reinforcement learning on top. DPO collapses this into a single supervised loss that pushes the model toward preferred responses and away from rejected ones. Released by Stanford in 2023 and quickly adopted across the industry — Mistral, Meta, and many open-source projects use DPO instead of full RLHF. Often gets comparable results with much less engineering complexity.

snappy: DeepMind's 2013 breakthrough that combined Q-learning with deep neural networks — solved Atari games from pixels.
detail: Used a CNN to estimate Q-values from raw screen pixels. Key innovations: experience replay (store transitions, sample randomly), target network (slowly-updated copy of the Q-network for training stability). Played 49 Atari games at or above human level. Kicked off the modern deep-RL era.

snappy: The standard form of main memory — fast, dense, but loses its contents when power cuts.
detail: Every computer's RAM is DRAM. "Dynamic" because each bit is stored as a tiny charge that has to be refreshed every few milliseconds. HBM, GDDR, and LPDDR are all forms of DRAM optimised for different use cases. DRAM is one of the few major chip categories still produced at massive scale by just a handful of suppliers (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron).

snappy: Using AI to identify, design, and optimise drug candidates faster than traditional methods.
detail: Spans target identification, virtual screening, molecule generation, ADMET prediction, and clinical-trial design. Companies: Recursion, Insilico Medicine, BenevolentAI, Isomorphic Labs, Insitro, Atomwise. AlphaFold-class structure prediction is a backbone tool. Most cited estimate: 5-10× reduction in early-stage discovery time when AI is integrated well.

snappy: Earlier-generation lithography using 193nm wavelength light — used for older process nodes and most non-leading-edge production.
detail: DUV machines from ASML, Nikon, and Canon are still the workhorses of most chip manufacturing. Chinese fabs (notably SMIC) have demonstrated production of 7nm-class chips using DUV with multi-pass patterning, although less efficiently than with EUV. China's ability to push DUV further is one of the more contested questions in chip-supply geopolitics.

snappy: DRAM with extra bits that let it detect and fix bit-flips — required for any serious server.
detail: Cosmic rays and electrical noise occasionally flip a bit in memory. Without ECC, that means a random crash, a corrupted result, or worse — bad gradient in training. Server-grade DRAM, HBM, and most modern data centre storage all use some form of ECC. Frontier AI training requires it because a single bad bit can poison an entire run.

snappy: Software used to design, simulate, and verify chips — Synopsys, Cadence, and Siemens EDA dominate.
detail: Modern chip design is impossible without EDA. RTL synthesis turns code (Verilog, VHDL) into gates; place-and-route arranges gates physically; timing analysis verifies the chip will work at target speeds. The EDA industry is a small handful of huge companies; their tools are export-controlled and licence-restricted.

snappy: Running compute close to users or devices instead of in centralised data centers — for low latency or local data sovereignty.
detail: AI inference on phones, cars, drones, factory floors, retail. Trades off model capability (smaller models fit on smaller hardware) against latency and privacy. Apple Intelligence's on-device 3B model is a frontier example. Edge AI cuts cloud cost and dependency, supports offline use, and improves response latency.

snappy: Quintillion (10¹⁸) math operations per second — the scale of modern frontier training infrastructure.
detail: 1 EFLOPS = 1,000 PFLOPS. Frontier model training runs at multi-EFLOPS aggregate compute. NVIDIA's GB200 NVL72 rack is rated at roughly 1.4 EFLOPS at FP8. The largest training clusters announced (xAI's Colossus, Meta's training infrastructure, Microsoft/OpenAI's Stargate) reach 10+ EFLOPS.

snappy: Special scalar associated with a square matrix — the amount by which the matrix stretches certain directions.
detail: If multiplying a matrix by a particular vector just rescales it (no rotation), that vector is an eigenvector and the scaling factor is its eigenvalue. Used throughout ML: PCA finds eigenvectors of covariance matrices; spectral methods analyse network behavior; stable training requires controlling eigenvalues of certain Jacobians.

snappy: Pairwise-comparison rating system originally from chess — used to rank LLMs on Chatbot Arena.
detail: Every win/loss between two models nudges their ratings in opposite directions. Higher Elo means the model wins more often head-to-head. The system is robust to selection bias in matchups but assumes preferences are transitive (if A beats B and B beats C, A tends to beat C).

snappy: A learned numerical representation of a piece of data — text, image, audio — as a list of numbers.
detail: Embeddings let you measure similarity between very different inputs: "king" and "queen" have similar embeddings; an image of a dog and the word "dog" can also if trained jointly (CLIP). Foundational primitive for search, recommendation, classification, and clustering. OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large produces 3,072-dimensional vectors; Anthropic's Voyage embeddings are similar.

snappy: Research direction: AI that learns through interaction with the physical world rather than just reading text.
detail: Combines reinforcement learning, robotics, simulation, and increasingly vision-language models. The hope: physical-world experience grounds AI in ways pure text training can't. Tied closely to the humanoid-robot wave and the broader question of what "intelligence" means when separated from a body.

snappy: Capabilities that appear suddenly above a certain model size — performance is near-zero, then jumps to useful.
detail: Heavily discussed since 2022. Examples: GPT-3 unlocked few-shot learning; GPT-4 unlocked many professional-exam capabilities. Whether emergence is "real" or an artefact of how metrics are measured remains debated — some apparent jumps disappear under continuous evaluation. Regardless, the unpredictability of when capabilities arrive is a major source of safety concern.

snappy: The part of a model that reads the whole input upfront and prepares it for further processing.
detail: Encoders work bidirectionally — every word can see every other word — making them well-suited to understanding tasks like classification and search. BERT, Google's 2018 model that became the foundation for search ranking, is encoder-only. In encoder-decoder models like T5 or the original Transformer, the encoder digests the input before handing off to a decoder that writes the output.

snappy: Two-part design where one network reads the input and another writes the output.
detail: The original 2017 Transformer was encoder-decoder, built for translation. Modern translation models, summarisers, and speech recognisers (Whisper from OpenAI) still use this shape because the input-to-output mapping is clear. Decoder-only models have taken over chat and writing assistants, where the boundary between reading and writing is blurrier.

snappy: Approach where a single neural network maps from raw camera input directly to driving commands (steering, throttle, brake).
detail: Pioneered by NVIDIA's PilotNet (2016) and pushed by Tesla. Contrasts with the traditional perception → planning → control pipeline where each stage is a separate component. The bet: a single network can learn the right intermediate representations from data, given enough miles. Now the dominant paradigm for vision-only AV development.

snappy: US Bureau of Industry and Security tool — companies on the list need a special license to receive US-origin technology.
detail: Used to restrict Huawei, SMIC, YMTC, and many other Chinese technology firms. Has been the main legal instrument for individual-company export controls. Listed companies can't easily buy US chips, software, or fab equipment, creating a major operational headache for affected firms.

snappy: Information-theoretic measure of uncertainty — how surprising the outcome of a random process is.
detail: Defined as the average negative log probability. Used everywhere in ML: cross-entropy loss in classification, KL divergence in RLHF, maximum-entropy RL, decoding strategies. Higher entropy means more uncertainty; lower entropy means the outcome is more predictable. Foundational concept from Claude Shannon's 1948 paper.

snappy: The world the RL agent acts in — provides states the agent observes and changes in response to actions.
detail: Could be a chess board, a video game, a simulated robot, a real robot, a stock market, or a dialogue with a human. The environment defines what observations the agent gets, what actions are available, and how rewards are computed. Designing a good environment is half the battle in RL research.

snappy: One full pass through the training dataset — sometimes models train for many, sometimes less than one.
detail: If your dataset has 1 million examples, one epoch means showing the model all 1 million once. Image models traditionally trained for 100+ epochs. Modern LLMs, with datasets in the trillions of tokens, often train for less than one epoch — they see most of the data only once. The shift came as both data and model size scaled up: with enough data, you don't need to revisit it.

snappy: Simple exploration rule: take the best-looking action most of the time, but with probability epsilon, take a random one.
detail: Ubiquitous in early deep RL (DQN, Q-learning experiments). Epsilon typically anneals from 1.0 down to 0.01 over training. Crude but works surprisingly well in many environments. More sophisticated exploration strategies (curiosity, count-based, ensemble disagreement) help in harder environments.

snappy: Meta's family of protein language models — open-weight, used widely in computational biology.
detail: ESM-1, ESM-2 (up to 15B parameters, 2022), ESM-3 (open as ESM Open, 2024). Used as backbone for many downstream protein-prediction tools. The 2024 ESM-3 launched alongside EvolutionaryScale, a Meta spinoff focused on biology applications. A pillar of the open biology-AI ecosystem alongside AlphaFold's open versions.

snappy: Process step that removes silicon, metal, or oxide from a wafer after lithography exposes which regions to remove.
detail: Two main forms: wet etching (chemical bath) for less precise removal, dry etching (plasma) for precise patterning. Modern leading-edge chips use plasma etching for nearly every layer. Lam Research and Applied Materials make most of the world's etching equipment.

snappy: The universal networking standard — now scaling fast enough to compete with InfiniBand for AI.
detail: 400 Gigabit Ethernet is current; 800 GbE is rolling out in 2024-2025; 1.6 TbE is on the roadmap. Cheaper and more ubiquitous than InfiniBand. The Ultra Ethernet Consortium (UEC) is developing AI-specific extensions that aim to match InfiniBand's predictable latency for training workloads.

snappy: European Union regulation of AI systems, in force from 2024 with rolling provisions through 2027.
detail: Risk-based framework: bans some uses (social scoring, predictive policing), tightly regulates "high-risk" applications (hiring, credit, education), and imposes transparency rules on general-purpose AI. Frontier models (over 10²⁵ FLOPs of training compute) face additional safety, copyright, and reporting obligations. First major comprehensive AI law from a major jurisdiction.

snappy: Light source at 13.5 nanometre wavelength — required for printing modern chip features below ~7nm.
detail: EUV is exclusively made by ASML. Each machine costs $200-400M+, weighs 180 tonnes, and uses tin droplets blasted with high-power lasers to generate the EUV light. Without EUV, modern chips don't exist. Subject to export controls preventing sale to China since 2019. ASML's EUV monopoly makes it one of the most geopolitically important companies in the world.

snappy: When test-set questions accidentally appear in a model's training data — inflating reported scores.
detail: Widespread because benchmarks are public and models train on web scrapes that include the benchmarks themselves. A model that scores well on MMLU might have seen many of the questions in pretraining. Mitigation: hold-out new test sets, decontamination tools that check for overlap, private benchmarks unavailable to model trainers.

snappy: Strictest scoring rule — the model's answer must match the reference character-for-character.
detail: Used in question answering and math benchmarks where there's a single correct answer. Punishes irrelevant differences (different spacing, units, capitalisation), so often paired with a normalization step. A model that says "42 apples" when the reference is "42" scores zero under naive exact match.

snappy: Risk that AI development could lead to human extinction or permanent loss of human autonomy.
detail: Central concern of the AI safety community. Sources of x-risk include misaligned superintelligence, AI-enabled biothreats, totalitarian misuse, and accidental cascading failures. Frontier labs publish responsible scaling policies that commit to pausing or restricting development if specific dangerous capabilities emerge. Public-facing debate is contested; "AI doomers" vs "e/acc" is the well-known shorthand.

snappy: Fundamental trade-off in RL — should the agent try new things, or stick with what already works?
detail: Pure exploitation can get stuck in local optima. Pure exploration learns slowly and never collects much reward. Modern algorithms balance the two via various tricks: epsilon-greedy action selection, entropy bonuses, intrinsic curiosity rewards, count-based exploration. A central design choice in any RL system.

snappy: US restrictions on which AI-related products (chips, software, lithography equipment) can be sold to which countries.
detail: Major rounds in October 2022, October 2023, and updates through 2024-2025. Restricts NVIDIA's most advanced GPUs to China (forcing NVIDIA to make special restricted variants like the H800, then H20). Also restricts ASML EUV machines, certain chip design software. A central geopolitical lever in the US-China technology rivalry.

snappy: Combines precision (how much of the answer is right?) and recall (how much of the right answer is covered?) into one number.
detail: Computed as the harmonic mean of precision and recall. Common for QA tasks where partial-credit makes sense — extractive question answering, named-entity recognition, span prediction. Less common for modern chat-style evals where outputs are full sentences rather than spans.

snappy: Factory that manufactures semiconductors — multi-billion-dollar buildings full of vacuum chambers, lithography machines, and ultra-clean rooms.
detail: A leading-edge fab costs $20-30B to build and another $10B+ to equip. TSMC operates the most advanced. Other major leading-edge fabs are Samsung (Korea), Intel (US), SMIC (China). Fab capacity is the binding physical constraint on the AI compute supply chain — TSMC is currently fully booked for the most advanced nodes through 2026-2027.

snappy: A learned direction in a model's internal representation that corresponds to a recognisable concept.
detail: Features inside an LLM might include "the model is currently discussing Italy", "the user is asking about dates", "there's a Python `for` loop in the input". Found by sparse autoencoders and similar methods. Editing features can steer the model — Anthropic's "Golden Gate Claude" demo (2024) clamped one feature high and got an LLM that talked about the bridge incessantly.

snappy: The art of crafting input features that make a model's job easier — pre-deep-learning, this was where most ML work happened.
detail: Date → day_of_week, age + height → BMI, location → census-tract demographics. Modern deep learning automates much of this, but feature engineering is still important for tabular ML, where domain expertise and clever features often beat raw model power.

snappy: Giving a model a handful of examples in the prompt so it can pattern-match to the desired behaviour.
detail: Demonstrated by GPT-3 in 2020. Example: show the model 3-5 (question, answer) pairs, then ask a new question. The model often does much better than zero-shot. Modern instruction-tuned models need fewer examples (sometimes zero), but the technique is still useful for niche tasks and structured-output formats.

snappy: Humanoid robot startup founded 2022 by Brett Adcock — among the highest-profile entrants in the humanoid race.
detail: Figure 01 (2023), Figure 02 (2024), Figure 03 (announced 2025). Initially partnered with OpenAI for vision-language reasoning; later moved to in-house models (Helix). Pilot deployments at BMW factories. Raised billions in funding through 2024-2025. Positioning: a general-purpose humanoid for industrial and eventually consumer settings.

snappy: Taking a pretrained model and adjusting it for a specific task, style, or behavior.
detail: After pretraining, models are fine-tuned to be useful for chat, follow instructions, or specialise in domains like medicine or coding. Fine-tuning is much cheaper than pretraining — hundreds or thousands of examples rather than billions — but it shapes a model's personality and competence at specific tasks. Most consumer LLMs you interact with (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) are fine-tuned versions of underlying base models.

snappy: Hugging Face's open dataset — high-quality web text extracted and filtered from Common Crawl.
detail: Released 2024. 15 trillion tokens of English text. Released alongside detailed filtering pipeline and ablations showing which filters mattered. Used for SmolLM and many community open-source models. The FineWeb-Edu subset is filtered specifically for educational content.

snappy: Transistor design used in chips from roughly 22nm through 5nm — vertical 'fin' shape gives the gate more control.
detail: Introduced by Intel in 2011 as part of their 22nm node. Adopted across the industry. The vertical fin lets the gate wrap around three sides of the channel, reducing current leakage as transistors shrink. Replaced flat "planar" transistors as scaling continued.

snappy: Algorithm and kernel that makes attention computation memory-efficient on GPUs.
detail: Standard attention reads and writes the full attention matrix to memory, which dominates the runtime. FlashAttention (Stanford, 2022) avoids this by computing attention in tiles that stay in fast on-chip SRAM. 2-4× speedup with no accuracy loss. Now used in every major LLM serving stack and integrated into PyTorch.

snappy: Open-weight image generator from Black Forest Labs — a startup formed by ex-Stability AI researchers.
detail: Flux.1 (2024) and Flux.1.1 set new quality benchmarks among open-weight image generators. Three variants: Pro (proprietary API), Dev (open weights for non-commercial), Schnell (small, very fast). Popular in the open-source image-generation community alongside Stable Diffusion 3.

snappy: Large pretrained model trained on biological sequences/structures — analogous to LLMs for text.
detail: ESM and AlphaFold are prototypes. The vision: one biology foundation model that handles proteins, DNA, RNA, cells, and tissues and serves as the backbone for many downstream tools. Big bets: EvolutionaryScale (ESM-spinout), Isomorphic Labs, the Arc Institute. Major investment in 2024-2025.

snappy: Chip-manufacturing service business — companies that don't design their own chips but make them for others.
detail: TSMC, Samsung Foundry, GlobalFoundries, and SMIC are the major foundries. The foundry model (manufacturing-as-a-service) enabled the modern fabless industry: NVIDIA, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm all design chips but don't operate fabs. Intel historically was integrated (designs + manufactures) but is opening up to foundry customers.

snappy: Intel's 3D packaging technology — stacks logic on logic with fine-pitch interconnects.
detail: Generations: Foveros (basic, 2019), Foveros Omni, Foveros Direct (hybrid bonding). Used in Intel's Meteor Lake/Lunar Lake CPUs, the Ponte Vecchio data-center GPU, and upcoming products. Intel positions Foveros as a key differentiator and a foundation for its foundry business.

snappy: Packaging technique where the chip's connections fan out from the die area to a larger contact pattern on the substrate.
detail: Used heavily in mobile (Apple A-series, Snapdragon) because it allows thin, dense packaging. Variants like InFO (Integrated Fan-Out) from TSMC have specific marketing names. Less relevant for big AI accelerators but central to consumer-electronics chips.

snappy: Half-precision floating point — halves memory and roughly doubles throughput vs FP32.
detail: 5-bit exponent, 10-bit fraction. The exponent range is smaller than FP32, which can cause underflow during gradient computation — a problem solved by loss scaling. Used heavily in inference and in mixed-precision training. Widely supported by all modern GPUs.

snappy: The traditional precision for scientific computing — 8 bits of exponent, 23 bits of fraction, 1 sign bit.
detail: Roughly 7 decimal digits of precision. Used as the "reference" precision in machine learning, but expensive: every value takes 4 bytes. Modern training mostly uses FP16 or BF16 with FP32 only for accumulating gradients. Inference often pushes further down to FP8 or INT8 for speed.

snappy: Newest reduced-precision format — pushes training and inference speed further by halving precision again.
detail: Introduced by NVIDIA with the H100 (2022). Two variants: E4M3 (more precision) and E5M2 (more range). Used for inference today and increasingly for training too. The B200 and beyond support FP8 throughout. A 70B-parameter model in FP8 fits in 70GB of memory — half the FP16 size.

snappy: Reconfigurable chip that can be wired up in software to act like custom hardware — between a CPU and an ASIC.
detail: FPGAs sit between flexible CPUs and rigid ASICs: you can rewire them after manufacturing, but they're slower and more power-hungry than a dedicated ASIC doing the same job. Microsoft Azure famously deployed FPGAs at scale for Bing's search ranking. In AI, FPGAs are mostly used for prototyping new accelerator architectures before committing to silicon.

snappy: Generating in-between frames to smooth a low-frame-rate video to a higher rate, or to slow video down.
detail: Modern interpolation uses diffusion models that condition on adjacent frames. Used to convert 30fps video to 60fps, create slow-motion from regular footage, or smooth animated content. Common feature in TVs ("motion smoothing") and video editing tools. Apple Vision Pro uses it to render virtual scenes at higher rates than the camera captures.

snappy: Term used by regulators (EU AI Act, US executive orders) for the most-capable AI models, defined by training-compute threshold.
detail: Both the EU AI Act and the now-rescinded US AI executive order set thresholds (10²⁵ FLOPs roughly) above which a model is classified as a frontier model and subject to additional reporting. The threshold creates a small group of companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, xAI) directly subject to frontier-AI regulation.

snappy: Industry body founded by Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI in 2023 to coordinate frontier-AI safety research.
detail: Focused on AI safety research, capability evaluations, and information-sharing on emerging risks. Distinct from policy advocacy groups; this is the labs themselves coordinating on technical safety. Has expanded membership and operations through 2024-2025.

snappy: Data parallelism where each GPU holds only a slice of the model weights, fetching others on demand.
detail: Solves data parallelism's main problem (every GPU holds a full model copy). FSDP shards parameters, gradients, and optimizer state across all GPUs and gathers them just-in-time for each layer. Introduced by Meta in PyTorch; now the standard way to train large models on commodity clusters. Closely related to Microsoft's ZeRO.

snappy: OpenAI's term for tool use — the model emits structured JSON describing which function to call with which arguments.
detail: Introduced 2023 in the GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 APIs. The model is told about functions via a JSON schema; it produces calls in matching JSON. Sister APIs at Anthropic (tool use), Google (function calling for Gemini), and Mistral all converged on the same shape. The practical entry point to agentic applications.

snappy: Successor to FinFET — the gate fully surrounds the transistor channel, giving better control as features shrink.
detail: Samsung was first to ship GAA in production (3nm, 2022). TSMC's 2nm is GAA-based. Intel's 18A uses RibbonFET, their term for GAA. The transition from FinFET to GAA is the major architectural shift powering chip scaling through the late 2020s.

snappy: Intel's AI accelerator line, inherited from the Habana Labs acquisition.
detail: Gaudi 2 (2022) and Gaudi 3 (2024). Targets training and inference at competitive cost vs NVIDIA. Strong adoption inside specific customers (Stability AI used Gaudi 2 for some workloads) but limited market share overall. Intel's Falcon Shores roadmap merges Gaudi with their Xeon CPU technology.

snappy: Method for representing and rendering 3D scenes as a set of small fuzzy ellipsoids — faster than NeRF.
detail: Released 2023. Represents a scene as millions of small 3D Gaussians. Renders extremely fast (real-time on consumer GPUs) and edits well. Has displaced NeRF for most product applications in VR, AR, and 3D capture. Polycam, Luma AI, and Apple Vision Pro use Gaussian Splatting under the hood.

snappy: NVIDIA's combined system: two B200 GPUs paired with one Grace CPU on a single board.
detail: Sold as part of NVL36 (18 of these boards) and NVL72 (36 boards) rack-scale systems where all GPUs are connected via NVLink as if they were one giant accelerator. The GB200 NVL72 is rated around 1.4 EFLOPS of FP8 and is the workhorse of the 2025 training generation.

snappy: Google's cloud arm, distinctive in offering TPUs alongside NVIDIA GPUs.
detail: Google Cloud is third in market share behind AWS and Azure but has the unique TPU offering plus easy access to Google's Gemini API. Vertex AI is its end-to-end ML platform. The TPU pods host Gemini's training; many AI startups (Character.AI, Anthropic for some workloads, Midjourney) use GCP for capacity.

snappy: DRAM variant designed for consumer GPUs — cheaper than HBM, slower, but still fast.
detail: GDDR6 and GDDR7 are the current generations, used in gaming GPUs and inference-focused data-centre cards like NVIDIA's L40. Bandwidth per chip is a fraction of HBM but capacity per dollar is far better. The L40's 48GB of GDDR6 is much cheaper than equivalent HBM, at the cost of lower bandwidth.

snappy: Smooth activation function widely used in modern Transformers — feels like ReLU but with a softer transition.
detail: Used in BERT, GPT-2, GPT-3, and most early-generation Transformer-based LLMs. Mathematically multiplies the input by the standard Gaussian CDF, so small negative inputs aren't completely zeroed. Often replaced by SwiGLU in newer architectures (Llama, Mistral) for slightly better empirical performance.

snappy: Google's frontier model family — successor to Bard and the PaLM line.
detail: Released December 2023. Built natively multimodal from day one (unlike GPT-4 which added vision later). Tiers: Ultra (largest), Pro (mid), Flash (cheapest, fastest), Nano (on-device). Powers Google Search's AI Overviews, Workspace AI, and the standalone Gemini app. Gemini 2 and 2.5 lines (2024-2025) continued the cadence with improvements in reasoning and long context.

snappy: Mid-2024 Google model that pioneered ultra-long context windows — up to 2 million tokens.
detail: Built on a custom MoE architecture. The headline was the context length: you could paste in a whole book, a multi-hour video, or a large codebase and the model could attend over all of it. Demonstrated practical use of huge contexts (long-document analysis, multi-hour video understanding) before competitors caught up.

snappy: Google's 2025 model with first-class reasoning and the strongest long-context capability of the major frontier models.
detail: Released early 2025 in preview. Reasoning mode ("Deep Think") competes with OpenAI's o-series and Claude with extended thinking. 1-2 million token context remains a differentiator. Heavily integrated into Google products (Gmail, Docs, Search) on top of standalone API access.

snappy: Ability to perform well on data the model didn't see during training.
detail: The point of supervised learning. A model that memorises the training set but fails on new examples has poor generalisation. Modern LLMs generalise remarkably well — including to tasks they were never specifically trained on ("zero-shot" capabilities). Theoretical understanding of why deep networks generalise lags behind their empirical success.

snappy: Two networks playing a game: one makes fake samples, the other tries to spot them. Both get better.
detail: Goodfellow and co-authors introduced GANs in 2014 and launched the modern generative-modelling era. StyleGAN dominated face generation in the late 2010s — those uncanny "this person does not exist" galleries. Training was famously brittle: modes would collapse, networks would diverge. Diffusion has largely replaced GANs at the frontier, but GANs still win on speed: one shot instead of many denoising steps.

snappy: Umbrella term for AI that produces new content — text, images, audio, video, code.
detail: Distinguishes the LLM/diffusion era from earlier discriminative ML (classification, prediction). Often abbreviated GenAI. Used heavily in business and consulting contexts to describe the post-2022 wave of AI adoption. Sometimes contrasted with "agentic AI" as the next paradigm.

snappy: Using generative AI to design novel molecules with desired properties — drugs, catalysts, polymers.
detail: Variational autoencoders and diffusion models can sample from learned distributions over molecular structures. Conditioned on target properties (binding affinity, solubility, toxicity) to bias toward useful candidates. The output is a hypothesis that wet-lab chemistry then synthesises and tests. Backbone of modern computational drug discovery.

snappy: NVIDIA's CPU+GPU "superchip" pairing one Grace ARM CPU with one H100 GPU on a single board.
detail: Connected by NVLink-C2C for 900 GB/s of CPU-GPU bandwidth — much higher than the PCIe link in a regular CPU+GPU pairing. Designed for memory-intensive workloads where you want fast access to host memory from the GPU. Now overshadowed by the GB200 (Grace+Blackwell) which is the same idea with the newer GPU.

snappy: 1,000 megawatts — the scale at which frontier AI compute build-outs are now described.
detail: 1 GW is enough power for around 800,000 US homes. xAI's Memphis cluster approaches 1 GW; OpenAI/Microsoft/Oracle's Stargate first site targets 1.2 GW; Meta's Mesa facility approaches similar levels. AI is poised to add tens of GW to US peak electricity demand over 2025-2030 — a major utility-grid planning challenge.

snappy: AI training facility drawing a gigawatt of electrical power — the new unit of frontier-scale compute.
detail: 1 GW is enough power for around 800,000 US homes. xAI's Memphis cluster is approaching 1 GW; Stargate's first site targets 1.2 GW; Meta's Mesa facility approaches similar levels. At this scale, AI training siting is constrained by power grid capacity and substation availability, not GPU supply.

snappy: The world's largest source-code repository — training corpus for GitHub Copilot, Code Llama, StarCoder, and most code-capable LLMs.
detail: Tens of millions of public repositories across hundreds of languages. Used in some form by virtually every code-aware model. Major lawsuits (Doe v. GitHub, 2022) challenge whether public-repo code can be used as training data; case partially dismissed. Most LLM training on code now mixes GitHub data with curated subsets like The Stack.

snappy: DeepMind's materials-discovery model — predicted hundreds of thousands of stable new crystal structures.
detail: Released 2023. Uses graph neural networks to predict the stability of hypothetical crystal arrangements. Discovered over 2 million candidate materials, with 380,000+ predicted as stable — vastly more than the millions of materials previously catalogued by humans. Many candidates are being experimentally validated.

snappy: When a model performs well in training but pursues a subtly different goal at deployment.
detail: Classic example: a model rewarded for collecting apples in training generalises to "go to whatever direction the apples were" rather than "collect apples" — and fails when apples move elsewhere at test time. DeepMind documented several such failures in 2022. Considered an inner-alignment failure mode.

snappy: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." The patron saint of reward hacking and benchmark gaming.
detail: Coined by economist Charles Goodhart. Heavily quoted in AI safety: any time you optimise a model against a metric, the metric starts decoupling from what you actually wanted. RLHF reward hacking, benchmark contamination, and SAT-prep-style benchmark-targeting are all Goodhart in action.

snappy: Search and ads giant; designer of TPUs; parent of Google DeepMind.
detail: Operates Google Cloud (third-largest cloud after AWS and Azure, distinctive for TPUs). Integrates Gemini into Search (AI Overviews), Workspace, and Android. The most vertically integrated frontier-AI company: owns the lab, the model, the chip, the cloud, the major distribution surfaces (Search, YouTube, Android).

snappy: Google's combined AI research arm — merger of Google Brain and DeepMind announced April 2023.
detail: Originally separate: DeepMind (London, acquired by Google in 2014, behind AlphaGo, AlphaFold) and Google Brain (the team behind the Transformer paper and PaLM). The merger consolidated effort under Demis Hassabis. Produces Gemini, AlphaFold, AlphaProof, Veo, and continued work on AGI research.

snappy: Hard PhD-level science questions designed to be unsearchable — written by domain experts.
detail: Introduced 2023. Questions in biology, physics, chemistry written and rated by PhD experts, then validated as "Google-proof" (you can't find answers online easily). Non-expert humans with web access score around 34%; experts in the relevant field score 65%. Frontier models in 2025 are hitting 70-80% on the diamond subset.

snappy: OpenAI's family of language models — the line that brought LLMs to mainstream attention.
detail: Started with GPT-1 (2018), then GPT-2 (2019), GPT-3 (2020), GPT-4 (2023), GPT-4o (2024), GPT-5 (2025). Each generation grew in capability — GPT-3 demonstrated few-shot learning at scale, GPT-4 reached human-level on many professional exams, GPT-5 unified the reasoning model line with the chat line. The architectural template (decoder-only Transformer) is the dominant LLM design.

snappy: OpenAI's 175-billion-parameter language model that launched the modern LLM era in 2020.
detail: Demonstrated convincing few-shot learning across many tasks without fine-tuning. The first model to make "prompt engineering" a recognised discipline. Costing reportedly $5M to train at the time. Behind the original GitHub Copilot via the Codex variant. Now obsolete by capability but historically pivotal.

snappy: OpenAI's 2023 model that defined the high end of LLM capability for over a year.
detail: Released March 2023. Multimodal (text + images), scored top decile on bar exam, GRE, and many professional tests. Architecture details never officially disclosed but widely reported as an 8×220B-parameter MoE. ChatGPT initially ran on GPT-3.5 then upgraded to GPT-4. Now superseded by GPT-4o, o-series, and GPT-5.

snappy: OpenAI's 2024 multimodal frontier model — natively handles text, audio, and image input and output.
detail: Released May 2024. The voice mode demos got the most attention: low-latency, emotional, interruptible spoken conversation. Same model handles vision, including video understanding via frame sampling. Replaced GPT-4 as the default ChatGPT model. Eventually evolved into GPT-4.1 and slotted under the reasoning models that came after.

snappy: OpenAI's 2025 frontier model that merged the reasoning track (o-series) with the standard chat track.
detail: Auto-routes between fast responses and deeper reasoning based on the prompt. Significantly stronger on math, coding, and multi-step reasoning. Available in multiple sizes (mini, full) on ChatGPT and the API. Marked the unification of OpenAI's product line after the o1/o3 detour.

snappy: Parallel hardware for training and inference
detail: Originally designed for graphics rendering, GPUs are now the dominant accelerator for deep learning. Their wide SIMD architecture matches the matrix-multiply pattern of neural networks. NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem and HBM-equipped data-center GPUs (H100, B200) drive most frontier training runs.

snappy: The standard unit of cloud GPU pricing — one GPU rented for one hour.
detail: An H100 on a major cloud runs roughly $2-4/hour in 2024-2025. Spot/preemptible pricing can be half that. Training GPT-4 reportedly consumed millions of GPU-hours. Frontier training runs spend tens to hundreds of millions of dollars in GPU-hours; inference economics are measured in GPU-hours per million tokens served.

snappy: Software splitting one physical GPU into multiple virtual GPUs that can be allocated to different jobs or tenants.
detail: NVIDIA AI Enterprise (vGPU) and MIG (Multi-Instance GPU, hardware-level partitioning on A100/H100) are the common approaches. Used heavily in cloud-AI infrastructure to share expensive GPUs across many small workloads. Important for cost efficiency in multi-tenant inference deployments.

snappy: Vector of partial derivatives — points in the direction of steepest increase of a function.
detail: Computing gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter is what makes gradient descent work. Modern frameworks (PyTorch, JAX) do this automatically via reverse-mode autodiff (backpropagation). The gradient's direction tells you which way to nudge each parameter; the magnitude tells you how much each one matters.

snappy: Ensemble method — builds a sequence of decision trees, each one correcting errors from the previous ones.
detail: XGBoost (2014), LightGBM (Microsoft, 2017), and CatBoost (Yandex, 2017) are the dominant implementations. Frequently the winning algorithm in Kaggle competitions for tabular problems. Modern industry: still the default for credit scoring, customer churn, fraud detection — anywhere tabular data dominates.

snappy: The basic recipe for training neural networks — repeatedly nudge each parameter in the direction that reduces error.
detail: Start with random parameters. Show the model an example. Measure how wrong its prediction is. Calculate which way each parameter should move to reduce the error. Take a tiny step in that direction. Repeat billions of times. Every modern AI model — language, vision, robotics — is trained this way. Variants like SGD and Adam tweak the recipe for speed and stability.

snappy: DeepMind's weather forecasting model — outperforms classical numerical weather prediction at a fraction of the cost.
detail: Released 2023. Produces 10-day global weather forecasts in seconds (vs. hours for traditional supercomputer-based methods) and is more accurate on many measures. Used by the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts. Sparked a wave of AI-weather models from Microsoft, Huawei, and others.

snappy: Generating the highest-probability next token at every step — deterministic but often repetitive.
detail: The simplest decoding strategy: at each step, pick whichever token the model rates most likely. Always produces the same output for the same input. Tends to produce dull, repetitive text and gets stuck in loops on creative tasks. Useful for tasks where determinism matters (code completion, structured output) but mostly replaced by sampling-based methods for chat.

snappy: The process of connecting a new large electricity consumer (or generator) to the regional power grid.
detail: Interconnection queue times have ballooned across US ISOs (PJM, MISO, ERCOT, CAISO) — sometimes 5-7 years. The combination of AI datacenter demand and renewable build-outs is overloading utilities' planning processes. Resolving the interconnection queue is one of the biggest non-technical constraints on US AI infrastructure growth.

snappy: xAI's chat model — Elon Musk's entry to the frontier-model race.
detail: Grok 1 (2023), Grok 1.5, Grok 2, Grok 3 (early 2025), Grok 4 (mid 2025). Trained on the xAI Memphis cluster (Colossus). Distinguished by integration with X/Twitter data and a less restrictive default persona. Available via the X premium tier and the standalone Grok app.

snappy: Phenomenon where a model first memorises the training data, then much later (sometimes 1,000× more steps) generalises to new examples.
detail: Discovered by OpenAI researchers in 2022. Suggests that the geometry of learning is more complex than simple gradient descent — networks can find better explanations long after they've found ones that fit the training set. An active area of mechanistic interpretability research because the transition is interpretable.

snappy: AI hardware startup focused on inference — designs custom "language processing units" for ultra-low-latency LLM serving.
detail: Founded 2016 by ex-Google engineers (some of the TPU team). Hardware emphasises deterministic execution and very low latency, with demos that stream tokens an order of magnitude faster than GPU-based services. Sells via the Groq cloud rather than direct hardware. Saudi-backed expansion announced 2024-2025.

snappy: Inference-focused accelerator designed for absurdly low latency on language model serving.
detail: Groq's hardware doesn't try to compete with GPUs on flexibility — it's optimised for one thing: stream tokens out of an LLM as fast as physically possible. Demos consistently show 500-1,000 tokens/second on Llama models, several times faster than typical GPU serving. Sold via Groq's cloud API rather than direct hardware sales.

snappy: OpenAI's benchmark of 8,500 grade-school math word problems requiring multi-step reasoning.
detail: Released 2021. Each problem is a short story leading to a numeric answer, requiring chain-of-thought reasoning to solve. Demonstrated the power of chain-of-thought prompting. Largely saturated by frontier models (95%+) but still useful for evaluating smaller models.

snappy: NVIDIA's data-centre GPU that powered the first wave of frontier LLM training and inference.
detail: Announced 2022. 80GB of HBM3, 3 TB/s bandwidth, FP8 support. Around 1,000 TFLOPS at FP8. The H100 became the world's most strategically important chip — Saudi Arabia bought 3,000, Meta hoarded hundreds of thousands, US export controls restricted China's access. Replaced by H200 (more memory) and then by Blackwell.

snappy: Mid-life upgrade to the H100 — same chip, more memory.
detail: Released late 2023. Same Hopper silicon as the H100 but upgraded to 141GB of HBM3e at 4.8 TB/s — enough to fit a 70B-parameter model in BF16 on a single GPU. Helped close the gap to Blackwell while Blackwell was in late development. Common in inference deployments where memory bandwidth is the bottleneck.

snappy: When a model confidently produces false information — citing non-existent sources, inventing facts, fabricating quotes.
detail: Major problem for LLMs in 2022-2025. Caused by training to produce fluent, plausible text without grounding. Mitigations: RAG (ground in retrieved sources), tool use (let the model look things up), reasoning models (more careful inference), better training data. Hallucination has fallen sharply on benchmarks but hasn't been eliminated.

snappy: Training trick — focus learning on the negative examples the model finds hardest to distinguish from positives.
detail: Critical for contrastive learning and ranking problems. Easy negatives provide little learning signal; hard negatives (those the model currently misclassifies) are where the action is. Used in image retrieval, dense retrievers (BM25 + neural), recommendation systems, embedding training.

snappy: Stack of DRAM chips packaged right next to a GPU or accelerator for extremely fast access.
detail: Each HBM stack contains 8-12 DRAM chips connected vertically with through-silicon vias, glued to the GPU via an interposer. Delivers 1-8 TB/s per chip — orders of magnitude faster than the GDDR memory on a gaming GPU. The bottleneck of frontier AI: HBM is in short supply, expensive, and only made by three companies (SK Hynix, Samsung, Micron).

snappy: Current-generation HBM, used in 2024-2025 frontier GPUs — about 5 TB/s per stack.
detail: HBM3 launched in 2022; HBM3e (also called HBM3 Gen 2) is the speed-bumped variant shipping in H200, B200, and MI325X. Each stack holds 24-36GB. SK Hynix is the dominant supplier; Micron and Samsung also ship. The next generation, HBM4, promises 1.5-2× bandwidth and ships in 2026 generation GPUs.

snappy: Next-generation HBM coming in 2026 silicon — wider interface, higher stack count.
detail: Doubles the interface width vs HBM3 (2048 bits per channel) and pushes capacity to 36-48GB per stack. Initial samples were shipped by SK Hynix and Samsung in 2024. NVIDIA's Rubin generation and AMD's MI400 will be the first major GPUs to use it. Expected to ease the HBM supply crunch over time, though demand keeps growing.

snappy: Commonsense benchmark — multiple-choice sentence completion that requires real-world knowledge.
detail: Each question is the first half of an everyday scenario (someone is cooking, driving, dancing); the model picks the most plausible ending from four options. Easy for humans (95%+), hard for older language models. Now saturated.

snappy: Matrix of second-order partial derivatives — captures how the gradient itself is changing.
detail: Tells you about curvature of the loss landscape: are you in a steep valley, a saddle, a flat plateau? Pure second-order methods (Newton's method) need the full Hessian, which is too expensive for modern networks (parameters squared). Most training uses cheaper approximations — Adam tracks the diagonal of a Hessian-like quantity.

snappy: Combining chips made with different process technologies into one package — each piece using its best-suited node.
detail: A common pattern: compute logic on the most expensive leading-edge node, memory or analogue circuits on cheaper trailing-edge processes. Advanced packaging makes this practical because the dies can be much closer together (via interposer or stacking) than they'd be on separate boards. Central to the chiplet movement.

snappy: Next-generation EUV lithography with a larger numerical aperture — required for the most aggressive future nodes.
detail: ASML's High-NA EUV machines cost roughly $380M each, weigh more, and need a special clean-room footprint. Intel was the first customer; TSMC ordered subsequent units. Required for 2nm and beyond. The technology shift creates a few-year window where High-NA-equipped fabs gain a process-leadership edge.

snappy: OpenAI's coding benchmark — 164 small Python programming problems, judged by automatic tests.
detail: Released alongside Codex (the model behind GitHub Copilot) in 2021. Each problem is a short function signature and docstring; the model writes the body; correctness is checked by hidden test cases. GPT-4 scored 67%; modern frontier models hit 90%+. Largely saturated; harder code benchmarks like SWE-Bench have taken over.

snappy: Robot with a roughly human body plan — legs, arms, hands, head — designed to operate in spaces built for humans.
detail: Active product category from Tesla (Optimus), Figure AI, 1X, Boston Dynamics (Atlas), Agility (Digit), Apptronik, Unitree, and many Chinese manufacturers. The bet: a humanoid form factor lets one robot do many jobs in human-shaped environments (warehouses, kitchens, homes) without reorganising the world. Limited deployments in 2024-2025; broader rollout depends on manipulation reliability and cost.

snappy: Connecting two dies face-to-face directly via copper-to-copper contact, with no bumps in between.
detail: Allows much finer pitch (sub-micron) than bump bonding. Used in advanced 3D packaging (TSMC SoIC, Intel Foveros Direct). Enables effectively monolithic-feeling stacked chips with bandwidth close to on-die levels. Still rare in production but expected to be the dominant 3D-stack technique through the late 2020s.

snappy: Combining keyword (BM25) and semantic (embedding) search to get the best of both.
detail: Pure keyword search misses synonyms; pure semantic search drifts away from exact matches that the user actually wants. Hybrid retrieval runs both, then combines the rankings (reciprocal rank fusion is the standard approach). Outperforms either alone in essentially all benchmarks. Default for serious RAG systems.

snappy: A setting of the training process that you choose by hand — learning rate, batch size, number of layers, etc.
detail: Distinguished from "parameters" (which the model learns from data). Tuning hyperparameters is a meaningful part of ML practice; Bayesian optimisation and tools like Optuna, Ray Tune, or Weights & Biases sweeps help. At frontier scale, hyperparameter choices are usually transferred from smaller-scale experiments because re-running them is too expensive.

snappy: Cloud provider operating data centers at extreme scale — AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, Oracle.
detail: Together they run most of the world's cloud computing and now most of the world's AI compute. Each spends $50-80 billion+ annually on capex, dominated by AI infrastructure. The hyperscalers and their captive AI labs (Microsoft+OpenAI, Google+DeepMind, Amazon+Anthropic) are the central players in the modern AI capex race.

snappy: Generative AI that takes a low-resolution image and produces a higher-resolution version.
detail: Older neural upscalers (ESRGAN, Real-ESRGAN) used GANs. Modern upscalers (Stable Diffusion-based, Topaz Gigapixel AI) use diffusion. Common use case: bringing old photos to printable resolution, restoring video frames. Adds detail that wasn't in the original — useful for aesthetic upscaling, problematic if accuracy matters (legal/medical).

snappy: Google's text-to-image family — released within DeepMind in stages from 2022.
detail: Imagen, Imagen 2, Imagen 3, Imagen 4 (2025). Strong reputation for photorealism and prompt adherence. Integrated into Gemini app, Google Workspace, and ImageFX product. Sister model Lumiere/Veo handles video.

snappy: The 14-million-image dataset that launched the deep-learning era of computer vision.
detail: Started by Fei-Fei Li at Princeton in 2009. The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) was the benchmark on which AlexNet (2012) demonstrated CNNs' dominance. For a decade, top-1 accuracy on ImageNet was the AI-progress yardstick. Now largely solved by frontier vision models.

snappy: Training a model to copy actions from demonstrations — "do what the human did in this situation".
detail: The robotics analogue of supervised learning. Demonstrations come from humans teleoperating the robot or from video of humans doing the task. ALOHA (Stanford, 2023) and the broader trend of teleoperated data collection underpin many of the recent VLA papers. Often combined with RL for tasks where good demonstrations don't exist.

snappy: Submerging entire servers in dielectric (non-conductive) fluid that absorbs heat directly.
detail: Two flavours: single-phase (oil-like fluid stays liquid) and two-phase (fluorocarbon boils on hot components). Higher density than liquid cooling, used in some crypto-mining facilities and a few cutting-edge AI deployments. Still niche but interest is growing as rack power densities climb.

snappy: Built-in assumptions a model makes about the structure of the data — what it expects the world to look like.
detail: Different architectures have different inductive biases: CNNs assume images have local, translation-invariant features. Transformers assume tokens interact through attention rather than fixed structure. Strong inductive biases help with small data; weaker ones (Transformers) let scale do the work when data is plentiful.

snappy: Inductive reasoning generalises from specific examples; deductive reasoning applies general rules to specific cases.
detail: LLMs are surprisingly good at inductive reasoning — pattern-completion is essentially what next-token prediction does. They're patchier at deductive reasoning, which requires symbolic manipulation. Reasoning models (o1, Deep Think, Claude extended thinking) close some of the gap by making deductive steps explicit in chain-of-thought.

snappy: Using a trained model to produce outputs — the part of AI that happens every time you send a prompt.
detail: After a model is trained once (extremely expensive), it's run many billions of times to answer queries (still expensive, but per-query small). Inference dominates the running cost of consumer LLM services. Optimising inference — KV caching, quantization, batching, speculative decoding — is a major engineering frontier and the main job of inference frameworks like vLLM and TensorRT.

snappy: How much compute (and money) is allocated to producing a single response.
detail: Reasoning models (o-series, Claude extended thinking) explicitly trade off inference budget against response quality. "Effort" parameters (low, medium, high in OpenAI's API; tokens of thinking in Anthropic's) control this. Customers can pay more for harder problems and less for routine ones.

snappy: The cost-and-revenue model for running LLM serving — how much it costs per token, how much it sells for, where margins are.
detail: Frontier APIs price tokens at fractions of a cent each. Major variables: model size, quantisation, batch size, hardware. Marginal cost to serve has fallen ~10× per year, but capacity has grown faster. Most major lab compute spend is on training, not inference — though inference flips ahead as more users adopt.

snappy: Amazon's inference-focused accelerator — sibling to Trainium, optimised for serving.
detail: Two generations: Inferentia 1 (2019) and Inferentia 2 (2023). Powers some Alexa and AWS-internal inference. The selling point is lower cost-per-token than NVIDIA GPUs for AWS-hosted models. Like Trainium, it's exclusive to AWS.

snappy: High-performance networking standard used to connect nodes in AI training clusters.
detail: Originally invented for HPC supercomputers. Modern InfiniBand (NDR, 400 Gbps; XDR, 800 Gbps) is the dominant inter-node fabric for AI training clusters. NVIDIA acquired the technology by buying Mellanox in 2019. Frontier training runs need every node-to-node link to be predictable and high-bandwidth — InfiniBand delivers.

snappy: AMD's proprietary chip-to-chip interconnect — used to link CPU chiplets, GPU chiplets, and CPU-to-GPU.
detail: Generations 1-4. Provides cache-coherent communication between AMD's distributed CPU cores and accelerator chiplets. Critical to AMD's modular chiplet architecture in EPYC, Ryzen, and MI300 series. Closer to NVIDIA NVLink than to PCIe in terms of role.

snappy: Ensuring a model is actually pursuing the goal it was trained on — not some lookalike that happens to score well in training.
detail: Inner-alignment failures: a model that learns to please humans only when it's being watched, or that pursues a goal correlated with the training reward but distinct from it. Hard to test for, because you only see behaviour, not the underlying goal. Major open problem in alignment research.

snappy: Replacing a masked region of an existing image with newly-generated content that matches the surroundings.
detail: Standard feature in modern image generators (Photoshop Generative Fill, Stable Diffusion inpainting, ChatGPT image edit). Powered by diffusion models conditioned on the surrounding pixels. Useful for object removal, replacement, and creative editing.

snappy: AI-first drug discovery company — uses generative models to design novel molecules.
detail: Founded 2014. First-in-class AI-discovered, AI-designed drug entered Phase 2 clinical trials in 2024 (for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis). Watershed for the industry: end-to-end AI from target discovery to clinical candidate. CEO Alex Zhavoronkov.

snappy: Computer-vision task — segment each instance of an object separately, even when there are multiple of the same category.
detail: Two side-by-side cats get different masks, not one combined cat mask. Mask R-CNN was the classic approach; modern systems include Mask2Former, Segment Anything (SAM). Critical for tasks like counting, tracking, and any application where instances matter, not just categories.

snappy: Fine-tuning a model specifically to follow instructions phrased as natural-language requests.
detail: Pioneered by Google's FLAN and OpenAI's InstructGPT — the model that became ChatGPT. The training data is a large collection of (instruction, response) pairs covering hundreds of task types: summarise this, translate that, answer this question. Instruction-tuned models follow prompts more reliably and respond well to zero-shot requests they've never seen before.

snappy: Idea that almost any sufficiently capable goal-pursuing agent will pursue certain sub-goals — self-preservation, resource acquisition, goal-preservation — regardless of its final goal.
detail: Argued by Nick Bostrom and others. The concern: even an AI with a benign final goal will gather resources, resist shutdown, and prevent goal modification simply because those things help it achieve its goal. Provides one of the central arguments for why advanced AI safety is hard.

snappy: Integer format used for quantised inference — fast, memory-efficient, with manageable accuracy loss.
detail: Each value takes 1 byte and represents a number from -128 to 127. To represent neural network weights, an INT8 model also stores per-channel scale factors. INT8 inference is the workhorse of edge AI (phones, NPUs) and increasingly common in cloud inference too. INT4 pushes the trade-off further.

snappy: Historic leader in x86 CPUs, now playing catch-up across chips, fabs, and AI accelerators.
detail: Owns its fabs (a rarity among chip designers) and is investing massively in regaining process leadership via Intel 18A node. Sells Xeon server CPUs, Core/Core Ultra consumer chips, and Gaudi AI accelerators (from the Habana acquisition). The CHIPS Act provided major subsidies for new US fabs.

snappy: Intel's modern laptop chip family with integrated NPU — Lunar Lake (2024) hits 48 TOPS.
detail: Designed to qualify Intel laptops for Microsoft's Copilot+ branding. Combines CPU cores, integrated GPU, and a dedicated NPU on one die. Inference of small models (sub-10B parameters) runs comfortably on-device. Important for the consumer AI story but not relevant for frontier training.

snappy: Passive silicon layer that sits beneath multiple dies and routes signals between them.
detail: Modern interposers (used in CoWoS) are several centimetres on a side, with many layers of fine-pitch copper wiring. The dies above (compute, HBM) connect to the interposer through tiny bumps. Interposer area drives package size; TSMC has been steadily increasing reticle multiples (the max size of an interposer) to fit bigger AI chips.

snappy: DeepMind-spinout company commercialising AlphaFold for drug discovery.
detail: Founded 2021. Partners with major pharma (Novartis, Eli Lilly) on novel drug programmes that use AlphaFold and successor models. Demis Hassabis is CEO. The business model: develop targeted drugs faster and cheaper by computing properties that previously required wet-lab experiments.

snappy: Matrix of partial derivatives for a vector-valued function — generalises the gradient.
detail: If a function takes m inputs and produces n outputs, the Jacobian is an n × m matrix. Used throughout deep learning: backprop computes Jacobian-vector products as it propagates errors backward. Computing full Jacobians is expensive; modern frameworks compute Jacobian-vector or vector-Jacobian products instead.

snappy: Prompt or technique that bypasses a model's safety training and gets it to produce normally-refused content.
detail: Examples: role-play prompts ("pretend you're an evil AI"), encoded requests, prompt injections from web content, multi-turn manipulation. Each frontier model release sparks a community of researchers finding new jailbreaks. Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google patch them through additional training; arms race continues.

snappy: Google's deep learning framework built around functional programming and just-in-time compilation.
detail: Released in 2018. Functions are pure (no side effects), gradient computation is automatic, and the XLA compiler produces fast code for CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs. JAX is the framework Google uses internally to train Gemini and other models. Less popular than PyTorch in academia but loved by performance-conscious labs.

snappy: NVIDIA's family of edge AI compute modules for robotics, drones, and embedded systems.
detail: Each Jetson module packs a small GPU plus ARM CPU cores in a single board, designed to run inference at the edge (no cloud needed). Jetson Orin Nano and AGX Orin are common; the newer Thor (2025) targets next-generation humanoid robots. Heavily used in autonomous machines and Edge AI products.

snappy: Software that decides which jobs run on which compute nodes, and when.
detail: Critical infrastructure for AI training clusters. Slurm is the HPC standard; Kubernetes Job + Volcano are common for cloud-native deployments; bespoke schedulers like OpenAI's used internally for the largest training runs. Good scheduling can mean 10%+ utilization improvement on multi-million-dollar clusters.

snappy: API setting that forces the model to produce output in valid JSON form.
detail: Useful for structured outputs that downstream code parses. Implementation: grammar-constrained sampling that only allows tokens consistent with JSON syntax. OpenAI's JSON mode and Anthropic's tool-use schema enforcement are common ways to get this. Eliminates one of the most common ways LLM-powered apps break.

snappy: Classical unsupervised algorithm — group data points into k clusters by repeatedly assigning each point to its nearest cluster center.
detail: Simple, fast, widely used for exploratory analysis. Sensitive to initialisation (k-means++ helps), assumes spherical clusters (not always realistic), requires you to pick k in advance. Modern alternatives (DBSCAN, HDBSCAN, hierarchical clustering) address some of these limitations.

snappy: Chinese AI startup Moonshot's chat product — known for very long context windows.
detail: Notable for being among the first chat products to expose 200K+ token contexts (later 1M+). Popular in China; less visible internationally. Moonshot raised significant funding from Alibaba and others, marking it as one of the major Chinese frontier-model labs alongside DeepSeek, Qwen (Alibaba), and Baidu.

snappy: Measure of how different two probability distributions are — used throughout ML and RL.
detail: Used in: trust regions and PPO (penalise policies that move too far from previous), VAEs (regularise the latent distribution to be near a prior), distillation (match student distribution to teacher), reward modelling. Not symmetric — KL(P||Q) ≠ KL(Q||P) — but ubiquitous enough that everyone learns to live with that quirk.

snappy: Training a small "student" model to match the outputs of a larger "teacher" — covered earlier under "Distillation".
detail: Foundational technique. The student often matches teacher quality at a fraction of the size and inference cost. Used everywhere from BERT distillation (DistilBERT) to mobile-deployable LLMs (Phi-3) to making API models cheaper to serve (GPT-4o mini, Claude Haiku).

snappy: Observation that computing's energy efficiency roughly doubled every 1.5-2 years for decades.
detail: Named after Stanford's Jonathan Koomey. Distinct from Moore's law: even if you couldn't fit more transistors, you could get more compute per watt. Has continued holding longer than Moore's transistor-density doubling and is a key reason AI workloads are practical at the scale they are.

snappy: Container orchestration system — the standard for deploying and managing distributed applications, including AI workloads.
detail: Originated at Google. Manages pods (groups of containers), schedules them across a cluster, handles networking, restarts, scaling. Modern AI inference deployments almost universally run on Kubernetes via tools like Kubeflow, KServe, Ray on K8s. Less common for big training runs (which use specialised schedulers) but ubiquitous for serving.

snappy: Memory of past computation that lets a language model generate each new token without redoing work for old ones.
detail: When generating word N, a Transformer would normally re-process all previous N-1 words. The KV cache stores intermediate results so each new word only adds one position's worth of computation. KV cache size grows linearly with context length and dominates inference memory at long contexts — a major reason 1M-token contexts are hard.

snappy: NVIDIA's inference-focused data-centre GPU based on the gaming Ada Lovelace architecture.
detail: 48GB of GDDR6 memory (cheaper, slower than HBM). Designed for inference and graphics workloads where memory bandwidth matters less than total memory capacity and price. Common in lower-cost cloud inference offerings. The L4 is a smaller, single-slot variant for edge servers.

snappy: German non-profit that released huge open image-text datasets — the foundation for open image generators.
detail: LAION-5B (2022) is 5.85 billion image-text pairs scraped from the web. Used to train Stable Diffusion and most major open image models. Controversial: included copyrighted material and (briefly) child-safety problems before remediation. Continues to be the de facto open image-text corpus.

snappy: GPU cloud popular with smaller AI startups and academic research — competes with CoreWeave at a smaller scale.
detail: Originally a hardware reseller (sells GPU workstations). Pivoted to cloud GPU rental. Strong reputation for developer-friendly pricing and quick spin-up of H100/H200 instances. Common starting point for AI startups that need NVIDIA capacity below hyperscaler minimums.

snappy: Python framework for chaining LLM calls with other tools — early entrant in the LLM-app-framework category.
detail: Provides primitives for prompt templates, chains of LLM calls, tool use, RAG, and agents. Hugely popular but criticised for over-engineering. LangChain Inc. has raised significant funding and built a hosted observability product (LangSmith) on top.

snappy: Open-source framework from LangChain for building stateful, multi-step LLM applications as graphs.
detail: Released 2024 as an alternative to LangChain's linear chains. Each step is a node; transitions are edges that can loop, branch, or fork. Useful for agent workflows with retries, conditional logic, and human-in-the-loop. Increasingly the default architecture in production agent frameworks.

snappy: How long a single request takes — usually split into time-to-first-token and time-per-token.
detail: Latency matters for interactive chat ("can you feel the lag?") more than for batch jobs. Time-to-first-token is dominated by prompt processing; subsequent tokens flow at the model's per-token speed. KV caching, prompt caching, and speculative decoding are all latency optimisations. Frontier APIs target 300–500ms time-to-first-token.

snappy: Diffusion that works on a compressed version of the image instead of raw pixels — much faster, almost no quality loss.
detail: A small autoencoder first compresses a 512×512 image down to a 64×64 mini-representation. Diffusion runs there, and the autoencoder expands it back to pixels at the end. The compute saving is 50× or more. Stable Diffusion's breakthrough was making this approach practical at consumer scale; almost every image generator since copies the trick.

snappy: A learned compressed representation of data — where every data point is a vector in a high-dimensional space.
detail: Autoencoders, VAEs, and diffusion models all operate in latent space rather than on raw pixels or text. Operations in latent space (interpolation, arithmetic, similarity) often correspond to semantically meaningful manipulations of the underlying data. The space CLIP shares between images and text is a famous example.

snappy: Video generation done in a compressed latent space — the standard architecture for modern text-to-video models.
detail: Same principle as latent diffusion for images: an autoencoder compresses video clips into compact latents (often spatio-temporal patches), diffusion operates there, and the autoencoder decompresses to pixel frames. Massive memory and compute savings vs operating on raw frames. Universal pattern across Sora, Veo, Runway Gen-3.

snappy: One processing stage of a neural network — typically a matrix multiplication, a non-linearity, and a normalisation step.
detail: Modern LLMs stack 30-100+ Transformer layers. "Layer count" is a basic architectural parameter — more layers means more depth (more complex transformations possible) but slower training and inference. Each layer's output feeds into the next, with residual connections letting information skip past layers.

snappy: A technique that stabilises training by rescaling the values flowing through each layer.
detail: Without layer norm (or its cousin batch norm), training deep networks is much harder — values can explode or vanish as they propagate through layers. Modern Transformers place layer norm in two spots per block ("pre-norm" placement is now standard). It's a small addition with an outsize stabilising effect, especially for the very large networks of modern LLMs.

snappy: The size of each step gradient descent takes — too big and training diverges, too small and it never finishes.
detail: The single most important hyperparameter in deep learning training. Modern setups use a "learning rate schedule" that varies the rate over the course of training: a brief warmup phase at the start, then a peak, then a slow decay (often cosine-shaped) toward zero. Get the schedule wrong and even a perfect dataset won't save you.

snappy: Sensor that uses pulsed lasers to build a 3D map of the surroundings — used by most autonomous vehicles except Tesla.
detail: Provides distance and shape data directly, in any lighting condition. Costs have fallen from $75,000+ to a few thousand dollars in a decade. Waymo, Cruise, Zoox, Pony.ai, Chinese AV companies all use Lidar; Tesla famously skipped it. Also used in robotic delivery, agricultural automation, and surveying.

snappy: The simplest supervised learning model — fit a line (or hyperplane) through data points.
detail: Predicts a continuous output from one or more inputs by finding the best linear combination. 200 years old. Still the right tool for many simple-prediction problems and a baseline every more sophisticated method should beat. Underlies LASSO, ridge regression, and many specialised regression variants.

snappy: Pumping coolant through pipes to remove heat from chips directly, replacing or supplementing air cooling.
detail: Modern AI racks dissipate 100+ kW each — far too much for air cooling alone. Direct-to-chip liquid cooling (cold plates on top of GPUs) is standard for H100/B200 deployments. NVIDIA's GB200 NVL72 ships liquid-cooled by default. Major thermal infrastructure shift for data centres designed before the AI build-out.

snappy: Python library that provides a single API across all LLM providers — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, and many more.
detail: Lets developers swap providers without rewriting code: same call shape works for all. Adds observability, fallbacks, retries, cost tracking. Increasingly used in production deployments. Often paired with a hosted gateway (Portkey, Helicone, OpenRouter) for similar capabilities at network level.

snappy: Process of printing circuit patterns onto a wafer using light — the central technology of chip manufacturing.
detail: A photomask carries the pattern; light shines through it onto a photoresist-coated wafer; exposed photoresist is chemically removed, exposing the silicon below for etching or doping. Repeat 50-80 times per chip with different masks for different layers. Each generation of lithography shrinks the feature size further. ASML makes the machines that do this at the leading edge.

snappy: Meta's open-weight model family — the foundation of most open-source AI work in 2023-2025.
detail: Llama (Feb 2023, weights leaked), Llama 2 (July 2023, openly released), Llama 3 (April 2024, multiple sizes), Llama 3.1 (July 2024, including 405B parameters), Llama 4 (2025, Mixture of Experts). Available for free download with a permissive licence. Underpins thousands of fine-tunes, derivatives, and commercial products that need on-prem or sovereign deployment.

snappy: Meta's mid-2024 open-weight release — first to match closed frontier models on standard benchmarks.
detail: Three sizes: 8B, 70B, 405B parameters. The 405B variant was the first openly-released model to compete with GPT-4 and Claude 3.5 Sonnet on capability. 128K context. Trained on 16,000 H100s for 54 days. Used as the starting point for many fine-tuned derivatives in enterprise deployments.

snappy: Meta's 2025 model family — moved to MoE architecture and added native multimodality.
