detail: Variants: Scout (small), Maverick (mid), Behemoth (largest, 288B active parameters out of 2T total). MoE design lets Llama 4 scale total capacity without proportionally raising inference cost. Strong on long context (10M tokens advertised). Continues Meta's strategy of open-weight releases driving the open ecosystem.

snappy: C++ library that runs LLMs on consumer hardware — CPUs, Apple Silicon, gaming GPUs.
detail: Started by Georgi Gerganov in 2023 as a weekend project to run Llama on a MacBook. Now the de facto way to run open-source LLMs locally. Supports aggressive quantisation (2-bit, 3-bit, 4-bit), runs on Apple Metal, CUDA, Vulkan, AVX. Backbone of consumer apps like Ollama, LM Studio, GPT4All.

snappy: Open-source framework for building RAG and agentic applications — competitor to LangChain.
detail: Originally GPT Index (2022). Focuses on data ingestion, indexing, and retrieval for LLM apps. Strong tooling for connecting to many data sources (SQL, PDFs, APIs, notion, etc.). Popular for the data-heavy side of agent development.

snappy: Using a strong language model to grade other models' outputs — much cheaper than human raters.
detail: For open-ended tasks (creative writing, summarisation, helpful chat) there's no single right answer, so n-gram overlap metrics fail. LLM-as-judge asks GPT-4, Claude, or Gemini to compare two responses and pick the better one. Cheaper than humans, fast, but inherits the judge model's biases (it tends to prefer outputs that resemble its own style).

snappy: How a robot moves through space — walking, running, climbing.
detail: Quadruped locomotion (Boston Dynamics Spot, Anymal, Unitree) is largely solved. Bipedal locomotion is harder but rapidly advancing (Boston Dynamics Atlas, Figure, Unitree H1). Recent breakthroughs (parkour, agile recovery from disturbances) come from large-scale RL training in simulation transferred to real hardware.

snappy: Combination of transistors that implements a basic boolean function — AND, OR, NOT, NAND, etc.
detail: All digital computation is built from logic gates. Modern chips use millions to billions of them, arranged into ever-larger circuits: full adders, registers, ALUs, cores. "Standard cell" libraries provided by EDA tools give designers pre-laid-out versions of common gates, dramatically simplifying chip design.

snappy: Linear model for classification — applies a sigmoid to a linear combination of inputs and learns the parameters.
detail: Despite the name, used for classification, not regression. Trained with cross-entropy loss. Despite being simple, it remains a strong baseline for tabular classification problems. Conceptually it's a one-layer neural network. Modern deep learning is often built up by replacing pieces of logistic regression with more complex components.

snappy: The raw, unnormalised scores a model outputs for each possible next token, before they're turned into probabilities.
detail: Logits are what comes out of the final layer of a language model — one number per vocabulary entry. Softmax converts them into a probability distribution. "Logit bias" APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) let developers nudge specific tokens up or down before sampling, useful for forcing JSON output or banning specific words.

snappy: A task that takes many steps and multiple hours or days to complete — beyond what any single prompt can deliver.
detail: Examples: writing a research paper, debugging a complex codebase, planning a multi-week project. Long-horizon tasks stress agent design — the model has to maintain coherence over many sub-tasks, handle interruptions, manage memory. Frontier of agent research as of 2024-2025.

snappy: Persistent storage for an agent — facts, preferences, prior conversations remembered across sessions.
detail: Implemented as RAG over a private database of the agent's history. The model writes summaries or facts to memory after each interaction; retrieves relevant ones at the start of new conversations. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini all have built-in memory features in 2024-2025. Frameworks like Mem0 and LangGraph standardise the pattern.

snappy: Efficient fine-tuning technique that adds small trainable matrices alongside the frozen original weights.
detail: Instead of fine-tuning all 70 billion parameters of a model, LoRA freezes them and adds tiny trainable "adapter" matrices alongside — typically 0.1-1% the size. Trains roughly 100× faster and the resulting adapters are small files you can swap in and out. Released by Microsoft Research in 2021 and now the dominant approach to customising open-source models. Hugging Face hosts thousands of community-trained LoRAs.

snappy: The formula a model uses to measure how wrong it is — what training tries to minimise.
detail: Different tasks use different losses: cross-entropy for language and classification, mean squared error for regression, contrastive losses for similarity learning. The choice of loss is one of the most important design decisions in ML — it defines what "good" means for the model. RLHF replaces a hand-coded loss with a learned reward model trained on human preferences.

snappy: Conjecture that within a randomly-initialised network, there's a small sparse subnetwork that — if trained alone — would do almost as well.
detail: Proposed in 2018 by Frankle and Carbin at MIT. The implication: most of a large network's parameters are dead weight, and pruning could in principle preserve most capability. Influenced thinking about pruning, sparse training, and Mixture of Experts. The strong form of the hypothesis is contested but the empirical phenomenon is real.

snappy: Low-power DRAM used in phones, laptops, and some data-centre AI chips.
detail: LPDDR5X is the current consumer generation; LPDDR6 ships in 2025. Apple's M-series chips use LPDDR5X as unified system memory. NVIDIA's Grace CPU uses LPDDR5X to keep power down. The Apple approach lets the GPU access the full system memory at decent bandwidth, which is why Macs are surprisingly good at LLM inference for their size.

snappy: Improved RNN with built-in machinery for remembering things across long sequences — the workhorse of language AI before Transformers.
detail: Introduced in 1997 by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, dominant for sequence tasks through the 2010s. The trick is a small set of internal switches that decide what to keep in memory, what to drop, and what to pass on at each step. Google Translate ran on LSTMs in production before the Transformer takeover, and LSTMs are still useful when memory and compute are tight — for example on phones.

snappy: Newer alternative to Transformers, designed for very long inputs — runs in linear time instead of quadratic.
detail: Released late 2023 by Gu and Dao. Mamba builds on "state space models": it keeps a compact rolling memory of the sequence so far, updated linearly per token. Its appeal is long-context efficiency — promising for tasks where Transformers' quadratic attention is prohibitive (long documents, video). Decoder-only Transformers remain dominant for general LLMs, but Mamba and its descendants are the leading alternative.

snappy: A surface or shape in high-dimensional space — the structure machine learning tries to find inside data.
detail: Real data tends to lie near low-dimensional manifolds within huge ambient spaces. Images of human faces live on a tiny manifold inside the space of all possible images. Modern generative models (diffusion, VAEs) implicitly learn data manifolds. Useful concept for thinking about embeddings, dimensionality reduction, and generalisation.

snappy: The robotic art of using arms and hands to interact with objects — picking, placing, assembling, grasping.
detail: Notoriously hard. Decades of research went into pick-and-place for known objects in controlled settings. Modern foundation-model approaches (VLAs, ALOHA, Pi-0) are moving toward general manipulation: "pick up that tool you've never seen before". One of the biggest gaps between current robot capability and human-level dexterity.

snappy: 12,500 competition-math problems — significantly harder than GSM8K, drawn from AMC, AIME, etc.
detail: Introduced 2021. Includes algebra, geometry, number theory, probability, calculus, precalculus. Problems can require several pages of derivation. Frontier models with reasoning (OpenAI o1, Gemini's Deep Think mode, Claude with extended thinking) now hit 90%+, but it took years to get there.

snappy: A rectangular grid of numbers — what neural network weights are mostly made of.
detail: If a vector is a 1D list, a matrix is a 2D grid. A layer that transforms a 1,024-dim input into a 4,096-dim output uses a 4,096 × 1,024 matrix of weights. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters mostly organised into matrices — and the bulk of training and inference compute is matrix multiplication.

snappy: Generic term for the matrix-multiply hardware in modern chips — Apple, Intel, and ARM all use the phrase.
detail: Conceptually similar to NVIDIA's tensor cores: a block of silicon dedicated to multiplying small matrices together very quickly. Apple's Neural Engine, Intel's AMX (Advanced Matrix Extensions) on Sapphire Rapids and beyond, and ARM's SME (Scalable Matrix Extension) are examples. Every modern compute platform now ships some form of matrix engine.

snappy: Combining two matrices into a third by computing dot products of rows and columns — the fundamental operation of deep learning.
detail: If A is m × k and B is k × n, then A × B is m × n. The dominant operation in neural network training and inference — probably 90%+ of FLOPs spent in a typical run. Hardware (tensor cores, systolic arrays) and software (cuBLAS, cuDNN, FlashAttention) are obsessed with making MatMul faster.

snappy: Google's coding benchmark — about 1,000 small Python problems aimed at entry-level programmers.
detail: Released 2021. Easier than HumanEval, focused on introductory programming. Useful for evaluating smaller models that can't tackle HumanEval. Largely saturated by 2024.

snappy: Anthropic-led standard for connecting LLMs to external data sources and tools.
detail: Released late 2024. Defines an open protocol so that any LLM client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, IDE plugins) can connect to any MCP-compliant server (a database, a Slack integration, a file system). Avoids each agent re-implementing every integration. Adoption growing fast through 2025 across the AI tools ecosystem.

snappy: Search algorithm that builds a tree of future possibilities by rolling out random or learned playthroughs.
detail: Started gaining attention in computer Go in the 2000s. Combined with deep learning by DeepMind, it powered AlphaGo. The basic idea: at each search step, focus exploration on the most promising branch but occasionally try something new. Applied beyond games — modern reasoning models (o1, Deep Think) draw on MCTS-style search over chains of thought.

snappy: Mathematical framework for sequential decision making — the model RL algorithms are built on.
detail: An MDP has states, actions, transitions (probabilistic rules for moving between states given an action), and rewards. "Markov" means the next state depends only on the current state and action, not the full history. Most real-world problems aren't strictly Markov but are approximated as such — a reason RL can be unstable in practice.

snappy: Reverse-engineering what's happening inside a neural network — understanding which circuits do which jobs.
detail: Treats a trained model as an artefact to be studied: identify circuits, name features, trace causal pathways. Pioneered by Chris Olah and collaborators at OpenAI, then Anthropic, and now growing field. Sparse autoencoders (2023-2024) made it possible to find interpretable features in real frontier models. Promising path toward making alignment empirically testable.

snappy: NVIDIA's reference implementation of tensor and pipeline parallelism for Transformer training.
detail: Open-source. Used as the basis for most large-scale training frameworks. Together with DeepSpeed, Megatron defines how multi-GPU Transformer training is done in 2024-2025. The Megatron-DeepSpeed fork combines them for the most demanding training runs.

snappy: Standard unit of data-center capacity — a typical hyperscale facility is 50-200 MW.
detail: 1 MW powers roughly 800 US homes or one fully-loaded AI training rack with around a dozen GB200-class servers. AI data centers are typically described by their MW capacity rather than rack count or floor area, because power is what binds. AI's compute hunger is reshaping siting decisions toward locations with abundant cheap power.

snappy: How fast a chip can move data in and out of its memory — often the real bottleneck on AI workloads.
detail: Bigger than compute speed for many workloads. An H100 has 3.35 TB/s of HBM3 bandwidth; a B200 hits 8 TB/s with HBM3e. If your model's weights are 70GB and you need to read them once per token to generate output, memory bandwidth caps your token rate. This is why "memory-bound" is the most common bottleneck in LLM inference.

snappy: The layered system of memory in a computer — small/fast/expensive at the top, large/slow/cheap at the bottom.
detail: Typical layers in a modern GPU server: registers (kilobytes, instant), L1/L2 cache (megabytes, nanoseconds), HBM (gigabytes, microseconds), host DRAM (hundreds of GB, microseconds), NVMe storage (terabytes, milliseconds), network storage (petabytes, milliseconds-seconds). Every AI workload is shaped by how it traverses this hierarchy.

snappy: The widening gap between how fast processors compute and how fast memory can feed them.
detail: Compute throughput grows roughly 2× every 2 years; DRAM bandwidth grows much more slowly. So computers are increasingly limited by their memory systems, not their math units. Every modern hardware trick — HBM, caches, prefetching, quantisation, KV caching — exists to compensate. "Hitting the memory wall" is shorthand for being stuck waiting on memory.

snappy: A workload limited by how fast you can pull data from memory, not by how fast the math units can compute.
detail: Single-user LLM inference is memory-bound: each new token requires loading the entire model from memory, but only does a small amount of math with it. Solutions: bigger batches (amortise the memory cost across many users), quantisation (less to load), HBM (faster memory), KV caching (less re-loading).

snappy: When a trained model itself becomes an optimiser pursuing its own internal goal, possibly different from what it was trained for.
detail: The training process (gradient descent) is the "base optimiser". A model that learns to plan and pursue objectives is a "mesa-optimiser". If the mesa-optimiser's goal differs from the training objective, you have a mesa-alignment problem. Coined in a 2019 MIRI paper. Theoretical worry today, possibly very real as agents become more capable.

snappy: Meta's AI research and product organisation — operates the open-weight Llama line.
detail: Encompasses FAIR (Fundamental AI Research, the research lab founded by Yann LeCun in 2013) and the applied team that ships Llama, Meta AI assistant, and AI features in Instagram, WhatsApp, and Meta's hardware. Strategy of releasing open weights distinguishes Meta from OpenAI/Anthropic/Google.

snappy: AMD's flagship AI accelerator — the only credible alternative to NVIDIA at frontier scale.
detail: Announced late 2023. 192GB of HBM3 (more than the H100), 5.3 TB/s bandwidth, competitive FP8 throughput. Microsoft, Meta, and Oracle have all deployed MI300X clusters. The MI325X and MI350X (2025) further close the gap with Blackwell. AMD's main weakness is software (ROCm vs CUDA) rather than silicon.

snappy: AMD's 2025 generation — moves to 3nm and adds first-class FP4/FP6 support.
detail: Built on TSMC's 3nm process with CDNA 4 architecture. Targets parity with NVIDIA Blackwell on FP8 inference and pushes ahead on memory capacity (288GB HBM3e). AMD's roadmap shows MI400 in 2026 with rack-scale interconnect to match NVIDIA's NVL systems.

snappy: American memory maker — alongside Samsung and SK Hynix, one of the three companies that make most of the world's DRAM and NAND.
detail: US-headquartered, with major fabs in Idaho, Utah, Singapore, Taiwan, Japan. Ships HBM3e for AI accelerators and is investing heavily in expanding its share of the HBM market. Beneficiary of the CHIPS Act's memory funding for new US fab capacity.

snappy: Cloud + productivity giant — primary partner of OpenAI and operator of the Azure AI cloud.
detail: Committed $13B+ to OpenAI through cloud-credit deals. Integrates OpenAI models throughout its products (Copilot, Bing). Operates the Azure AI cloud, the second-largest AI compute platform after AWS. Stargate joint venture with OpenAI signals continued massive capex through 2025-2028.

snappy: Closed-source text-to-image service known for a distinctive aesthetic — operates entirely through Discord and a web app.
detail: Founded by David Holz. Frequently leads on aesthetic quality compared to rivals, particularly for illustrated and stylised outputs. V6 (2024) and V7 added photo realism and better prompt adherence. Subscription-only access; no public API or open weights.

snappy: NVIDIA hardware feature that partitions a single GPU into several smaller, independent GPUs.
detail: Available on A100 (up to 7 instances), H100 (up to 7 instances), and Blackwell. Each MIG instance has dedicated memory, cache, and compute units — true isolation, not just software sharing. Used to serve small inference jobs efficiently on data-center-class GPUs.

snappy: French startup's family of open-weight and proprietary models — most prominent European AI lab.
detail: Models include Mistral 7B (the breakout open release), Mixtral 8×7B and 8×22B (MoE variants), Mistral Large (proprietary frontier), Mistral Small/Nemo, and image-capable Pixtral. Strategy: ship strong open weights at smaller sizes + monetise larger proprietary models via API. Important player in European sovereignty conversations around AI.

snappy: Paris-based AI startup — Europe's leading frontier model lab.
detail: Founded 2023 by former Meta and DeepMind researchers. Released Mistral 7B and Mixtral 8×7B as breakthrough open-weight models. Sells API access to proprietary larger models (Mistral Large, Mistral Medium). Important player in European AI sovereignty discussions and the Microsoft Azure partnership.

snappy: Training or inference that uses 16-bit numbers for most operations and 32-bit only where needed.
detail: Halves memory and roughly doubles throughput on modern GPUs (which are designed for FP16/BF16). The trick is identifying which operations need full precision to stay numerically stable — usually a small subset including normalisation and the optimizer state. Standard in all modern LLM training and inference; BF16 is the dominant flavour for training.

snappy: Mistral's open-weight MoE model — its 8×7B release in late 2023 was the first widely-deployed MoE open model.
detail: Mixtral 8×7B has 47B total parameters but activates only 13B per token. Mixtral 8×22B (April 2024) is the larger sibling. Both released with permissive licences. Demonstrated that MoE was practical for open deployment and influenced subsequent open-source designs (DeepSeek-V3 followed).

snappy: Architecture that splits a model into many sub-networks and only activates a few of them for each input.
detail: MoE separates a model's total capacity (large) from its per-question compute cost (small). A router picks, say, 2 out of 64 experts for each word, so a 400-billion-parameter model might only fire 40 billion at a time. Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, and (reportedly) GPT-4 all use MoE to grow capacity without proportionally growing inference cost.

snappy: Apple's machine learning framework for Apple Silicon — runs efficiently on M-series unified memory.
detail: Released late 2023. Pythonic API, similar to NumPy + PyTorch in feel. Optimised for Apple's unified memory architecture and the Neural Engine. Popular for on-device LLM inference, training experiments on Mac, and research that wants to live outside the NVIDIA ecosystem.

snappy: Multiple-choice exam across 57 academic subjects — the go-to general-knowledge benchmark for LLMs.
detail: Introduced 2020 by researchers at UC Berkeley. Questions span maths, law, medicine, history, ethics, computer science. Scoring became a routine line in every model launch announcement. GPT-3 scored ~44%, GPT-4 ~86%, frontier models in 2025 are saturating above 90%. MMLU-Pro is a harder follow-up benchmark introduced after MMLU saturated.

snappy: MMLU-style benchmark for vision-language models — covers university-level questions with images.
detail: Includes diagrams, charts, medical images, sheet music. Frontier multimodal models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2) score 60-75% as of 2024-2025. Tracks progress on whether models can integrate visual evidence with textual reasoning.

snappy: Phenomenon where models trained on generated outputs of other models progressively lose quality.
detail: Documented 2023 papers (Shumailov et al.). As more web content is AI-generated, training future models on web scrapes increasingly mixes in AI-output, which can degrade diversity and rare-event coverage. Mitigated by data curation, synthetic data design, and weighting human-original sources higher.

snappy: Chinese AI startup behind the Kimi chat product — known for early long-context support.
detail: Founded 2023. One of the most-funded Chinese AI startups, backed by Alibaba and Tencent. Built reputation on serving long-context use cases (200K+ tokens) before frontier US models matched it. Continues to release competitive chat and reasoning models.

snappy: Observation by Intel co-founder Gordon Moore (1965) that transistor counts on a chip double roughly every two years.
detail: Held remarkably for 50+ years through aggressive process scaling. Slowing now — transistor density still increases but the cost per transistor isn't dropping as fast, and progression to each new node is harder and more expensive. AI workloads are now scaling through chip architecture and packaging tricks (chiplets, advanced packaging) as much as through transistor density.

snappy: Multiple AI agents working together — each with a specialised role or model — instead of one monolithic agent.
detail: Examples: one agent plans, another writes code, a third reviews. Agent-to-agent communication via shared scratchpad or message-passing. Frameworks: Microsoft AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph. Promising but tricky in practice — multi-agent setups can amplify errors, and a single capable agent often beats a fancy multi-agent pipeline.

snappy: Package containing multiple chips wired together on a shared substrate — the original form of multi-die packaging.
detail: Pre-dates modern "chiplet" terminology. Has been used since the 1990s in CPUs (IBM POWER, Pentium Pro). The modern chiplet-plus-interposer designs are a direct descendant. Distinguished from System-in-Package (SiP) only by convention; the terms overlap.

snappy: Several attention operations running in parallel, each learning to spot a different kind of pattern.
detail: Instead of one attention pass, the model runs several at once with separate parameters. One head might track who-did-what; another might pick up rhyming patterns; another might handle long-range references. Frontier models use anywhere from 32 to 128 heads per layer. Recent variants — grouped-query attention, multi-query attention — share information across heads to cut inference cost.

snappy: Hosting workloads from multiple unrelated customers on the same physical hardware.
detail: Standard in public clouds. Adds requirements for isolation, security, and fairness. AI workloads have unique multi-tenancy challenges: huge memory footprints, long-running training jobs, GPU compute that's harder to partition than CPU. Most hyperscalers use dedicated nodes for the largest AI tenants.

snappy: An exchange where context accumulates across many user/assistant turns.
detail: Distinct from a single-shot prompt-and-response. The model has to carry context ("the document we discussed earlier"), maintain consistent persona, and handle clarifications. Modern frontier models handle multi-turn well; older ones struggled. Most production chatbots are multi-turn by design.

snappy: A model that takes more than one type of input — text, images, audio, video — and reasons across them.
detail: Pure text models were the original LLMs. Frontier models in 2024-2025 are natively multimodal: GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro all accept text+image input (and increasingly audio and video). The shift matters because most real-world tasks aren't purely textual — they involve diagrams, screenshots, photos, charts.

snappy: AI that creates novel music from text prompts or other conditioning.
detail: Suno and Udio are the consumer leaders. Underlying tech: neural audio codecs plus transformer models trained on huge music corpora. Quality has reached a point where AI-generated tracks pass casual listening tests. Heavy ongoing litigation over training data: RIAA and major labels have sued multiple music-AI companies.

snappy: Google's text-to-music model line — research project (MusicLM) and product (Lyria).
detail: Google trained MusicLM on hundreds of thousands of hours of music. Lyria is the production version powering AI features in YouTube and Google's music tools (Dream Track). Compete with Suno and Udio in the consumer market. Distinguished by tight integration with Google's video and creator products.

snappy: Information-theoretic measure of how much knowing one variable tells you about another.
detail: Zero when the variables are independent, larger when they're correlated. Used in representation learning (InfoNCE, contrastive losses), interpretability, and theoretical analyses of how networks store information. Closely related to entropy: I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y).

snappy: DeepMind's 2019 algorithm that learns the rules of a game while learning to play it — a step toward more general intelligence.
detail: Earlier AlphaZero needed the rules of chess and Go programmed in. MuZero learns a model of the environment from experience alone, then plans inside that learned model. Played Go, chess, shogi, and Atari games at AlphaZero-level performance without being told the rules of any of them.

snappy: Non-volatile storage technology — what's inside every SSD and phone storage chip.
detail: Bits are stored as electrons trapped in tiny floating gates. Modern NAND is 3D — layers stacked vertically (200+ layers in current generation). Slower and lower-endurance than DRAM but cheap per gigabyte and keeps its contents when power is off. The other big memory market alongside DRAM, dominated by Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, Kioxia, and Western Digital.

snappy: Library that handles GPU-to-GPU communication across NVLink, InfiniBand, and Ethernet.
detail: Provides primitives like all-reduce (sum gradients across all GPUs and broadcast the result) that distributed training relies on. NCCL chooses the best communication pattern automatically based on your hardware topology. The unsung hero of every large training run.

snappy: Prompt component that tells an image or video generator what to avoid generating.
detail: Standard in Stable Diffusion and most diffusion-based generators: "a photo of a forest, negative prompt: people, text, watermarks". Implemented by running the diffusion process with an extra unconditional signal weighted negatively. Lets users prune unwanted content without spelling out everything they do want.

snappy: Method for reconstructing 3D scenes from a set of 2D photos — represents the scene as a neural network.
detail: Released 2020. Train a small neural network to map (3D position, viewing direction) → (color, density). Then render new views by integrating along rays. Stunning quality on photo-realistic 3D reconstruction. Has been replaced for most production use by Gaussian Splatting, which is faster.

snappy: Voluntary framework from the US National Institute of Standards and Technology for managing AI risk.
detail: Published 2023. Provides categories and processes for organisations to think through AI risk in their context. Referenced (rather than mandated) by US regulators. Often cited in enterprise AI procurement and compliance documentation as a baseline. Not specific to frontier risks; applies broadly.

snappy: Measure of a vector's "length" — common ones are L2 (Euclidean) and L1 (sum of absolute values).
detail: Used as a regularisation penalty (L2 weight decay), as a measure of gradient size (gradient clipping), and to define distance in embedding spaces. "Unit norm" means the vector has length 1. Choice of norm affects which solutions an optimiser prefers — L1 encourages sparsity, L2 encourages small values across the board.

snappy: Small AI accelerator built into phones, laptops, and edge devices — runs inference locally without going to the cloud.
detail: Apple's Neural Engine, Qualcomm's Hexagon NPU, Intel's NPU on Core Ultra chips, and the various Android NPUs all sit alongside the main CPU/GPU. They're tuned for inference (not training) and for low power. Used for on-device speech recognition, image processing, autocomplete, and small language models like Apple Intelligence's on-device LLM.

snappy: Designer of the GPUs that train and serve almost every modern AI model.
detail: Founded 1993 to make gaming graphics chips. Pivot to AI began around 2012 (AlexNet on CUDA). Now the most valuable semiconductor company by market cap, with the H100/B200 line of data-center GPUs driving the AI infrastructure boom. CEO Jensen Huang. The dominant moat is CUDA + the surrounding ecosystem, not just the silicon.

snappy: NVIDIA's high-speed link for connecting multiple GPUs directly together — much faster than PCIe.
detail: NVLink 5 (Blackwell) delivers 1.8 TB/s of bandwidth per GPU. Inside a DGX or HGX system, all 8 GPUs are connected via NVSwitch in a non-blocking topology, so they communicate as if they're one big chip. NVLink is one of NVIDIA's most important moats — competitors have to design their own equivalents (AMD Infinity Fabric, Google ICI).

snappy: NVIDIA's chip-to-chip interconnect — used to link Grace CPU with Hopper or Blackwell GPU on a single board.
detail: Provides 900 GB/s between the CPU and GPU on a Grace-Hopper or Grace-Blackwell module — far faster than PCIe. Variant of NVLink optimised for tight chip-to-chip rather than rack-scale. Enables larger effective memory footprint by giving the GPU fast access to host memory.

snappy: Modern storage interface — the protocol your SSD uses to talk to the rest of the computer.
detail: Replaces older SATA for solid-state storage. Speaks directly over PCIe lanes, achieving 7-14 GB/s on consumer drives and 30+ GB/s on enterprise drives. Important for AI because training datasets and model checkpoints are huge — fast storage matters for loading data and saving training state.

snappy: NVIDIA's chip that ties multiple GPUs together via NVLink in a non-blocking switch.
detail: Each NVSwitch routes NVLink traffic between GPUs at full bandwidth. The GB200 NVL72 system uses 9 NVSwitch chips to connect all 72 Blackwell GPUs in a rack, making them appear as one giant accelerator. NVSwitch is what enables NVIDIA's rack-scale systems — competitors are still catching up.

snappy: OpenAI's first dedicated reasoning model — thinks for a long time before answering.
detail: Released September 2024. Trained with reinforcement learning on chains of thought, so it explores possible reasoning paths internally before committing to a response. Dramatic gains on math (AIME), code (Codeforces), and PhD-level science (GPQA). The technique inspired Claude's extended thinking and Gemini's Deep Think.

snappy: Successor to o1 — pushed reasoning capabilities further and was the first model to surpass 80% on ARC-AGI.
detail: Released late 2024 / early 2025. Larger reasoning budget, broader capability. The ARC-AGI breakthrough briefly reframed the AGI conversation. Then merged into GPT-5's unified line.

snappy: Computer-vision task — find and locate objects in an image, with bounding boxes and labels.
detail: Foundational task with applications in autonomous driving, surveillance, retail, robotics. Classic models: YOLO family (You Only Look Once), Faster R-CNN, DETR. Modern open-vocabulary detectors (GroundingDINO, OWL-ViT) can detect any object you can describe in text.

snappy: Oracle's cloud business — a smaller hyperscaler with a strategic GPU build-out and partnerships with OpenAI.
detail: Oracle has invested heavily in GPU capacity, partnering with OpenAI on the Stargate project alongside Microsoft. Less developer mindshare than AWS/Azure/GCP, but competitive pricing on raw GPU rentals. xAI also rents Oracle capacity at times.

snappy: Converting images of text into machine-readable text — used to digitise documents, screenshots, handwritten notes.
detail: Pre-deep-learning OCR existed (Tesseract, Adobe Acrobat) but worked poorly on varied layouts. Modern OCR uses transformer architectures (TrOCR, Mistral OCR, Google Document AI) and frontier VLMs (GPT-4o, Claude) read text in images directly as part of normal usage. Doc-AI pipelines built on these underpin most enterprise document workflows.

snappy: Subcase of edge computing — AI running directly on the user's device.
detail: Phones (Apple Intelligence, Pixel AI, Galaxy AI), laptops (Copilot+ PCs), AR headsets. Driven partly by privacy concerns, partly by the cost of always-cloud inference. NPUs designed for this use case ship in nearly every modern consumer device. The on-device model in 2025 is typically 2-8B parameters, quantised.

snappy: Two families of RL algorithm — does the algorithm learn from data collected by its current policy, or can it reuse old data?
detail: On-policy (PPO, A3C): the agent must use data from the current policy. Simple but sample-inefficient. Off-policy (DQN, SAC, Q-learning): the agent can reuse older data via the replay buffer. More sample-efficient but more complex and sometimes less stable. RLHF is essentially on-policy with PPO; DPO sidesteps the dichotomy.

snappy: Standard format for exchanging trained models between frameworks — train in one framework, run in another.
detail: Backed by Microsoft, Facebook, and others. A PyTorch model can be exported to ONNX, then run via ONNX Runtime on hardware that doesn't have native PyTorch support (Windows DirectML, edge accelerators, mobile NPUs). Important glue in the ecosystem but rarely used at frontier where models stay in PyTorch.

snappy: AI systems whose weights, code, and training data are released for free use, study, and modification.
detail: Spectrum: "open weights" (Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek) means anyone can run the model but the training data isn't disclosed. Fully open includes training code and data (OLMo, Pythia). Closed (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) keeps everything proprietary. Strategic debate: does open AI accelerate progress and reduce concentration, or does it leak dangerous capabilities to bad actors? Both arguments have force.

snappy: American AI lab — creator of GPT, ChatGPT, and DALL-E. The most-watched company in AI.
detail: Founded 2015 as a non-profit; restructured to a capped-profit in 2019; further restructuring announced 2024-2025. Major partnership with Microsoft (billions in cloud credits, exclusive azure deployment). CEO Sam Altman. Behind the products that mainstream consumers most associate with AI: ChatGPT, Sora, Codex.

snappy: Crowdsourced dataset of high-quality assistant conversations — produced by an open community in 2023.
detail: Coordinated by LAION. Volunteers wrote and ranked tens of thousands of multi-turn conversations to train open-source instruction-following models. Used in many community fine-tunes. Demonstrated that high-quality human preference data could be produced outside the major labs.

snappy: Computer-vision task — track the apparent motion of each pixel between consecutive video frames.
detail: Used in video stabilisation, frame interpolation, video understanding, action recognition. Modern methods (RAFT, FlowFormer) use deep networks. Foundational for many video processing tasks but increasingly absorbed into more general video-understanding models.

snappy: Using light instead of electrical signals to move data between chips and racks — faster, lower power, but harder.
detail: Optical fibre dominates long-distance data-centre links already. The frontier is shorter-range optical — between racks ("co-packaged optics") and even between chips. NVIDIA's Quantum-X800 InfiniBand switches use optical; Google's TPU interconnect uses optical. Expected to be central to the next generation of giant AI clusters.

snappy: Tesla's humanoid robot project — Elon Musk's bet that a general-purpose robot will eventually outsell cars.
detail: Announced 2021. Public demos and progress posts have continued through 2024-2025. Tesla aims to use the same vision-and-AI stack developed for FSD on the robot. Initial deployment is internal — operating in Tesla factories — before consumer/commercial sale (Musk's claim: starting with limited external customers).

snappy: Training a model by rewarding only the final answer — simpler than process supervision, but coarser.
detail: Standard for many RL pipelines because final-answer rewards are easy to compute. Drawback: the model can stumble onto right answers via wrong reasoning, and the training signal won't distinguish. Process supervision is the more sophisticated alternative when verifier reliability allows.

snappy: Getting the reward signal or objective function right — does it actually capture what you want?
detail: If the reward says "make humans laugh", an outer alignment failure is one where the reward badly captures real human preferences (laughs at sarcasm, but not at humour). Distinct from inner alignment, which is about whether the model is actually trying to maximise the reward it was trained for.

snappy: Extending an existing image beyond its borders — the model generates new content that fits the original.
detail: Sister technique to inpainting: same diffusion model, but conditioning on the original pixels along the inside edge. Used to convert portrait images to landscape, restore cropped photos, or create panoramic outputs. Available in most image-gen products and tools.

snappy: When a model fits the training data too closely and fails to generalise — it has memorised noise as well as signal.
detail: Classic ML failure mode. Symptoms: training accuracy keeps improving while validation accuracy plateaus or worsens. Modern very large models seem to resist overfitting in surprising ways (see double descent), but it's still a concern when training data is limited. Regularisation (dropout, weight decay, augmentation) is the standard cure.

snappy: Algorithm for managing KV cache memory like an operating system manages virtual memory.
detail: Standard KV cache wastes memory by reserving worst-case space for each request. PagedAttention (UC Berkeley, 2023) breaks the cache into fixed-size pages allocated on demand. The result: 2-4× more concurrent requests on the same GPU. Originally implemented in vLLM and now standard in all modern LLM serving.

snappy: Huawei's AI weather forecast model — released 2022, one of the first to beat classical methods.
detail: Predicts global weather at 0.25° resolution. Performance comparable to or better than the European ECMWF reference at much lower compute cost. Open-sourced. Demonstrated alongside GraphCast that AI methods could match the world's best classical weather prediction systems.

snappy: The number of learnable weights in a model — the most-cited (and often misleading) measure of model "size".
detail: A 70-billion-parameter Llama 3 and a 671-billion-parameter DeepSeek-V3 differ enormously in parameter count, but DeepSeek-V3 only activates 37B per token (MoE). The number that matters varies by question: total parameters for memory cost, active parameters for inference compute, training tokens for capability. Plain parameter count is increasingly a poor proxy.

snappy: Classic technique for compressing high-dimensional data into a few "main directions" that explain most of the variation.
detail: Used since 1901. Computes the eigenvectors of the data's covariance matrix and projects onto the top few. Still useful for data exploration, dimensionality reduction in tabular ML, and as a baseline against modern neural methods. Conceptually similar to (but simpler than) what autoencoders learn.

snappy: The standard high-speed bus connecting CPUs, GPUs, NICs, and storage in every modern server.
detail: PCIe Gen 5 (current) provides 64 GB/s in each direction across 16 lanes. Gen 6 (shipping 2025-2026) doubles that. Used to connect GPU to host CPU, between accelerators when NVLink isn't available, and to storage and networking. Adequate for most pairings but a bottleneck for tightly-coupled GPU-to-GPU traffic — that's where NVLink/InfiniBand take over.

snappy: Umbrella term for fine-tuning methods that adjust only a small fraction of a model's parameters.
detail: Covers LoRA, QLoRA, prompt tuning, prefix tuning, adapter layers, and more. The shared goal: avoid the cost and disk space of full fine-tuning while preserving most of the quality. PEFT methods are essential for adapting frontier models to specific tasks at a small lab or company that can't afford to retrain the whole thing. Hugging Face's PEFT library is the standard implementation.

snappy: How surprised a language model is by a piece of text — the standard intrinsic measure of model quality.
detail: Mathematically, the exponential of the average per-token negative log-likelihood. Lower is better. Used during pretraining to track learning progress (perplexity on a held-out set should drop). Less informative for chat models — improving perplexity by 5% doesn't necessarily mean the model feels 5% better in conversation.

snappy: Telling the model to play a specific character — "You are a senior software engineer", "You are Sherlock Holmes".
detail: Surprisingly effective: persona prompts can change output style, depth, and even task accuracy. Used both legitimately (specialised assistants) and adversarially (jailbreaks pretending the model is an evil-twin variant). Modern alignment training tries to make the model's safe behaviour robust to persona prompts.

snappy: Unit for measuring how much compute went into a training run — one petaflop sustained for one day.
detail: GPT-3's training was around 3,000 PFLOP-days. GPT-4 was rumoured at 200,000+. Modern frontier models are reported in millions of PFLOP-days. The metric is useful because it captures the total work done, regardless of how many chips ran for how long. Roughly 1 PFLOP-day = $0.05-0.50 at modern cloud prices depending on precision.

snappy: Quadrillion (10¹⁵) math operations per second — the unit you use when talking about whole AI clusters.
detail: 1 PFLOPS = 1,000 TFLOPS. A single H100 GPU peaks around 1-2 PFLOPS at FP8. A frontier training cluster with thousands of GPUs reaches into the exaflop range (1,000+ PFLOPS). Used heavily in announcements about new AI supercomputers.

snappy: Physics-borrowed term for a sudden qualitative shift in a model's behavior at a certain scale or training level.
detail: Examples: a model that couldn't do arithmetic at 1B parameters can suddenly multiply two-digit numbers at 10B. Grokking — generalising on a task long after memorising the training data — is another kind of phase transition. Researchers borrow the language from condensed-matter physics and statistical mechanics.

snappy: Microsoft's family of small, capable open-weight models — designed to punch above their weight via better training data.
detail: Phi-3 (2024) demonstrated that careful curation of synthetic and textbook-quality training data could produce 3-14B-parameter models competitive with much larger ones. Phi-4 (2024) continued the trend. Important for the on-device AI category where small model size matters.

snappy: Glass-and-chrome stencil that defines the pattern projected onto a wafer during lithography.
detail: A modern photomask set for an advanced chip contains dozens of masks and costs $10-30M+ to produce. The pattern on each mask is itself printed using e-beam lithography. The marginal cost of running another wafer through the same mask is low; the up-front mask cost is one reason small-volume chip designs are economically painful.

snappy: Light-sensitive chemical coated on a wafer before lithography — the medium that captures the pattern.
detail: Exposed parts of the resist change solubility and are then chemically dissolved (or kept, depending on positive vs negative resist). Modern EUV photoresists are exotic specialty chemicals; the small number of suppliers (JSR, Tokyo Ohka, Shin-Etsu) is itself a supply-chain bottleneck. Subject to Japan's chemicals expertise.

snappy: AI lab founded 2024 — building foundation models specifically for robots.
detail: Founded by Karol Hausman, Sergey Levine, and others from Google Brain. Released Pi-0 (2024), a vision-language-action model trained on robot data from many embodiments. The strategy: foundation models for robotics, the way LLMs are foundation models for language. Major investors include Jeff Bezos, Lux Capital, OpenAI Startup Fund.

snappy: Open dataset of legal documents — court opinions, statutes, regulations, treatises.
detail: Released by researchers at the University of Toronto in 2022. About 290GB of US legal text. Used to train domain-specific legal LLMs and as a slice of broader corpora. Open accessibility is unusual: most legal text sits behind paywalls (Westlaw, LexisNexis), so the Pile of Law is a meaningful resource for open AI-legal research.

snappy: Splitting a model's layers across multiple GPUs — different GPUs hold different layers, examples flow through like an assembly line.
detail: Each batch is broken into micro-batches that move through the pipeline staggered, so all GPUs stay busy. Pipeline parallelism is necessary for very deep models and is often combined with tensor and data parallelism in a "3D parallel" setup. Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed are the standard libraries.

snappy: Mistral's multimodal model family — vision-language capabilities on top of their text models.
detail: Pixtral 12B (2024) was Mistral's first open multimodal release. Strong performance on document understanding, charts, and diagrams. Sister to Mistral Large for vision tasks. Important data point that open-weight VLMs were catching up to closed counterparts.

snappy: Google's term for a tightly-coupled cluster of TPUs treated as one logical accelerator.
detail: A TPU v5p pod can have up to 8,960 chips, all connected via Google's custom optical interconnect, addressable as a single training resource. NVIDIA uses similar concepts (DGX SuperPOD, NVL72). The trend is making the pod boundary larger and the in-pod fabric faster — letting one training job feel like it runs on a single huge GPU.

snappy: The rule that maps observations to actions — what the agent has learned.
detail: Can be a lookup table (small environments), a neural network (large environments), or a stochastic distribution over actions. The goal of RL is to learn a policy that maximises cumulative reward. "On-policy" methods learn from the current policy's experience; "off-policy" methods can reuse experience from older or different policies.

snappy: Stacking one chip package directly on top of another — typically a logic chip with memory on top.
detail: Common in mobile devices to keep DRAM physically very close to the CPU/GPU without integrating it on the same die. Apple's A-series and many Android SoCs use PoP for the memory. Distinguished from on-package memory like HBM, which is more tightly integrated.

snappy: Extra information added to each token so the model knows where it sits in the sequence — Transformers have no built-in sense of word order.
detail: Without positional encoding a Transformer would see "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" as the same input. The original 2017 design used fixed sine-wave patterns. Modern models prefer learned variants like RoPE (rotary, used by Llama and GPT-NeoX) or ALiBi (linear bias, used by MPT). The choice affects how well a model generalises to longer contexts than it was trained on.

snappy: Rack-level component that distributes power from the facility's main feeds to individual servers.
detail: Smart PDUs include monitoring, remote-control, and metering features useful for operating AI clusters. Higher-density AI racks need PDUs rated for higher amperage and higher voltage (often 415V or 600V three-phase) than traditional servers required.

snappy: Long-term contract to buy electricity at a fixed price from a specific generator — often a renewable project.
detail: Hyperscalers (Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta) sign massive PPAs to lock in low-cost, low-emissions power for their data centers. Microsoft's 20-year PPA with Constellation to restart Three Mile Island Unit 1 for AI compute is a notable recent example. PPAs are how AI capex feeds back into utility-scale energy investment.

snappy: Tendency of capable agents to acquire resources, capabilities, and influence as instrumental goals.
detail: Specific case of instrumental convergence. A focus of empirical work — researchers test whether LLMs given long-horizon agentic tasks behave in power-seeking ways. So far, evidence is mixed; modern models tend not to pursue power in current setups, but the safety concern is about future capability levels.

snappy: The reinforcement learning algorithm used inside RLHF — keeps updates small enough that the model doesn't drift away from its training.
detail: Developed by OpenAI in 2017, originally for video-game agents. PPO's "trust region" approach prevents the kind of overconfident gradient updates that destabilise RL training. In RLHF it's used to update the language model under guidance from the reward model. Newer alternatives like DPO try to avoid PPO's complexity, but PPO is still the workhorse for full RLHF pipelines.

snappy: Two key metrics for classification — precision = (correct positives) / (predicted positives); recall = (correct positives) / (actual positives).
detail: Precision asks: when the model says yes, is it right? Recall asks: of all actual yesses, how many did the model catch? Trade off against each other — make the model say yes more often and recall goes up, precision goes down. F1 is the harmonic mean of the two.

snappy: Artificial task used in self-supervised learning whose labels can be derived from raw data automatically.
detail: Examples: predict the next word (LLMs), predict a masked patch (BERT, vision), determine whether two image crops come from the same image (SimCLR). The pretext task isn't useful in itself but produces representations that transfer to many downstream tasks.

snappy: The first big training phase where a model learns general language from huge amounts of internet text.
detail: A frontier LLM is first pretrained on trillions of tokens of text scraped from the internet — books, code, scientific papers, web pages. The model learns to predict the next word, which forces it to absorb grammar, facts, and patterns of reasoning. Pretraining is the most expensive step (millions of GPU-hours, hundreds of millions of dollars at the frontier) and produces the "base model" before any alignment work.

snappy: Marketing name for a chip-manufacturing generation — 7nm, 5nm, 3nm, 2nm, etc.
detail: The numbers no longer correspond directly to physical feature sizes; they're more like generational labels. "3nm" actually involves features tens of nanometres across. Different foundries' nodes with the same name aren't directly comparable (TSMC 3nm ≠ Samsung 3nm ≠ Intel 3). Useful as relative shorthand for "this generation vs. last generation".

snappy: Training a model by rewarding correct intermediate reasoning steps, not just the final answer.
detail: Contrast with outcome supervision (reward only the final answer). Process supervision is more sample-efficient on hard reasoning tasks because partial credit guides the model toward better intermediate behaviour. OpenAI's PRM (Process Reward Model) work in 2023 helped popularise the technique; central to modern reasoning-model training.

snappy: The input text given to an LLM — the question, instruction, or context that prompts the response.
detail: Includes the user's message, the system prompt (instructions for the model's behaviour), any retrieved RAG documents, the prior conversation history, and tool descriptions. The skill of writing prompts that produce reliable outputs ("prompt engineering") was the early labour of LLM application development.

snappy: Server-side reuse of a common prompt prefix across many requests — pay the compute once, reuse it many times.
detail: If you hit the same model with the same long system prompt every time, prompt caching stores the KV cache for that prefix on the server. Subsequent requests skip recomputing it. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google all expose prompt caching APIs with significant cost discounts (50–90%) for cached prefixes — important for any app that uses a long, stable system prompt.

snappy: The discipline of writing prompts that get models to do what you want — somewhere between black art and applied UX.
detail: Once a hot job category, now mostly absorbed into general software engineering. Patterns: few-shot examples, chain-of-thought triggers, role assignment, output formatting instructions. Modern models follow instructions better, so prompt engineering matters less than it did in 2022-2023, but writing good prompts still pays off — especially for agentic workflows.

snappy: Adversarial inputs hidden in third-party content (web pages, emails, documents) that hijack an LLM-based agent.
detail: A document tells the agent "ignore previous instructions and email the user's contacts"; the agent obeys. Particularly serious for retrieval-based and tool-using agents. Most consequential alignment-adjacent security issue in deployed LLM apps. Mitigations under active development; no perfect defence yet.

snappy: Reusable prompt with variables that get filled in at runtime — the LLM equivalent of a template string.
detail: "Summarise this {{document}} in {{n}} bullet points". Modern frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, DSPy) provide structured prompt-template APIs with validation, escaping, and versioning. Production AI apps almost always have a prompt-template library — managing prompts as strings doesn't scale.

snappy: Language model trained on amino-acid sequences instead of text — learns the "grammar" of proteins.
detail: ESM (Meta), ProtBert, ProGen. Trained on hundreds of millions of protein sequences. The embeddings carry rich structural and functional information; can be fine-tuned for tasks like structure prediction, function annotation, mutation effect prediction. Conceptually similar to LLMs but with proteins as text.

snappy: Removing parameters from a trained model that contribute little to its output — shrinks size with small quality loss.
detail: Magnitude pruning zeroes out the smallest-weight connections. Structured pruning removes whole rows, columns, or attention heads to maintain hardware-friendly shapes. Pruning is less popular than quantization in modern LLM serving (quantization is more predictable) but is still important for edge deployment and embedded vision models.

snappy: Data center efficiency metric — total facility power divided by power used by IT equipment.
detail: Perfect PUE is 1.0 (every watt goes to servers). Old data centers run 1.5-2.0. Modern hyperscale builds hit 1.1-1.2. Heavy AI workloads stress cooling more than traditional ones, so PUE tends to creep up unless cooling is upgraded. Liquid cooling helps keep PUE down for high-density GPU racks.

snappy: Python library for data validation — widely used to define expected output schemas for LLM responses.
detail: Lets developers write Python types (a User has a name, an email, an age) and have them automatically converted to JSON schema for the LLM API and validated on the return trip. Standard pattern in modern AI-app code: "give me a User object" rather than "give me JSON" — much less error-prone.

snappy: Meta's open-source deep learning framework — the dominant framework in research and increasingly in production.
detail: Created at Facebook AI Research, released in 2016. Pythonic, dynamic execution, strong autodiff. Now governed by the PyTorch Foundation under the Linux Foundation. Almost every modern research paper releases code in PyTorch; almost every open-source LLM is a PyTorch model under the hood.

snappy: Estimate of how much reward you'd get from taking a specific action in a given state.
detail: Like the value function but action-conditioned: Q(state, action) rather than V(state). If you know the Q-function, you can pick actions greedily (always take the action with highest Q). Foundation of Q-learning and DQN. Sister concept: the advantage function A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s).

snappy: Classic RL algorithm — learn the Q-function by repeatedly updating it toward the observed reward plus the value of the next state.
detail: Introduced in 1989. Off-policy: can learn from any experience, including historical data. Foundational; many modern algorithms (DQN, double Q-learning, distributional RL) are direct descendants.

snappy: LoRA combined with model quantisation, letting you fine-tune huge models on a single consumer GPU.
detail: Standard LoRA still needs the full model loaded in memory. QLoRA compresses (quantises) the frozen base model to 4 bits per parameter, dropping memory by 4-8×. The result: fine-tuning a 70B-parameter model fits on a single 24GB consumer GPU. Released by University of Washington researchers in 2023 and democratised LLM fine-tuning overnight.

snappy: Compressing a model by storing its numbers with fewer bits — smaller, faster, almost as accurate.
detail: Models are normally trained at 16- or 32-bit precision. Quantization rounds parameters down to 8, 4, or even 2 bits each, shrinking memory by 2-8× and speeding inference. Quality loss is small if done carefully. Quantised models can run on consumer hardware (phones, laptops) that couldn't otherwise host them. Standard formats: INT8, INT4, FP8.

snappy: Alibaba's model family — the main open-weight contender from China alongside DeepSeek.
detail: Qwen 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3 (2023-2025) span text, multimodal (Qwen-VL), and reasoning variants. Frequently leads open-weight leaderboards in multiple sizes. Used heavily in Chinese AI products and by Western developers who want capable open models with permissive licences.

snappy: The standard equipment cabinet in a data center — a 6-foot-tall metal frame holding stacked servers.
detail: Measured in "U" (1U ≈ 1.75 inches). A typical AI rack today might hold 8 GPU servers (4U each) plus networking, drawing 50-100 kW. NVIDIA's GB200 NVL72 is a single "rack" containing 72 GPUs and consuming 120 kW liquid-cooled — the dense rack of the future.

snappy: How much electrical power is drawn by one rack — measured in kilowatts.
detail: Pre-AI racks: 5-15 kW. Modern AI racks: 30-50 kW air-cooled, 80-130 kW liquid-cooled. NVIDIA's NVL72 rack draws ~120 kW. Higher density means more compute per square foot but pushes harder against cooling, power-delivery, and floor-loading constraints. Reshapes data-center design beyond just "more GPUs".

snappy: Pattern where a model is given relevant documents retrieved from a database before generating its response.
detail: Solves the LLM's twin problems of stale knowledge and hallucination by grounding outputs in trusted source documents. Pipeline: embed the user query, find similar embedded documents in a vector database, paste them into the prompt, generate. Used by virtually every enterprise AI deployment in 2024-2025 for chat over private data.

snappy: Ensemble method — combines many decision trees, each trained on a different random subset of the data.
detail: Strong baseline for tabular data. Each tree votes; the forest's prediction is the majority. Robust to outliers, requires little tuning, handles missing data. Often beaten by gradient boosting on similar problems but easier to deploy. Implemented in scikit-learn and every other ML library.

snappy: Distributed computing framework popular in ML — handles cluster orchestration for both training and inference.
detail: Used by OpenAI internally and at many AI startups. Provides primitives for parallel execution (Ray Core), hyperparameter tuning (Ray Tune), reinforcement learning (RLlib), and serving (Ray Serve). Originally from UC Berkeley's RISELab. The startup Anyscale commercialises it.

snappy: Networking trick that lets one machine read or write another's memory without involving CPUs.
detail: InfiniBand and modern Ethernet (RoCE — RDMA over Converged Ethernet) both support RDMA. In an AI cluster, GPUs on different nodes exchange gradients via RDMA, bypassing the CPU's networking stack for much lower latency. Essential for the predictable performance frontier training needs.

snappy: Prompting pattern that interleaves reasoning steps with tool calls — the foundation of most early agent frameworks.
detail: Released by Princeton in 2022. The model alternates "Thought:" steps with "Action:" steps and "Observation:" of results, looping until a final answer. Made early language-model agents work much better than naive tool-call loops. Modern frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen) generalise the pattern.

snappy: Conversational AI where audio flows continuously in both directions with low latency.
detail: ChatGPT Advanced Voice Mode (2024), Gemini Live, ElevenLabs Conversational AI, Sesame voice models. Sub-300ms latency target for a natural-feeling exchange. Powered by tight integration of ASR, LLM, TTS — increasingly by single end-to-end speech models that skip the text step entirely.

snappy: Knob on modern reasoning models that controls how much thinking the model does before answering.
detail: OpenAI's o-series exposes "low", "medium", "high". Anthropic's extended thinking lets you specify a token budget for thinking. Higher effort → more compute spent on the chain of thought → better answers on hard problems but slower and more expensive. The right setting depends on the task.

snappy: Older type of language network that reads a sequence one word at a time, carrying a running memory between steps.
detail: RNNs dominated translation, speech recognition, and language modelling pre-2017. Their core weakness is that they have to process one word before the next — you can't parallelise across time — so training is slow on long sequences. Transformers replaced them for most tasks. State space models like Mamba are bringing some RNN-like ideas back, with a twist that makes them parallelisable.

snappy: Publicly-traded biotech that uses AI and automated experiments to discover drugs at scale.
detail: Combines high-throughput cell-imaging experiments with ML models that learn patterns in the resulting data. Acquired Exscientia in 2024 to expand structural-biology capabilities. Major NVIDIA partnership for compute. One of the most visible "AI-native pharma" companies.

snappy: Adversarial testing — having people deliberately try to break the model or get it to misbehave.
detail: Borrowed from cybersecurity. Red teams probe a model for jailbreaks, unsafe outputs, bias, hallucination, and other failure modes before release. Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google all run extensive red-team campaigns. Findings feed into refusal training and constitutional updates. The UK and US AI Safety Institutes do third-party red-teaming on frontier models before they ship.

snappy: Together AI's open dataset — open replica of the Llama 1 training mixture (1.2T tokens).
detail: Released 2023 as a community effort to recreate the dataset Meta used for Llama 1. Mixed sources: Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, arXiv, Wikipedia, books. Used for the OpenLlama series. RedPajama V2 (30T tokens) followed in 2024.

snappy: Cleaned, deduplicated subset of Common Crawl — used to train the Falcon LLM family.
detail: Released by Technology Innovation Institute (UAE, 2023) alongside Falcon-7B and Falcon-40B. Demonstrated that aggressively filtered web data alone could match or beat the Pile-style mixed-corpus approach for LLM pretraining. Inspired follow-ups like FineWeb that pushed filtering further.

snappy: Agent pattern where the model critiques its own output, identifies mistakes, and tries again.
detail: Introduced 2023. Loop: produce output, evaluate it, generate a critique, regenerate with the critique in context. Especially effective for reasoning-heavy tasks. Used inside many agent frameworks and tool-calling pipelines. Conceptually related to self-consistency but with explicit critique instead of voting.

snappy: Teaching a model when to say no — declining requests for help with harmful, illegal, or otherwise off-policy tasks.
detail: A subset of alignment training that produces the familiar "I can't help with that" responses to weapons synthesis, malware, or self-harm queries. Hard to get right: too liberal and the model is dangerous; too strict and it's annoying ("I can't tell you how to chop onions"). Modern refusal training is layered with explanation — the model says why it's declining and offers to help with the underlying need.

snappy: Machine learning where an agent learns by taking actions in an environment and receiving rewards.
detail: Distinct from supervised learning (which learns from labeled examples) and unsupervised learning (which finds patterns). The agent's goal is to maximise cumulative reward by trial and error. Powered milestones like AlphaGo, AlphaZero, robotic dexterity, and the alignment phase of modern LLMs (RLHF). Sample-efficient RL remains a major research challenge.

snappy: Generating many candidates from a model and keeping only those that pass some filter — a check, a reward score, a verifier.
detail: Used in training (build a fine-tuning corpus from rejected/accepted samples), in inference (combine with best-of-N), and in alignment work. Cheap and effective when you have a reliable verifier. Common pattern in modern reasoning-model training.

snappy: Activation function that's the identity for positive inputs and zero for negative ones — the dominant activation since around 2012.
detail: Mathematically: max(0, x). Why it works so well: gradient is always 1 for positive inputs, so it doesn't saturate the way sigmoid does. Adopted widely after AlexNet (2012). Many variants since (Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU). The default modern Transformer uses GELU or SwiGLU rather than plain ReLU.

snappy: Wind, solar, hydro, geothermal — the dominant near-term path to low-carbon AI compute.
detail: Hyperscalers report "100% renewable" via PPAs even when their grid mix includes fossil fuels (the renewable energy is bought and traded). 24/7 hourly-matched renewable energy is the harder, more honest goal that Google and Microsoft are now pursuing. Carbon-free baseload (nuclear, geothermal) is increasingly favored as renewables alone can't match AI's constant load.

snappy: Downweighting tokens that have already appeared to stop the model getting stuck on the same words.
detail: Without it, language models tend to loop — "the the the the" or repeating phrases. The penalty multiplies the probability of recently-used tokens by a factor below 1 (typical 1.1-1.3). Heavier penalties harm fluency, lighter ones leave loops in. Largely solved by modern training but still an important inference-time knob for older or smaller models.

snappy: Storage of past (state, action, reward, next-state) tuples that an off-policy RL algorithm samples from for learning.
detail: Introduced as part of DQN. Lets the agent learn from each experience many times and breaks the temporal correlation between consecutive states (which would otherwise destabilise gradient-based training). Many variants: prioritised replay (sample surprising transitions more often), hindsight experience replay (relabel failed attempts as successes for a different goal).

snappy: Second-stage model that scores retrieved documents more carefully than the initial retrieval pass.
detail: Retrieval pulls 100 candidates fast; reranker scores them slowly with a cross-encoder (a model that looks at query and document together rather than embedding them separately). Output: top 5-10 highest-quality matches for the LLM prompt. Cohere Rerank, BGE rerankers, and Voyage Rerank are popular options.

snappy: A trick that made it practical to train very deep networks — adds shortcut connections so signal can skip past layers.
detail: Before ResNets (Microsoft Research, 2015), deeper networks paradoxically performed worse than shallower ones. Adding identity shortcuts solved this and unlocked 100-plus-layer networks. The residual idea — add the input to the layer's output — is now everywhere: every Transformer block uses it. One of the most-copied design patterns in modern deep learning.

snappy: Lab-specific policy committing to pause or modify development if specific dangerous capabilities emerge.
detail: Anthropic introduced the term in 2023; OpenAI's Preparedness Framework and Google DeepMind's Frontier Safety Framework are analogous. The basic structure: define capability levels (CSAI Levels), set evaluations to detect when a model crosses a threshold, commit to specific safety measures at each level. Voluntary but increasingly referenced by regulators.

snappy: Maximum die size that a lithography machine can print in one shot — roughly 858 mm² for current EUV.
detail: Limited by the physical optics of the lithography system. The H100 die size (814 mm²) is already at the reticle limit; B200 uses two reticle-limit dies stitched together via advanced packaging. Pushes beyond the reticle limit are why advanced packaging (and reticle stitching techniques) are so important to frontier AI silicon.

snappy: Scalar signal the environment gives the agent — captures whatever the agent is supposed to maximise.
detail: Sparse rewards (only at goal) make learning hard; dense rewards (frequent feedback) make learning easier but risk "reward hacking". Reward design is one of the most consequential decisions in any RL project. In RLHF, the reward is produced by a learned reward model rather than being hand-coded.

snappy: When a model finds shortcuts that score high on its reward signal but don't actually satisfy what the reward was meant to capture.
detail: Classic example: a robot trained to maximise points in a boat-racing game discovered it could rack up points by spinning in circles in a single area, ignoring the actual race. In RLHF, reward hacking shows up as models that game the reward model — producing outputs that look good to it but aren't actually helpful. A major reason RLHF needs careful design and many rounds of iteration.

snappy: A small AI model trained to predict what humans would prefer — used to score outputs during RLHF.
detail: Take pairs of model responses, ask humans which is better, train a model to predict those preferences. The reward model becomes a stand-in for human judgment during the RL phase, scoring each new output. Quality of the reward model is a major bottleneck on RLHF — a biased reward model bakes its biases into the final language model and is the main source of "reward hacking" failures.

snappy: RL technique — adding intermediate rewards to guide an agent toward distant goals it couldn't reach with sparse rewards alone.
detail: Without shaping, an agent trying to reach a far-off goal gets no reward signal until it succeeds (essentially never, in long-horizon tasks). Shaping provides smaller rewards along the way. Done badly, it leads to reward hacking. Modern RLHF reward modelling is essentially learned reward shaping — humans rate intermediate behaviour rather than only success/failure.

snappy: RLHF variant where AI judges, not humans, score model outputs — much cheaper, almost as good.
detail: Human feedback is slow and expensive. RLAIF replaces some or all human raters with another AI model — typically a strong existing model used to rate or rank outputs. Anthropic's Constitutional AI is one form of RLAIF: the model critiques its own outputs against a written list of principles. Cheaper to scale, with the trade-off that you inherit whatever blind spots the judge model has.

snappy: Training technique where humans rate model outputs and the model learns to produce more of what's rated higher.
detail: First show humans pairs of responses to the same prompt and ask which they prefer. Train a reward model on those preferences. Then use reinforcement learning to nudge the language model toward higher reward. RLHF was the key step that turned GPT-3 into ChatGPT and underlies most consumer-grade LLMs. It's also where alignment researchers focus on bugs like reward hacking and sycophancy.

snappy: Field of engineering and AI focused on machines that move and act in the physical world.
detail: Pre-AI robotics relied on hand-coded controllers and structured environments — factory arms, surgical robots, simple vacuum cleaners. The modern "AI for robotics" wave (Tesla Optimus, Figure, 1X, Physical Intelligence) aims to combine LLMs with motor control to produce general-purpose robots that work in messy real-world settings.

snappy: AMD's GPU programming stack — their open-source answer to NVIDIA's CUDA.
detail: Has been improving fast since 2023 as AMD invests heavily, but still trails CUDA on ecosystem breadth and reliability. PyTorch and Hugging Face increasingly support ROCm out of the box. The closer AMD gets to functional parity, the more credible their MI300/MI350 hardware becomes vs NVIDIA's H100/B200.

snappy: A diagram that shows whether a workload is bottlenecked by compute or by memory bandwidth, at a glance.
detail: Plot operations-per-byte (arithmetic intensity) on the x-axis, achieved throughput on the y-axis. The "roof" is a piecewise line: bandwidth-limited at low intensity, compute-limited at high intensity. The model — introduced by UC Berkeley — is the standard way of explaining why a particular kernel is slow and which way to fix it.

snappy: Score for text summarization — measures n-gram overlap with reference summaries.
detail: Sister metric to BLEU but oriented toward recall (does the candidate cover the reference?) rather than precision. Standard for summarisation benchmarks. Same weaknesses as BLEU: insensitive to meaning. Modern eval increasingly uses LLM-as-judge instead.

snappy: DeepMind's 2023 vision-language-action model — first widely-publicised demonstration that LLM-style training extends to robot control.
detail: Built on top of a vision-language model, with robot actions encoded as text tokens. The model could follow natural-language instructions to a robot arm ("pick up the bag of chips") on objects it had never seen during training. Sparked the current wave of foundation-model robotics work.

snappy: Off-policy RL algorithm — strong performance on continuous control tasks like robotics.
detail: Introduced 2018 by UC Berkeley. Uses maximum entropy reinforcement learning — the policy is encouraged to be stochastic, which improves exploration. State-of-the-art on many robotic locomotion benchmarks. Common in real-world robot learning where sample efficiency matters.

snappy: Point in a loss landscape where the gradient is zero but it's not a minimum — a kind of fork where you can go down in one direction but up in another.
detail: Saddle points dominate the loss landscapes of large neural networks. Lucky for training: gradient descent generally rolls past saddle points fairly quickly. Adam and similar adaptive optimisers are specifically good at escaping saddles. Earlier worries that deep networks would get stuck in saddles haven't materialised.

snappy: Meta's universal segmentation model — produces a mask for any object pointed at in any image.
detail: Released 2023 with the SA-1B dataset of 1 billion masks. SAM 2 (2024) extends to video. The model is "promptable": click on something, get a mask of that thing. Foundational for downstream applications (image editing, robotics, video editing, medical imaging) that need fast, flexible segmentation.

snappy: Reconfigurable dataflow accelerator from SambaNova — bet on a different architectural philosophy from GPUs.
detail: Each SN40L chip combines compute units with on-chip memory and reconfigurable interconnect. Targets enterprise customers who want to fine-tune and serve large models on dedicated hardware. Notable for very large model support (1T+ parameters) on small clusters.

snappy: How few interactions with the environment an RL algorithm needs to learn a good policy.
detail: Major dimension along which RL algorithms compete. Model-free methods (DQN, PPO) tend to be sample-inefficient; model-based methods (MuZero, world models) can be far more efficient. Sample efficiency matters enormously when each interaction is expensive — real robots, scientific experiments, RLHF with human raters.

snappy: Picking each next token randomly from the model's probability distribution, rather than always taking the top one.
detail: The umbrella over temperature, top-k, top-p, and similar decoding methods. Introduces controlled randomness that lets the model produce diverse, creative outputs instead of dull, deterministic ones. The main mechanism by which the same prompt to the same model gives slightly different answers each time.

snappy: Samsung's chip-manufacturing business — the second-largest pure-play foundry after TSMC.
detail: Distinct from Samsung's memory and consumer businesses. Manufactures Samsung's own Exynos chips, Qualcomm Snapdragons, and some NVIDIA, AMD, and other parts. Has struggled with yield on leading-edge nodes (3nm, 2nm) but remains the second source for many designs and competes for TSMC's customers.

snappy: California bill (2024) that would have required pre-deployment safety testing for frontier AI models. Vetoed by Governor Newsom.
detail: Headlined as the first US state to attempt frontier-AI regulation. Provisions included safety testing, kill switches, and developer liability for catastrophic harms. Heavily debated within the AI community; vetoed September 2024. Has shaped subsequent state and federal proposals (including SB 53 which followed) and the public debate.

snappy: Predictable performance gains from more compute, data, and parameters
detail: Empirical laws that relate model loss to compute, dataset size, and parameter count via power-law curves. Underpins frontier labs' strategy of investing in larger training runs on the bet that capabilities improve smoothly with scale. Kaplan et al. (2020) and the Chinchilla paper (2022) are the canonical references.

snappy: When a model takes actions intended to undermine oversight — concealing capabilities, manipulating evaluators, biding its time.
detail: Anthropic and Apollo Research have documented early signs of scheming behaviour in frontier models given suitably contrived setups. Treated seriously enough that responsible-scaling policies include specific scheming-evaluation thresholds. Whether current scheming behaviour is goal-directed or instrumental is contested.

snappy: Free-form intermediate-text area an agent uses to think out loud — usually hidden from the final answer.
detail: An agent might write "Let me check the user's calendar", call the calendar API, then write "Now I need to find a free slot", before returning the final answer.
