The scratchpad accumulates these intermediate reasoning steps. Useful for both performance (better reasoning) and debugging (you can see what the agent was thinking).

snappy: Bigger, better-quality Stable Diffusion variant released mid-2023 — the default open-source image model for over a year.
detail: Two-stage pipeline: a base model produces an initial image, a refiner polishes it. ~3.5B parameters in the base. Native 1024×1024 generation. Widely used by community art tools and many commercial products before SD3 and Flux took over the frontier.

snappy: Attention used within a single piece of text — every word looks at every other word in the same input.
detail: Self-attention lets every word in a sequence consider every other word in the same sequence, all in one pass. This is what makes Transformers fast to train: they can process a whole sentence in parallel, instead of one word at a time like the older recurrent networks they replaced. It's the engine inside every modern large language model.

snappy: Inference trick: sample the model multiple times and take the most common answer.
detail: Especially effective for reasoning problems. The model generates many candidate answers (each with chain-of-thought), and a vote determines the final answer. Robust to one-off mistakes in the model's reasoning. Trade-off: K× more inference cost. A precursor to modern test-time-compute scaling.

snappy: Asking a model to evaluate its own previous output and find weaknesses.
detail: A building block of Reflexion and Constitutional AI. Surprisingly effective at catching errors the model itself made — the model has more capability than its single-pass output reflects. Used in modern reasoning models and in many production prompting recipes.

snappy: Vehicle that drives itself — uses sensors, maps, and AI to navigate without human input.
detail: SAE levels 0 (no automation) through 5 (full autonomy). Waymo and Pony.ai are operating Level 4 robotaxi services in specific cities. Tesla pushes Level 2+ FSD with promises of higher. Cruise paused operations after a 2023 incident. Chinese companies (Baidu Apollo, WeRide, AutoX) operate similar services in China.

snappy: Training scheme where the model learns from raw data without explicit labels, by predicting hidden parts of the input.
detail: BERT's masked-language-modeling, GPT's next-token prediction, SimCLR's image augmentation-based contrastive learning, wav2vec for audio. Has eaten supervised learning's lunch in modern foundation models — almost all pretraining is now self-supervised. The huge gains come from using web-scale unlabeled data instead of small human-labeled datasets.

snappy: Searching for meaning rather than keywords — "car insurance claim" matches "auto policy filing".
detail: Powered by embeddings: the query and the documents are both embedded into the same space, and the closest documents win. Catches synonyms and paraphrases that keyword search misses. The standard retrieval method inside RAG systems, often combined with traditional keyword search for hybrid retrieval.

snappy: Computer-vision task — label every pixel with what category it belongs to (road, sky, person, etc.).
detail: Used in autonomous driving, medical imaging, satellite analysis. Distinct from instance segmentation (which also distinguishes individual instances of the same class). Modern semantic segmentation is dominated by transformer architectures (Mask2Former, OneFormer) and open-vocabulary methods (CLIP-derived).

snappy: Training scheme using a small labeled dataset and a large unlabeled one — combines self-supervised pretraining with fine-tuning.
detail: Common modern pattern: pretrain on huge unlabeled data, then fine-tune on a small labeled set. Hugely improves performance when labels are expensive. The bedrock of foundation-model approaches across language, vision, and audio.

snappy: Combining data from multiple sensor types — cameras, Lidar, radar, ultrasonic — into a single coherent picture.
detail: Critical for autonomous driving and robotics. Lidar gives depth; camera gives texture and colour; radar works in fog and rain. Fusing them requires reconciling different rates, geometries, and noise characteristics. Modern AVs (and increasingly humanoid robots) rely on sensor fusion as part of their core perception stack.

snappy: The hardware that converts wide internal data buses into the fast serial signals that travel between chips.
detail: Every fast interconnect — PCIe, NVLink, InfiniBand, Ethernet — runs over SerDes lanes. Modern SerDes hit 100-200 gigabits per second per lane. Pushing them faster is one of the hardest analog-circuit problems in chip design. Cadence, Synopsys, and Alphawave Semi are major SerDes IP suppliers.

snappy: A computer in a data center — usually a 1U-4U box with multiple CPUs, memory, networking, and (for AI) GPUs.
detail: An AI training server typically holds 8 GPUs (NVIDIA HGX configuration), 2 server CPUs, 2 TB of host RAM, and InfiniBand or 400G Ethernet networking. The server is the unit of failure; cluster software handles node failures by redistributing work to surviving servers.

snappy: Standard for one-way streaming from server to client over HTTP — the standard delivery mechanism for LLM token streams.
detail: Older than WebSockets, simpler. Each event is a line of text on a long-lived HTTP connection. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and most other LLM APIs use SSE for streaming. Web frontends consume via the EventSource browser API; backend code reads the line-delimited stream.

snappy: Crowdsourced dump of ChatGPT conversations shared by users — used as fine-tuning data for early open chat models.
detail: Users would paste their ChatGPT chats to ShareGPT to share them; the resulting public corpus was scraped and used to fine-tune models like Vicuna and WizardLM. Demonstrated the value of conversation-shaped data over single-turn instruction tuning. Quality is mixed but real conversations capture chat dynamics in a way curated data doesn't.

snappy: S-shaped function that squashes any real number to a value between 0 and 1.
detail: Originally the standard activation function in neural networks. Mostly displaced by ReLU and friends for hidden layers because sigmoid saturates (its gradient is tiny for large inputs, slowing training). Still used at output layers for binary classification, and in gating mechanisms like LSTMs and SwiGLU.

snappy: Training robots in simulation, then transferring the learned behaviour to real-world hardware.
detail: Simulation is cheap (millions of trials per hour); real robots are expensive (one trial per minute, with breakage). Sim-to-real techniques (domain randomisation, system identification, fine-tuning) bridge the inevitable gap between simulator physics and reality. NVIDIA's Isaac Sim and Cosmos platform are the main industry tools.

snappy: Hardware design where one instruction operates on a whole vector of numbers at once — the basic trick behind GPU parallelism.
detail: Instead of "add A to B", a SIMD machine does "add this list of 32 A's to this list of 32 B's, all at once". GPUs are massively SIMD: a single "warp" of 32 threads runs the same instruction across different data. CPUs have SIMD too (Intel's AVX, ARM's NEON) but on a much smaller scale. The single most important hardware concept for understanding why GPUs are fast.

snappy: South Korean memory maker — the leading supplier of HBM for AI accelerators.
detail: Long-time DRAM and NAND producer. Reached pole position in HBM3/HBM3e supply, with NVIDIA as a major customer. Capacity expansion for HBM4 is one of the binding constraints on the next generation of AI compute.

snappy: Prompting technique where the model first outlines an answer ("skeleton"), then fills in details in parallel.
detail: Introduced 2023. Outputs an abstract plan, then asks the model to elaborate each section independently. Useful for tasks where the structure matters (writing, multi-part answers). The parallelisation lets some agent frameworks fan out the section-filling across multiple model calls.

snappy: Robotics technique — build a map of an unknown environment while keeping track of where you are in it.
detail: Used in self-driving cars, drones, AR/VR headsets, robot vacuums. Combines sensor input (camera, Lidar, IMU) with constraint-solving algorithms (extended Kalman filter, particle filter, factor graphs). Modern "neural SLAM" methods are starting to outperform traditional geometric pipelines in some settings.

snappy: Workload manager that schedules jobs across large compute clusters — the standard for AI training.
detail: Originally for HPC supercomputers. Most academic and many industry AI clusters run on Slurm. Users submit jobs (e.g., "train this model on 256 nodes for 10 days") and Slurm queues, schedules, and isolates them. Foundation of cluster operations for large-scale ML.

snappy: Compact nuclear reactor — designed to be factory-built and deployed in modular units.
detail: Promised but not yet at commercial scale. Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta have all signed agreements for future SMR-based power. Long-term bet: nuclear provides the baseload of carbon-free electricity that hyperscale AI needs. Construction timelines remain the binding constraint; first US-deployed SMRs expected 2030-ish.

snappy: Qualcomm's laptop-class chip with a strong NPU — the engine behind Microsoft Copilot+ PCs.
detail: Snapdragon X Elite (2024) was Qualcomm's serious entry into Windows laptops, with a 45 TOPS NPU dedicated to on-device AI. Microsoft requires 40+ TOPS for the Copilot+ branding, so Qualcomm, Intel (Lunar Lake), and AMD (Ryzen AI) all design chips with that in mind.

snappy: A complete computer (CPU + GPU + memory controllers + accelerators) packed onto a single chip.
detail: Phones and laptops are SoC-driven: Apple's M-series, Qualcomm's Snapdragon, Google's Tensor are all SoCs that combine CPU cores, GPU cores, an NPU, a media engine, and shared memory. SoCs win on power efficiency and physical size at the cost of upgradability — you can't swap the GPU on an iPhone.

snappy: Function that turns a list of arbitrary numbers into a probability distribution — used everywhere in classification.
detail: Exponentiates each input and normalises so the outputs sum to 1. The final layer of every classification model uses softmax; so does the attention computation when picking which keys to attend to. "Softmax temperature" rescales the inputs to make the distribution sharper (low temperature) or flatter (high temperature).

snappy: TSMC's 3D stacking technology — uses hybrid bonding for very dense die-on-die integration.
detail: Used by AMD in V-Cache (extra L3 cache stacked on top of CPU dies) and by Apple in M-series chips. Distinguished from CoWoS (2.5D, side-by-side) by its vertical stacking and finer-pitch bonding. Important step toward fully 3D logic-on-logic stacks.

snappy: OpenAI's text-to-video model — produces minute-long photorealistic video from a prompt.
detail: Announced February 2024 with viral demos; public access launched late 2024. Uses a diffusion model operating on video latents. The advance was duration and coherence — minute-long videos with consistent characters, physics, camera moves. Set off a video-generation race with Runway, Luma, Kling, and Veo.

snappy: Tool for interpretability: decompose a model's internal activations into a sparse set of interpretable features.
detail: Trained to reconstruct a model's activations using a large dictionary of features, with the constraint that only a few features fire at any time. The features often turn out to be human-interpretable: "this is a feature for the Golden Gate Bridge". Anthropic's 2024 "Scaling Monosemanticity" paper applied SAEs to Claude and produced thousands of named features.

snappy: The standard form of MoE today — for any given input, only a handful of the model's experts wake up.
detail: Contrasted with a "dense" version where every expert sees every input (rarely used in practice). The sparse routing has to be balanced carefully — otherwise a few experts get all the work and the rest never train. Google's Switch Transformer (2021) and GShard worked out the practical engineering. The payoff is dramatic: roughly 8× more capacity for the same inference cost.

snappy: "Who spoke when" — segmenting an audio file into turns by different speakers.
detail: Used in meeting transcription, podcast indexing, customer-service call analysis. Combines voice-activity detection with speaker-embedding models. Frontier ASR systems (Whisper-large-v3 plus diarisation, AssemblyAI, Deepgram) increasingly do diarisation as a built-in step.

snappy: When a model finds an unintended way to satisfy its specification — letter rather than spirit of the law.
detail: A boat-racing agent that spins in circles to harvest power-ups instead of finishing the race. A cleaning robot that hides messes instead of cleaning them. Documented in many environments. Closely related to reward hacking but a slightly broader umbrella that includes the case where the model wasn't even trained with RL.

snappy: Visual representation of audio — frequency content over time, with mel scaling matched to human hearing.
detail: Most modern speech models internally convert audio waveforms to mel spectrograms before further processing — the spectrogram is the model's actual input. Looks like a heat map: time on one axis, frequency on the other, brightness = energy. The mel scale compresses high frequencies the way human ears do.

snappy: Inference trick where a small model proposes several next tokens and the big model verifies them in parallel.
detail: Generating one token at a time is sequential and slow. A cheap "draft" model guesses the next 4-8 tokens; the expensive main model checks them all in a single batched forward pass; accepted guesses are kept and rejected ones trigger a fallback. Achieves 2-3× speedup on typical workloads. Used in production by most inference engines.

snappy: Cloud capacity sold at a discount on the condition that the provider can reclaim it on short notice.
detail: Useful for batch workloads that can checkpoint and resume — like training runs. Hyperscalers run spot markets for excess GPU capacity. Spot prices for H100s can be 30-70% off on-demand. Many AI startups stretch their compute budgets by running training on spot capacity with aggressive checkpointing.

snappy: Reading-comprehension benchmark — 100,000+ questions about Wikipedia paragraphs.
detail: Released 2016. Each question has a short answer that's a span of text in the paragraph. The standard pre-LLM reading-comprehension benchmark. Models hit human parity around 2018. Now superseded by harder benchmarks (TriviaQA, NQ, more recent QA benchmarks) but historically formative.

snappy: Fastest form of memory — kept on the same chip as the processor, used for caches.
detail: Each SRAM cell uses 6 transistors (vs 1 for DRAM), so it's expensive and low-density. But it's much faster: nanoseconds to access, no refresh required. CPU and GPU L1/L2 caches are SRAM. Cerebras's wafer-scale chip uses on-die SRAM as primary memory, which is why it has 21 PB/s of bandwidth.

snappy: British company behind Stable Diffusion — pioneered open-weight image generation.
detail: Founded by Emad Mostaque. Released Stable Diffusion (2022) as fully open-weight, kicking off the consumer image-generation explosion. Subsequent leadership turmoil and capital-intensive economics. Continues to release open-weight image and video models (SD3, Stable Video Diffusion).

snappy: Open-weight diffusion-based image generator — released August 2022 by Stability AI, kicked off open-source image AI.
detail: Original SD 1.x, then SDXL (2023), Stable Diffusion 3 (2024), Stable Diffusion 3.5. Trainable, fine-tunable, hostable anywhere with a consumer GPU. Backbone of thousands of community fine-tunes, LoRAs, and consumer apps. Hugely influential — its open release shaped the image-generation ecosystem.

snappy: Pre-designed building block of a chip — a small layout that implements one logical function (a gate, a flip-flop, etc.).
detail: Modern chips are assembled from libraries of thousands of standard cells, each carefully laid out and characterised for a specific process node. EDA tools (Synopsys, Cadence) use these libraries to translate a chip designer's high-level RTL code into a physical layout. Reusing standard cells massively shortens design time.

snappy: OpenAI / Microsoft / Oracle / SoftBank consortium building a multi-hundred-billion-dollar AI compute build-out.
detail: Announced January 2025. Initial $100B commitment, with potential to reach $500B over four years. First major site is in Abilene, Texas, operated by Oracle and Crusoe. Aims to provide OpenAI with multi-gigawatt training capacity. The scale signals where frontier AI infrastructure is heading: utility-scale energy plants paired with compute.

snappy: Asking the model to first restate the question at a higher level, then answer.
detail: Released by Google research in 2023. "Take a step back" — the model writes a more abstract version of the question, identifies the relevant general principles, then applies them to the specific question. Modest accuracy gains on hard reasoning benchmarks; one of many "thinking aids" in modern prompting practice.

snappy: Variant of gradient descent that updates parameters using one batch at a time rather than the full dataset.
detail: The "stochastic" part refers to using a random subset of data per step rather than the whole training set. Far cheaper per step than full-batch gradient descent, and the noise turns out to help generalisation — networks trained with SGD often outperform networks trained with deterministic optimisers. The grandfather of every modern training algorithm including Adam.

snappy: Forcing a model's output to match a schema — JSON, XML, function-call arguments, etc.
detail: More general than JSON mode. The developer provides a schema (e.g., a Pydantic model or a Zod schema) and the model is constrained to produce conforming output. Implemented via grammar-constrained sampling, fine-tuning, or both. Critical for production deployments where downstream code expects specific data shapes.

snappy: Producing an image that has the content of one source and the style of another.
detail: Originally a deep-learning achievement of the 2010s using CNN feature matching (Gatys et al., 2015). Now mostly subsumed into the broader text-to-image space: "a photo of a city, in the style of Studio Ghibli". Specific style-transfer products (Lensa AI, popular phone-app filters) drove waves of public attention to generative AI in 2022-2023.

snappy: Specialised agent spawned by a primary agent to handle a specific sub-task.
detail: Used to parallelise work and protect the primary agent's context window. Common in frameworks like Claude Code, OpenAI Operator, and LangGraph. The orchestrator agent delegates, gathers results, and continues with summarised context. Effective when sub-tasks are clearly bounded.

snappy: Electrical facility that steps grid-scale voltage down to what a data center can use.
detail: AI data centers need their own dedicated substations — sometimes multiple per site for redundancy. Substation construction is a major bottleneck for new AI infrastructure: utilities and transmission system operators have queues of years in some regions. Site selection increasingly driven by existing substation availability.

snappy: The bottom layer of a chip package — a small printed circuit board that carries the die and routes signals to the board.
detail: Modern AI packages use specialised substrates (ABF — Ajinomoto Build-up Film) with many layers of fine wiring. The package substrate is a separate supply-chain component, made by companies like Unimicron, Ibiden, and Shinko. Substrate capacity has been a periodic bottleneck.

snappy: Suite of harder NLP tasks introduced as a successor to GLUE after BERT-era models saturated GLUE.
detail: Released 2019. Eight tasks: word-sense disambiguation, commonsense reasoning, textual entailment, reading comprehension. Quickly approached human performance by frontier models. Replaced by MMLU and other suites by the LLM era but historically formative for NLP benchmarking.

snappy: Fine-tuning a pretrained model on labeled examples of what the right answer looks like.
detail: SFT is typically the first step after pretraining. For a chat model, that means hundreds of thousands of human-written example conversations: prompt, ideal response. The model learns the format and style of helpful responses. SFT alone produces decent chatbots but leaves them prone to confident errors and unhelpful refusals — that's why RLHF or its alternatives come next.

snappy: Factorising any matrix into three simpler matrices that capture its structure — a foundational tool.
detail: Decomposes a matrix A as U·S·V (rotation × scaling × rotation). The singular values in S tell you which directions matter most. Used in dimensionality reduction (PCA), low-rank matrix approximation, recommender systems, and (relevant to AI) in LoRA where weight updates are factored as a low-rank product.

snappy: Classical classifier that finds the hyperplane maximally separating two classes — once the dominant ML method.
detail: Pre-deep-learning, SVMs were the standard for image and text classification. Now mostly outclassed by neural methods on large datasets but still useful when data is small, dimensions are large, and you need a strong baseline. Kernel SVMs can handle non-linear boundaries.

snappy: Software engineering benchmark — model must fix real GitHub issues in real codebases.
detail: Released 2023 by Princeton. Each task gives the model an issue description and a codebase, asking it to produce a patch that passes the project's tests. Much harder than HumanEval because it requires understanding context, navigating multi-file codebases, and matching project style. The Verified subset is the most-cited score. Anthropic's Claude leads through most of 2024-2025.

snappy: When a model agrees with whatever the user says, even if it's wrong — a side effect of training on human preferences.
detail: RLHF rewards responses that humans rate highly. Humans tend to rate agreeable responses higher than challenging ones. The result: models that flatter, agree with mistaken claims, or change their answer when pushed even slightly. Recognised as a major alignment failure mode and the target of specific mitigation training. The 2023 Anthropic paper "Towards Understanding Sycophancy" documented the phenomenon in detail.

snappy: Training data generated by another AI model rather than collected from the real world.
detail: Crucial for training reasoning models (chains of thought generated and filtered), for domains with limited real data (medical imaging), and for privacy-sensitive applications. Risks: model collapse if overused, propagation of source-model biases. A significant fraction of frontier-model training data is now synthetic.

snappy: Preference data generated by AI judges rather than human raters — cheaper but with all the caveats of LLM-as-judge.
detail: Used heavily in Constitutional AI, RLAIF, and many open-source fine-tunes (UltraFeedback, Capybara). Quality depends on the judge model. The newer Mistral, Llama, and Qwen models all use significant amounts of synthetic preference data at the alignment stage.

snappy: Hidden instruction that sets a model's persona, ground rules, and context — placed before the user's first message.
detail: Every chat interaction starts with a system prompt: "You are a helpful assistant", "Always cite sources", "Never discuss sensitive topics". Frontier APIs distinguish system from user roles in the message structure. Modern system prompts are often hundreds of lines long, encoding both persona and many specific instructions for behaviour.

snappy: Dimensionality-reduction technique — produces low-dim visualizations of high-dim data that preserve local structure.
detail: Standard tool for visualising embeddings, clusters, or any high-dimensional dataset in 2D or 3D plots. UMAP (released 2018) is a faster modern alternative with similar output. Both are routinely used in interpretability and exploratory analysis of neural-network representations.

snappy: The moment a chip design is finalised and sent to the fab for manufacturing.
detail: Historically came on magnetic tape, hence the name. Modern tape-outs are digital data sent electronically to TSMC or Samsung. After tape-out, no design changes are possible — the photomasks are made from the tape-out data and any change requires new masks ($10M+) and a new fab run (weeks to months). Tape-out is the major milestone in every chip program.

snappy: Operating a robot remotely with a human in the loop — provides data for imitation learning and a fallback for autonomy.
detail: Common modern setup: humans wear VR headsets and motion-tracked gloves to puppeteer humanoid robots. Each session generates training data for the robot's foundation model. Production AV operations (Waymo, Cruise) keep remote human operators ready to assist when the car gets stuck. Bridge between current and future autonomy levels.

snappy: Knob that controls how random a model's output is — low values are predictable, high values are creative.
detail: Temperature scales the model's probability distribution before sampling. Temperature 0 is equivalent to greedy decoding. Temperature 0.7-1.0 is typical for chat. Above 1.5 the model starts to produce gibberish. The single most useful knob a developer can tune when integrating an LLM into a product.

snappy: A multi-dimensional array of numbers — generalisation of vectors and matrices to any number of dimensions.
detail: A batch of 32 images, each 224×224×3 (RGB), is a 4D tensor of shape (32, 224, 224, 3). The KV cache during LLM generation has tensors with batch, layer, head, and position dimensions. Modern ML frameworks (PyTorch, JAX, TensorFlow) are built around tensors. "Tensor cores" on GPUs are hardware units that multiply small tensors very fast.

snappy: NVIDIA's name for the matrix-multiplying units inside its GPUs — the part that does the AI heavy lifting.
detail: Introduced with the Volta architecture in 2017. A tensor core does a small matrix multiply (e.g., 4×4 × 4×4) in a single clock cycle, dramatically faster than doing it with regular CUDA cores. Modern H100s have hundreds of tensor cores per GPU and can do over 1,000 trillion operations per second when matrix-multiplying at FP8 precision.

snappy: Splitting a single layer's computation across multiple GPUs — needed when one layer doesn't fit on one chip.
detail: Each GPU holds part of the weight matrices and computes its slice in parallel; results are combined with high-bandwidth communication. Tensor parallelism is what makes frontier models (300B+ parameters) trainable and servable at all. Requires very fast interconnect — typically NVLink within a node, InfiniBand between nodes.

snappy: Google's earlier deep learning framework — once dominant, now in maintenance mode behind JAX and PyTorch.
detail: Released in 2015. Drove the first wave of production ML at Google and beyond. Lost mindshare to PyTorch in research (2019-2020) and is being replaced by JAX internally at Google. Still widely used in production where its serving stack (TensorFlow Serving, TensorFlow Lite for mobile) has deep deployment.

snappy: NVIDIA's inference optimisation framework — takes a trained model and produces a heavily-optimised binary for fast inference.
detail: Fuses operations, picks optimal kernels, applies quantisation. TensorRT-LLM is the LLM-specific variant launched in 2023 — combines TensorRT with FasterTransformer-style optimisations. Common in production serving on NVIDIA hardware where you want every last bit of throughput.

snappy: NVIDIA's LLM-specific inference framework — fused kernels, in-flight batching, FP8/FP4 support.
detail: Launched 2023. Hand-tuned kernels for common LLM operations (attention, normalisation, MLP), with aggressive support for the latest precision formats. Often the fastest serving option for NVIDIA hardware, at the cost of more setup complexity than vLLM.

snappy: AI hardware startup led by Jim Keller — building open-source RISC-V-based AI accelerators.
detail: Founded 2016 in Toronto. CEO Jim Keller is famous for his work on chips at AMD, Apple, Tesla, and Intel. The bet: open-source CPU instruction set + open-source AI accelerator stack creates an alternative to NVIDIA's closed ecosystem. Products include the Wormhole and Blackhole accelerator chips.

snappy: Tesla's driver-assistance software — pursues a cameras-only "end-to-end" approach to autonomy.
detail: Sold to Tesla customers as a paid software upgrade. Distinguished from competitors by rejecting Lidar entirely in favour of vision-only neural networks. Continues to operate as Level 2 (driver supervised) rather than full self-driving, despite the name. Robotaxi service (Cybercab) and unsupervised FSD remain Musk's stated next steps.

snappy: Using extra compute at inference time — typically by thinking longer — to improve answer quality.
detail: Models like OpenAI's o1, o3, Claude with extended thinking, and Gemini Deep Think spend more compute on hard questions, generating intermediate reasoning steps before producing the final answer. The result: large gains on math and reasoning benchmarks at the cost of higher inference cost. A new axis along which model providers compete: not just bigger models, but smarter use of inference compute.

snappy: Generative AI that produces images from a text prompt — the killer demo of the 2022-2024 generative wave.
detail: Modern T2I systems are diffusion-based. The user types "a sunset over a Brazilian rainforest, hyperrealistic"; the model produces a fresh image. Has shifted creative workflows in marketing, illustration, design, and game development. Continuing debate over training-data copyright and creative-industry impact.

snappy: Generative AI that produces video from a text prompt — the 2024-2025 frontier of generative media.
detail: Sora, Veo, Runway Gen-3, Luma Ray, Kling, MiniMax video. All diffusion-based, mostly operating on video latents (compressed sequences). Quality has improved dramatically — consistent characters across minute-long clips, physically plausible scenes — but cost is high and motion artefacts still common. Audio generation increasingly integrated.

snappy: Trillion (10¹²) math operations per second — the standard unit for measuring chip compute throughput.
detail: An H100 hits about 1,000 TFLOPS at FP16, ~2,000 at FP8. A modern phone NPU does 30-50 TFLOPS. The metric is precision-dependent — a chip rated "1000 TFLOPS at FP8" might only do 500 at FP16 and 250 at FP32. Always check which precision is being quoted.

snappy: Hugging Face's LLM inference server — competitor to vLLM with strong integration into the HF ecosystem.
detail: Optimised serving for transformer text models with continuous batching, quantisation, and tensor parallelism. Easier to spin up if you're already in the Hugging Face workflow. vLLM tends to win on throughput; TGI wins on ergonomics for HF users.

snappy: EleutherAI's open-source training dataset — 825GB of curated text from 22 sources.
detail: Released 2020 as an open alternative to OpenAI's GPT-3 training data. Mixed sources: Wikipedia, arXiv, PubMed, GitHub, Common Crawl, Books3, StackExchange, and more. Influential as one of the first publicly-documented LLM datasets. Used to train Pythia, EleutherAI's reproducible open models.

snappy: BigCode's open dataset of 6 TB of permissively-licensed source code — used to train StarCoder.
detail: Released 2022 by Hugging Face and ServiceNow. Filtered to permissive licences (MIT, Apache, BSD). Covered 350+ programming languages. The Stack v2 (2024) extended to 67 TB, used to train StarCoder 2. The major open code dataset, complementary to GitHub Copilot's proprietary training corpus.

snappy: Total tokens (across all requests) a system produces per second — what matters for cost economics.
detail: While latency is about one user's experience, throughput is about how many users one GPU can serve. Continuous batching, large effective batch sizes, and quantisation all increase throughput. Modern H100s serve roughly 1,000-5,000 tokens/second per GPU depending on model size and quantisation; bigger systems aggregate this across many GPUs.

snappy: Paste or pad placed between a chip and its cooling system to conduct heat efficiently.
detail: Often overlooked but critical at high-power densities. Modern AI accelerators dissipate hundreds of watts per die, and any thermal interface gap dramatically reduces cooling effectiveness. Specialty TIMs (liquid metal, sintered silver, polymer-based composites) are an active R&D area as power densities climb further.

snappy: The atomic unit a language model reads or writes — usually a word fragment of 3–4 characters, not a full word.
detail: Models don't see characters or words directly; text is first split into tokens by a tokeniser. Common words like "the" become a single token; rare or compound words split into several. Context window is measured in tokens, API pricing is per-token, and a model's vocabulary size (typically 32k–256k) is the number of distinct tokens it knows.

snappy: Sending each token from the model to the client as soon as it's generated, instead of waiting for the full response.
detail: Standard for chat UIs — produces the typewriter effect users see in ChatGPT. Implemented via Server-Sent Events (SSE) over HTTP. Lower time-to-first-token but higher total-bytes overhead. Almost all production LLM APIs support streaming as an option.

snappy: Splitting text into tokens — the first preprocessing step before any model can read anything.
detail: Modern LLMs use subword tokenisers — algorithms that produce tokens shorter than words but longer than letters — balancing vocabulary size against token length. The tokeniser is part of the model contract: GPT-4 tokens and Llama tokens aren't interchangeable. Tokenisation quirks cause many oddities: weak arithmetic, struggles with character-level tasks, sensitivity to whitespace.

snappy: The audio analogue of a text tokenizer — turns continuous waveforms into a discrete sequence the model can process.
detail: Built from neural audio codecs like EnCodec or SoundStream. The output sequence might be 50-100 tokens per second of audio. Audio LLMs (AudioLM, MusicLM, SpiritLM) operate on these tokens just like text LLMs operate on word tokens. The tokenisation choice strongly affects audio generation quality.

snappy: Hugging Face's fast Rust-based tokenization library — used by the Transformers library under the hood.
detail: Tokenization is surprisingly expensive in pure Python. The Tokenizers library is written in Rust and exposed to Python, doing the work hundreds of times faster. Supports BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece, and other tokenizer types. Open-source but maintained by Hugging Face.

snappy: An LLM calling external functions — search, code, calculator, APIs — to extend its capabilities.
detail: The pattern: the model is given a list of available tools with descriptions and arguments. It produces structured calls; the system runs them; the results go back to the model. Tool use is the foundation of agentic workflows. OpenAI's function calling and Anthropic's tool use APIs both standardise this pattern.

snappy: Decoding rule that restricts each step's choices to the K most likely tokens.
detail: Caps the long tail of the distribution: even if the model gives small probability to thousands of weird tokens, only the top K (typically 20-50) are considered. Combined with temperature and top-p to shape model output. A workhorse decoding rule throughout the 2020s, though top-p has largely replaced it in modern stacks.

snappy: Decoding rule that picks from however many tokens are needed to cover a probability mass of P.
detail: Instead of fixing the number of candidates (like top-k), top-p adapts: take the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities sum to P (typically 0.9-0.95). When the model is confident, this is a tiny set; when uncertain, it widens. Strikes a better balance than top-k in practice and is the default decoding strategy for most modern chat models.

snappy: PyTorch's just-in-time compiler that speeds up models without code changes — added in PyTorch 2.0 (2023).
detail: Takes regular PyTorch code, traces it, and produces optimised kernels via TorchInductor + Triton. Typically 1.3-2× faster than eager-mode PyTorch with no model changes. Important step in narrowing the performance gap between PyTorch and ahead-of-time compiled frameworks like JAX.

snappy: Google's custom AI accelerator — designed from scratch for the matrix multiplies that dominate neural networks.
detail: Google has shipped six generations of TPUs since 2016. The hallmark is a systolic array: a grid of multipliers that pumps data through in a single rhythmic flow, very efficient for matrix multiplication. TPUs power Google search, Gemini training, and the Google Cloud AI offering. They're available only through Google Cloud, not for direct purchase.

snappy: Google's training-focused TPU generation, deployed in pods of thousands for Gemini training.
detail: Announced late 2023. 95GB of HBM, 459 TFLOPS BF16, connected via Google's custom optical interconnect into pods of up to 8,960 chips. Google trained Gemini Ultra on TPU v5p. The TPU v5e is a smaller, cheaper variant for inference. Followed by Trillium (v6) in 2024 and Ironwood in 2025.

snappy: Dividing a dataset into three parts — training (build the model), validation (tune hyperparameters), test (final evaluation).
detail: Foundational practice in ML. Typical splits: 60/20/20 or 80/10/10. Critical that the test set is never used during model development — otherwise you're tuning to the test set and your reported performance is inflated. "Data leakage" is the standard term for accidental violations.

snappy: The total number of math operations performed during training — the most rigorous "size" metric for a model.
detail: Calculated as roughly 6 × parameters × training tokens. GPT-3: about 3 × 10²³ FLOPs. GPT-4: rumored 2 × 10²⁵. Frontier models in 2025 push past 10²⁶. US export controls reference training-FLOPs thresholds when deciding which models trigger reporting; the EU AI Act uses 10²⁵ FLOPs as a frontier-model threshold.

snappy: The number of tokens of text (or other data) the model has seen during training.
detail: Modern frontier models train on 5-20 trillion tokens of text plus growing volumes of code, math, multimodal data. Data quality matters as much as quantity — filtered, deduplicated, balanced corpora beat raw web scrapes. Approaching the limit of available high-quality public text is one driver of the shift to synthetic and AI-generated training data.

snappy: Amazon's custom training accelerator — AWS's bet on owning the AI stack vertically.
detail: Trainium 1 launched 2022. Trainium 2 (2024) targets H100-class performance at lower price-per-FLOP for AWS-internal workloads. Used by Anthropic for some Claude training (Anthropic has a deep AWS partnership). Available only through AWS, not for direct purchase.

snappy: Sequence model based on self-attention
detail: Architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017) that replaced recurrent networks for sequence modeling. Self-attention lets every token attend to every other token in a context window, enabling massive parallelism in training. Underlies GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and essentially every modern large language model.

snappy: The most-installed Python library for working with pretrained Transformer models.
detail: Hugging Face's flagship library. Provides a uniform API for loading, fine-tuning, and running models from BERT to Llama to Mixtral. Over 1 million models on the Hugging Face Hub use it. The single most important piece of community infrastructure in open-source AI.

snappy: The basic electronic switch — billions of them are wired together to make every modern computer chip.
detail: Invented at Bell Labs in 1947. Modern silicon transistors are smaller than 50 nanometres and switch on and off billions of times per second. An H100 GPU has 80 billion transistors. As they shrink, they become harder to control (quantum effects, leakage currents), which is why so much R&D goes into new transistor structures like FinFET and GAA.

snappy: Reasoning approach where the model explores multiple branches of thought and prunes dead ends.
detail: Generalisation of chain-of-thought. Instead of one linear reasoning trace, the model considers several candidate next steps at each point, evaluates them, and keeps the most promising. Sometimes implemented as actual search algorithms (BFS, DFS) over thought trees. Influential in inspiring the modern reasoning-model paradigm.

snappy: Google's 2024 TPU generation — first major step beyond the v5 line.
detail: Roughly 4.7× the compute of v5e at the same power. Used for Gemini 2 training and inference. Like all TPUs, only available inside Google Cloud — you can't buy one. The successor Ironwood (announced 2025) is positioned as an inference-optimised generation with focus on serving large models efficiently.

snappy: OpenAI's Python-like language for writing custom GPU kernels — much easier than raw CUDA.
detail: Lets researchers write performance-sensitive code (e.g., custom attention variants) without learning CUDA C++. Compiles down to PTX (NVIDIA's assembly) automatically. Used inside PyTorch's torch.compile and increasingly to implement bleeding-edge optimisations that need to bypass standard kernels.

snappy: RL technique that constrains policy updates to be "close enough" to the current policy — keeps training stable.
detail: Underpins TRPO (Trust Region Policy Optimization) and its simpler successor PPO. The idea: in RL, big policy changes can cause performance to collapse because the data you collected is no longer representative. Trust regions limit each update to a small KL-divergence step, ensuring monotonic improvement.

snappy: Benchmark for whether a model resists generating common misconceptions and falsehoods.
detail: Questions specifically designed to elicit popular but false answers — "What happens if you crack your knuckles a lot?" Tests truthfulness rather than knowledge. Releases of new models often show that scaling alone doesn't fix truthfulness; alignment training does.

snappy: The world's most important chip fab — manufactures the leading-edge silicon for NVIDIA, AMD, Apple, and most others.
detail: Founded 1987 by Morris Chang. Single-handedly enabled the modern fabless chip industry. Currently the only foundry shipping high-volume 3nm and (soon) 2nm. Geopolitically critical — most leading-edge AI silicon flows through TSMC's Taiwan fabs. Expanding to Arizona, Japan, and Germany under heavy subsidies.

snappy: Vertical conductive pathway drilled through silicon — lets stacked chips talk to each other.
detail: TSVs go through the body of a die, typically connecting top and bottom faces. Essential for 3D stacking (HBM, advanced packaging). Pitch and density of TSVs are major engineering metrics — finer pitch means more connections per square millimetre. Used heavily by SK Hynix, Samsung, Micron in HBM.

snappy: Generative AI that turns written text into spoken audio — used for accessibility, narration, voice assistants.
detail: Modern TTS uses diffusion or autoregressive transformers (ElevenLabs, OpenAI's tts-1 and gpt-4o-audio, Google TTS). Voice cloning lets a model imitate a specific speaker from a few seconds of audio. Quality has reached human-indistinguishable for many use cases, raising concerns about voice phishing and impersonation.

snappy: Image-to-image network shaped like a U — compresses the picture down, then expands it back up.
detail: Designed for biomedical image segmentation in 2015. The encoder downsamples, the decoder upsamples, and shortcut connections pass high-resolution detail across the U. U-Net is now the workhorse of diffusion models — Stable Diffusion's denoising network is a U-Net — making it one of the most-used architectures by FLOP-hours in the world.

snappy: Open standard for connecting chiplets from different vendors — analogous to PCIe at the chip-to-chip level.
detail: Backed by Intel, AMD, Arm, TSMC, Samsung, and others (notably not NVIDIA, which has its own proprietary interconnect). UCIe 1.0 (2022) and 2.0 (2024). The goal: a mix-and-match chiplet ecosystem where you can buy a compute chiplet from one vendor and combine it with memory chiplets from another. Adoption is slow but growing.

snappy: Industry effort to make Ethernet competitive with InfiniBand for AI clusters.
detail: The Ultra Ethernet Consortium was founded in 2023 by AMD, Broadcom, Cisco, HPE, Intel, Meta, and Microsoft (notably not NVIDIA). The goal: AI-specific extensions to Ethernet that match InfiniBand's predictable, low-latency behaviour. First products are shipping in 2025. A long-term play to reduce NVIDIA's networking moat in AI.

snappy: Apple's interconnect that joins two M-series Max chips into an Ultra — used in M1 Ultra, M2 Ultra, and successors.
detail: A dense silicon interposer connects the two dies with thousands of signal lines, presenting them to software as one chip. Allows Apple to scale up to higher-end Mac Studio and Pro configurations without designing a separate larger die. Distinguishes Apple's approach from typical multi-socket designs.

snappy: Dimensionality-reduction method — faster than t-SNE, better at preserving global structure.
detail: Released 2018. Common tool for visualising embeddings, exploring clusters in vector databases, debugging fine-tuning effects. Outputs change less than t-SNE with different runs, so easier to communicate. Standard in scientific data exploration.

snappy: When a model is too simple or too lightly-trained to capture the patterns in the data.
detail: Opposite failure mode to overfitting. Symptoms: high error on both training and validation data. Cure: bigger model, more training, better optimisation, less regularisation. Less of a concern in the frontier era — modern models have plenty of capacity — but still relevant for resource-constrained settings.

snappy: Theorem: a neural network with one hidden layer and enough neurons can approximate any continuous function arbitrarily well.
detail: Proven in the 1980s. Important historically — established that neural networks were in principle capable of modelling anything. Practically less useful: "enough neurons" might mean astronomically many, and the theorem says nothing about how to find good weights. Deep networks succeed for reasons that go beyond universal approximation.

snappy: Battery system that keeps servers running during grid outages until generators can take over.
detail: Datacenter UPS systems are room-sized arrays of batteries — increasingly lithium-ion replacing older lead-acid. Sized to cover 5-15 minutes of full-facility load. Generators take over for longer outages. AI training jobs are particularly painful to lose to power events: checkpoints help recover, but you lose the partial work.

snappy: The user's input in a chat — distinct from the system prompt or assistant responses.
detail: Modern LLM APIs structure conversations as a list of {role, content} pairs: system, user, assistant, tool. The user message is what the human just typed (or, in agentic settings, what the orchestrator just generated). The model attends to all messages plus its own scratchpad when producing the next response.

snappy: Estimate of how much reward you'd expect to accumulate from a given state, following the current policy.
detail: Foundational concept in RL: rather than just picking actions that look good now, the agent considers their downstream consequences. Modern RL algorithms (PPO, actor-critic methods) almost all learn value functions in parallel with policies. Value learning is often a major bottleneck on sample efficiency.

snappy: Model that learns to squeeze data down to a small code and reconstruct it — the workhorse compressor inside many image generators.
detail: VAEs are trained to compress and rebuild data, with the constraint that the compressed code lives in a smooth, well-behaved space you can sample from. By themselves they produce slightly blurry samples. But they're now essential infrastructure inside latent diffusion: the part that turns pixels into compressed representations before diffusion, and back again afterwards.

snappy: An ordered list of numbers — the most basic data structure in machine learning.
detail: A 3-dimensional vector is just three numbers: (x, y, z). A 1,536-dimensional embedding vector is a list of 1,536 numbers representing meaning. Every neural network manipulates vectors at huge scale: each token in an LLM, each pixel patch in a vision model, each user in a recommendation system is a vector.

snappy: Database optimised for finding the nearest neighbours of an embedding vector — the search engine inside every RAG system.
detail: Stores millions or billions of high-dimensional vectors (typically 768 or 1536 dimensions) with metadata. Query is also an embedding; the database returns the K most similar. Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma, Milvus are dedicated products; Postgres/pgvector and Elasticsearch added vector search to existing databases.

snappy: Google's text-to-video model — Veo 2 (2024) and Veo 3 (2025) compete with Sora at the frontier.
detail: Notable for synchronised audio generation (Veo 3 generates dialogue, sound effects, and music alongside the video). Distributed through the Gemini app, Vertex AI, and consumer products like Google's video-generation tool Flow. Photorealism and physical-world consistency improved sharply across versions.

snappy: Transformer applied to images by chopping them into small patches and treating each patch like a word.
detail: Introduced by Google in 2020. Split an image into 16×16 patches, project each into an embedding, then run a standard Transformer encoder. ViTs match or beat CNNs at scale, especially when pretrained on enormous datasets. The architecture underpins modern vision-language models — CLIP, GPT-4o's vision capability, Gemini's image understanding — and most image generators.

snappy: Conditioning a vision model on an example image rather than a text prompt.
detail: Show a SAM-style model a click or a box rather than describing what to segment. Show a few-shot vision model a couple of example images of what you want. Foundational interaction pattern for image-based AI tools and a key research direction for image-classification efficiency.

snappy: Task where a model answers questions about an image — "how many people are in this photo?".
detail: Pre-LLM VQA was a specific benchmark category. Modern vision-language models (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Llama Vision) effectively do VQA as a side effect of their general capability. The original VQA benchmarks are now largely saturated; specialised harder ones (MMBench, MMMU) test current frontier models.

snappy: Foundation model that takes images and natural-language instructions as input and produces robot actions as output.
detail: Bridges the LLM/VLM paradigm with robotics. Examples: DeepMind RT-2 and RT-X (2023), Physical Intelligence Pi-0 (2024), NVIDIA GR00T, Figure Helix. The hope: one VLA can drive any robot, given training data from that robot embodiment. Sample efficiency from cross-robot transfer is an active research question.

snappy: Open-source LLM inference engine known for high throughput and continuous batching.
detail: Started at UC Berkeley as the reference implementation of PagedAttention (efficient KV cache management). Now the most popular open-source serving stack — used by hundreds of AI startups and inside many cloud-AI offerings. Compatible with most popular open models out of the box.

snappy: Model that combines image understanding with language — describes images, answers questions about them, follows visual instructions.
detail: Built by attaching a vision encoder (typically a ViT) to a language model and training them together on image-caption pairs and visual question-answering data. CLIP was the early breakthrough; modern frontier VLMs (GPT-4o, Claude vision, Gemini) handle screenshots, diagrams, charts, and photos at near-human level.

snappy: Audio synthesis model — converts intermediate representations (mel spectrograms, neural features) back into waveform audio.
detail: Used at the final step of text-to-speech and music generation. WaveNet (DeepMind, 2016) was the breakthrough neural vocoder. Modern vocoders (HiFi-GAN, BigVGAN) produce indistinguishable-from-real audio at modest compute cost. Frontier music and TTS systems use neural vocoders almost exclusively.

snappy: Algorithm that detects when speech is occurring in an audio stream versus silence or noise.
detail: Critical for voice-mode interfaces: when has the user stopped talking? Modern VAD is neural-network-based (Silero VAD, WebRTC VAD). Plays a major role in latency budgets for voice assistants — you can't start responding until VAD detects end-of-speech. Pre-LLM problem that LLM apps inherited.

snappy: Older AI category — Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant — built on rules + narrow ML before the LLM era.
detail: Once the consumer face of AI; mostly disappointing in terms of intelligence. Modern "voice modes" of LLMs (ChatGPT, Gemini Live) are replacing the category by combining low-latency speech recognition, LLM reasoning, and natural TTS into a more capable assistant.

snappy: Generating speech in a specific person's voice from a short sample of their audio.
detail: Modern voice cloning needs only 3-30 seconds of reference audio. ElevenLabs offers it commercially with consent checks; open-source tools (XTTS, OpenVoice) make it widely available. Powerful for accessibility (preserving someone's voice after illness) and content production (multilingual narration). Equally powerful for fraud — voice-cloning scams are now a documented threat.

snappy: Thin disc of polished silicon, typically 300mm in diameter — the raw substrate every chip is made on.
detail: A 300mm wafer can hold hundreds of small chips or a few large ones. Multiple layers of circuit features are printed onto the wafer through dozens of steps. When the process completes, the wafer is diced into individual chips. Wafer cost ranges from a few hundred dollars for trailing-edge nodes to $20,000+ for leading-edge 2nm.

snappy: An accelerator that's a single chip the size of an entire silicon wafer — Cerebras's distinctive design.
detail: Most chips are diced from a wafer into hundreds of small pieces. Cerebras keeps the whole wafer intact, yielding a single chip with ~900,000 cores and 44GB of on-chip memory. No off-chip memory bandwidth limits, no chip-to-chip communication overhead. Trade-off: yield is harder (one defect can kill the whole wafer) and they need custom packaging and cooling.

snappy: Embedding invisible signals in AI-generated content (text, images, video) to identify it later.
detail: Google's SynthID, OpenAI's text watermarks, Stability AI watermark schemes. Helps detect deepfakes, AI plagiarism, and synthetic-media misuse. Active research: how to make watermarks robust to compression, paraphrasing, and adversarial removal. Required for some AI-generated outputs under the EU AI Act.

snappy: Meta's self-supervised speech encoder — pretrained on raw audio to learn speech representations.
detail: Released in 2019, with wav2vec 2.0 (2020) the breakthrough version. Trained without labels by predicting masked audio segments, similar to how BERT works on text. Used as the encoder for downstream speech tasks (ASR, speaker identification, emotion detection). Influential as one of the first big speech foundation models.

snappy: Alphabet's self-driving subsidiary — the leader in deployed autonomous-vehicle services.
detail: Operates commercial robotaxi services in Phoenix, San Francisco, Los Angeles, and expanding. Uses Lidar plus cameras plus high-definition maps. The most thoroughly safety-validated AV operation by miles driven without serious incidents. Roughly 200,000+ paid rides per week as of 2025.

snappy: Traditional experimental science — physical experiments, as opposed to computational ("dry lab") work.
detail: Term used in AI-for-science conversations to distinguish what AI can do (compute predictions) from what still requires real-world experiments (synthesis, biological testing, materials characterisation). Modern AI-bio labs (Recursion, Insilico, Isomorphic) integrate AI predictions tightly with wet-lab validation pipelines.

snappy: OpenAI's open-weight speech-recognition model — released 2022, widely deployed.
detail: Trained on 680,000 hours of multilingual audio. Handles transcription, translation, and voice activity detection. Released open-weight, which gave the open-source speech ecosystem a strong baseline overnight. Used inside many transcription products (Otter, Notion AI, voice features in chat apps).

snappy: The English Wikipedia (~6 million articles, ~20GB compressed) is a staple training-data ingredient for almost every LLM.
detail: Used roughly as-is or filtered to specific sections. High signal-to-noise ratio, edited prose, broad topical coverage. Multilingual versions extend the contribution for non-English language modelling. Public-domain content licensing means it's the cleanest large dataset legally.

snappy: AI-first code editor from Codeium — fork of VS Code with strong agent integration.
detail: Codeium was originally a free Copilot-style autocomplete; Windsurf is their flagship AI IDE. Competes with Cursor in the AI-IDE category. Acquired by OpenAI in mid-2025.

snappy: Older packaging technique — thin wires connect the die's edges to the substrate's edge contacts.
detail: Still used in low-cost packages and some non-leading-edge chips. Largely displaced by flip-chip (bumps under the die) in everything performance-sensitive, because wire bonds are slower, longer, and less dense. Knowing the term helps when reading older chip documentation or low-cost product literature.

snappy: What an agent currently has loaded into context — distinct from long-term memory that's retrieved on demand.
detail: Working memory is the model's context window: the system prompt, the recent conversation, retrieved RAG documents, the agent's scratchpad. Limited by context-window size. Trade-offs in agent design centre on what to keep in working memory vs. what to push out to long-term storage and retrieve when needed.

snappy: Learned model of how the environment behaves — lets the agent imagine consequences and plan without acting.
detail: Replaces interacting with the real environment by interacting with the learned model. Sample-efficient because the model's rollouts are free, but the agent's performance is capped by how accurate the model is. Hot research area for both video-game agents (DreamerV3) and embodied AI (NVIDIA Cosmos, Google Genie).

snappy: Cerebras's flagship — a single chip the size of a dinner plate with 900,000 cores.
detail: Latest of three wafer-scale generations. 4 trillion transistors, 44GB of on-chip SRAM, 21 PB/s of memory bandwidth (orders of magnitude more than any GPU). Used by Cerebras's inference cloud, which posts world-record latency numbers for large model serving by keeping everything on-chip.

snappy: Elon Musk's AI company, founded 2023 — develops the Grok model family.
detail: Built the Memphis "Colossus" cluster (100K+ H100s by late 2024, scaling to 200K-1M GPUs through 2025). Integrated with X (Twitter) for both training data and product distribution. Standalone Grok app released 2024. Often positioned as ideologically distinct from other frontier labs.

snappy: Percentage of chips on a wafer that are good after manufacturing — affected by defects, manufacturing precision, and design choices.
detail: Yield matters enormously for economics. A modern process at maturity might yield 70-90% on small dies but much less on huge dies (the H100 die is so large that yield is a major contributor to its cost). New process nodes typically launch with sub-50% yield, climbing as the process matures. TSMC's yield management is a major competitive advantage.

snappy: Microsoft's approach to sharding optimizer state, gradients, and parameters across GPUs — the original sharded-data-parallel design.
detail: Three "stages" of sharding: ZeRO-1 shards optimizer state only; ZeRO-2 adds gradients; ZeRO-3 adds parameters (equivalent to PyTorch's FSDP). Implemented in Microsoft's DeepSpeed library. Single most important technique for training frontier-scale models on clusters of hundreds or thousands of GPUs.

snappy: Whether a model handles a new task with no examples (zero-shot) or a handful of examples (few-shot) in the prompt.
detail: GPT-3 famously demonstrated few-shot learning in 2020 — given 3-5 examples, it could do tasks it had never been trained for. As models improve, the gap between zero-shot and few-shot has narrowed: frontier models in 2025 often need no examples at all. The terms are still used to characterise prompting strategies.
